sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Cardiac arrest is a medical emergency that occurs when the heart suddenly stops beating effectively, leading to a cessation of blood flow to the brain and other vital organs. This condition can result in loss of consciousness and, if not treated immediately, can lead to death within minutes.
A coordinated, well-rehearsed resuscitation attempt, with a team leader and all team members effectively delivering specific roles, will improve the effectiveness of CPR and the overall resuscitation.
Four people with a minimum skillset of basic life support (BLS) and defibrillation should attend patients in cardiac arrest where possible. This should include at least one registered healthcare professional with advanced life support (ALS) competencies. One person with clinical decision making and leadership skills should form part of the team.
Some members may not be part of the emergency response, for example, a first aider with CPR training.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Unresponsive with no signs of life
- Inadequate or no respirations
- No palpable pulses
- As soon as Cardiac Arrest is established, commence resuscitation. If in doubt, start resuscitative efforts until facts are established
- If obvious death has occurred or injuries incompatible with life, cease resuscitation efforts

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Depending on the cause, ensure scene safety. Responder safety is paramount
- Move patient if in confined space that prohibits initiation of rescue efforts
- Take over compressions from bystanders on arrival
- If you have no back up, stick to BLS. Do not compromise compressions, oxygenation and defibrillation for ALS skills
- Interventions such as vascular access, medications or advanced airway management are not a priority and should only occur prior to the arrival of further clinicians if they do not interrupt high quality CPR
- Once back up has arrived, work as a team, communicate well and establish a ‘hands- off’ overview position once advanced skills have been administered
- Minimise interruptions in chest compressions
- Pulse checks should be performed only when there is evidence of an organised rhythm

- Pulse checks should be performed only when there is evidence of an organised rhythm
- Early preparation for transport is de-emphasised in most cases, except where there are compelling circumstances

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey, especially danger checks
- Apply defibrillator (Zoll X Series or Corpuls) and utilise manual mode if trained:
  - 200J if using a biphasic defibrillator
  - 360J if using a monophasic defibrillator
- If using an AED, follow device prompts
- Continue CPR at ratio of 30 compressions – 2 breaths
- Assess rhythm every 2 minutes; defibrillate when indicated with minimal pause

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- A supraglottic airway should be the first-line adjunct in most cases unless contraindicated
  - Do not attempt endotracheal intubation where a supraglottic device is adequate
- Without compromising high-performance CPR:
  - Gain IV/IO access

**Shockable rhythm (VF / Pulseless VT):**
  - Adrenaline after the second shock, then every second loop
  - Amiodarone after a total of 3 shocks (including pre-arrival AED shocks) if VF/pVT does not respond to defibrillation

**Non-shockable rhythm (Asystole / PEA):**
  - Adrenaline as soon as IV/IO access is established, then every second loop
  - Do not administer Amiodarone

- Consider alternate pad positioning (anterior/posterior) if still in VF/VT after 3 shocks and anti-arrhythmic medication
- Consider magnesium for torsades de pointes
- Consider reversible causes. Assess for all whilst performing high quality CPR:

**The Hs:**
- Hypovolaemia
- Hypoxia
- Hypo/Hyperkalaemia
- Hypo/Hyperthermia
- Hypoglycaemia

**The Ts:**
- Toxins
- Thrombosis
- Tamponade
- Tension Pneumothorax

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content: - The treatment for cardiac arrest patients revolves around core requirements; excellent quality chest compressions that are never interrupted unnecessarily, oxygenation and timely defibrillation

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Refer to the Traumatic Resuscitation Algorithm chart in the MRL Clinical Practice Guidelines Manual.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Refer to the Hypothermic Cardiac Arrest chart in the MRL Clinical Practice Guidelines Manual.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: "Return of spontaneous circulation" (ROSC) refers to the resumption of a sustained, effective heart rhythm and blood circulation after a cardiac arrest. It's a crucial goal of cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) and is indicated by signs like breathing, coughing, movement, a palpable pulse, or measurable blood pressure. Post-resuscitation care refers to the medical management provided after a patient achieves return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) following cardiac arrest. The goal is to stabilise the patient, prevent further complications, and optimise recovery.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content: - Return of spontaneous circulation post cardiac arrest

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Unless there are compelling reasons to move immediately, DO NOT Rush
- Continue to work as a team
- Anticipate re-arrest and plan accordingly
- The focus of post resuscitation care is on ensuring adequate cerebral perfusion, oxygenation and supportive treatment to allow the recovery of vital organs
- ROSC patients are often unstable initially. Time should be taken to plan patient packaging, handling and subsequent egress whilst they stabilise and build up blood pressure. Rough manual handling / jolting of the patient is undesirable
- Use this time to ensure IV lines are secure, and acquire 12 Lead ECG’s

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Re-evaluate Primary Survey
- Re-evaluate oxygenation and ventilation
- Provide supportive ventilation if required
- Avoid trying to correct abnormally high EtCO2 in the first few minutes
- Titrate oxygen delivery as required, aiming for SpO2 between 94 - 98%
- Avoid hyperventilation / hyperinflation (as this can impede venous return and stimulate a vagal response, inhibiting the effective rise of mean arterial pressure)
- Vital Sign Survey
- Where practicable, attempt targeted temperature management (32°-36°)
- If not already contacted, ensure consultation with on-call doctor

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider and address precipitating causes of arrest
- Perform serial 12-lead ECGs, as reperfusion rhythms may not be of diagnostic quality
- Manage hypotension in post-ROSC patients if BP is slow to rise:
  - Hypotension = Systolic BP < 100 mmHg
  - Administer Intravenous Crystalloid (Normal Saline)
  - If hypotension persists post IV/IO fluids, administer Adrenaline 50mcg bolus (0.5ml 1:10,000 or 1ml 1:20,000) every 3–5 minutes to maintain SBP ≥ 90 mmHg
  - Ensure persistent and frequent reassessments

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- All ROSC patients require a 12 lead ECG
- 1:10000 = 1mg in 1ml adrenaline in 9ml normal saline
- 1:20000 = 1mg in 1 ml adrenaline in 19ml normal saline
- Prepare for handover: Downtime/time of arrest, shocks delivered, dosages of medication administered, etc

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Termination of resuscitation (TOR) is a critical and ethically complex decision point in the management of cardiac arrest, requiring careful consideration of patient prognosis, resource allocation, and the potential for meaningful neurological recovery. The process involves a structured assessment to determine when further resuscitative efforts are unlikely to result in return of spontaneous circulation or long-term survival. Recognition of life extinct (ROLE) is a definitive clinical determination that resuscitation should not be initiated or should be discontinued, and is guided by established protocols, situational factors, and, where available, validated decision rules. This guideline outlines the principles and criteria for the safe, appropriate, and compassionate termination of resuscitation efforts, integrating current evidence and best practice from leading resuscitation authorities.
TOR/ROLE
- Situations Where Resuscitation Should Not Be Commenced:
  - Where the rescuer has determined that to commence CPR may endanger their life or that of the emergency response team
- Presence of unequivocal signs of death:
  - Obvious and unmistakable indicators of death are present
  - Evidence of decomposition, putrefaction, or larval infestation
  - Visible signs of tissue breakdown or insect activity
- Presence of rigor mortis or gravitational-dependent post-mortem hypostasis (lividity) in association with established criteria for determination of death:
  - Muscle stiffening or fixed blood pooling in dependent areas, consistent with irreversible death
- Major traumatic injuries incompatible with life:
- Examples include:
  - Decapitation or significant cranial destruction
  - Significant truncal destruction
  - Hemicorporectomy or translumbar amputation
  - Incineration
  - Obvious injuries with exsanguination or profound blood loss inconsistent with life
  - Note: All relevant findings must be carefully documented
- Major incidents or multi-casualty scenarios:
  - Clinical resources are overwhelmed, and triage protocols indicate that resuscitation is futile for a victim who displays no signs of life TOR/ROLE
- When recognising life extinct, a Determination of Death must be made with the following criteria:
  - No Central Pulses at all and
  - Asystole for >30 seconds and
  - Fixed and Dilated pupils with NO corneal reflexes at all and
  - No signs of Breathing at all and
  - No Auscultated Heart Sounds (if in scope of practice)
  - Occasionally, pacemaker spike activity may persist for a period following death. If automated external defibrillation (AED) is attempted, the device must indicate ‘No Shock Advised’, particularly in the absence of a display screen Withholding Resuscitation
  - Always maintain initial resuscitation efforts until adequate information is available to make the call the discontinue efforts
  - There may be circumstances where the commencement of resuscitation may be very unlikely to reverse the situation favourably, unless there are compelling reasons or special circumstances. Determination of Death should be followed thereafter
  - If there is any uncertainty regarding all of the following criteria, or if the situation is not clear, initiate resuscitation immediately
  - Prolonged cardiac arrest: Estimated period of pulselessness exceeding 15 minutes
  - Generally unwitnessed event: No reliable witness to the onset of cardiac arrest
  - Initial assessed rhythm: Asystole on first rhythm analysis
  - No defibrillation delivered: No shockable rhythm detected or treated
  - No compelling reasons or special circumstances: No identifiable mitigating factors or conditions that would justify continued resuscitation efforts
  - In the absence of clear contraindications or definitive criteria for termination, proceed with resuscitation measures as indicated Terminating Resuscitation
  - If established criteria for ROLE or withholding resuscitation are met, resuscitation may be discontinued, and determination of death may be made, provided this is within the clinician’s scope of practice (registered medical professional [paramedic, nurse, doctor])
  - Where special circumstances or compelling reasons to continue resuscitation are identified (such as younger age, refractory ventricular fibrillation, or recurrent arrest), efforts should be extended in accordance with clinical guidelines
  - The decision to terminate resuscitation should be approached with caution and, where possible, discussed with the current on-call doctor or Royal Flying Doctor Service (RFDS) for confirmation Terminating Resuscitation Cont.
  - In all other situations, maximal resuscitation efforts must be maintained. However, it is acknowledged that certain cases may present with clinical features indicative of futility
  - Clinical judgment should always be exercised, incorporating a comprehensive assessment of relevant factors and prognostic indicators
- Termination of Resuscitation may be considered under any of the following circumstances:
  - Persistent asystole: The presenting rhythm is asystole, the arrest was not witnessed by medical personnel, and asystole persists after 20 minutes of maximally directed resuscitation
  - Shockable rhythm progression: The presenting rhythm is shockable, the arrest was not witnessed by the rescuer, and the rhythm rapidly progresses to asystole or wide, slow pulseless electrical activity (PEA, <40/min) after 20–30 minutes of resuscitation, with no other favourable clinical indicators (such as elevated end-tidal CO₂)
  - Prolonged resuscitation with no return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC): The destination emergency department is more than 15 minutes from the arrest location, at least 20 minutes of maximal basic and advanced life support have been provided, ROSC has not been achieved, and there are no special circumstances to justify ongoing efforts
  - Clinical assessment of futility: A specifically authorised clinician determines, either on scene or in consultation with the on-call doctor or RFDS, that further resuscitation is futile based on prognostic indicators
  - Blunt traumatic cardiac arrest: After addressing reversible causes, prolonged cardiopulmonary resuscitation in blunt traumatic cardiac arrest rarely results in a positive outcome. If transport to an emergency department is not feasible within 25 minutes of arrival on scene, and ROSC has not been achieved after 10 minutes of resuscitation, termination of resuscitation is reasonable if criteria for determination of death are met
  - In all cases, contact the on-call doctor or RFDS. ESO’s should have a low threshold to do so
  - The presence of pulseless electrical activity (PEA) with a cardiac rhythm potentially compatible with cardiac output may suggest a condition in which the heart is unable to pump an adequate amount of blood or a pseudo-PEA state, in which myocardial contractility persists but is insufficient to generate a clinically detectable central pulse. In such cases, clinicians should maintain a very low threshold for seeking consultation with the on-call doctor or RFDS
  - Any Termination of Resuscitation in patients with a PEA presenting with a narrow complex and/or a rate of 40 beats per minute or higher must have a consultation with either the on-call doctor or RFDS Decision Making Cont.
  - The decision to discontinue resuscitation efforts necessitates careful clinical judgment and a thorough understanding of the likely underlying cause of cardiac arrest, as well as a comprehensive patient background history. There is no universally defined or absolute timeframe for this determination, as each clinical scenario presents unique factors that must be considered
  - A general approach is to stop CPR after 20 minutes if there is no ROSC or viable cardiac rhythm re-established, and no reversible factors present that would potentially alter the outcome
  - Special circumstances or compelling reasons, such as sudden collapse with a presumed cardiac aetiology, young people who have persistent VF (until reversible factors have been fixed or therapeutic options have been exhausted), suspected overdose, asthma, asphyxia, drowning, or pregnancy, should prompt consideration for sustained resuscitation efforts and expedited transport to the emergency department, as these scenarios may involve potentially reversible causes that could benefit from ongoing intervention
  - Indicators of effective resuscitation, such as high-quality CPR as measured by feedback devices, palpation of CPR-induced femoral pulses, meaningful fluctuations in end-tidal carbon dioxide (EtCO₂), and the presence of refractory or recurrent ventricular fibrillation, support the continuation of advanced life support and prolonged resuscitation efforts,
  - Whereas features suggesting futility, including prolonged time in cardiac arrest, lack of response to resuscitative efforts, persistently low EtCO₂, slow pulseless electrical activity or agonal activity, significant comorbidities, poor baseline neurological or functional status, advanced frailty, limited physiological reserve, and low likelihood of intensive care unit admission, should prompt consideration for termination of resuscitation
  - Consideration of transport, time, and patient handling risks-including assessing the feasibility and safety of patient movement, accounting for factors such as bariatric status, location and distance to the emergency department, and the need for intervention in public spaces-should inform decisions regarding ongoing resuscitation and transfer.
  - When considering termination of resuscitation, include and discuss with your team
  - Document rationale for decisions clearly and fully

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- There will be occasions where some activity of a dying heart can be observed. These are most commonly defined as very wide, disorganised and extremely slow complexes.
Wait for this to deteriorate to Asystole. This may take several minutes, and Determination of Death can be made thereafter
- Explain and engage with family or other necessary parties – Families should not necessarily be asked whether efforts should cease as they are often unprepared. Be empathetic, tactful and honest. Provide support to them in their time of loss17 NEUROLOGICAL

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Altered level of consciousness (ALOC) refers to any change from a patient’s baseline alertness, orientation, or responsiveness. It may range from mild confusion to complete unresponsiveness (coma). ALOC is not a diagnosis but a symptom of an underlying condition which can include metabolic disturbances (hypo/hyperglycaemia), hypoxia, head injuries, CVA/TIA, seizures, intoxication (drugs, alcohol), infection, shock or toxins.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Reduced alertness or responsiveness, patient may be confused, dizzy or light-headed, drowsy, or unrousable
- Disorientated with inability to recognise time, place, or people
- Abnormal behaviour, anxious, restless, sleepy, sleep like state or comatose
- Inability to follow commands, may be unable to respond to verbal or painful stimuli
- Abnormal motor responses and may include posturing, seizures, or flaccidity
- Pupillary abnormalities, abnormal breathing patterns, or evidence of trauma
- Pallor, sweaty, nausea, palpitations
- Full recovery after a few minutes

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- The differential for ALOC can be broad

**Consideration should be given to:**
  - Intracranial pathologies such as CVA, subarachnoid haemorrhage, intracerebral haemorrhage, meningitis, encephalitis, post-ictal, status epilepticus, Wernicke- Korsakoff, hypertensive encephalopathy
  - Extra-Cranial pathologies such as dys/arrythmia, blood glucose deficits, electrolyte imbalances, sedatives, opiate, or anticonvulsant medications, infection, sepsis, environmental conditions, hypoxia, substance abuse, substance/medication withdrawal, factitious or psychiatric disorder

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Ensure scene safety, responder safety is paramount
- Primary Survey
- Consider patient positioning, spinal precautions if indicated
- Consider Oxygen if indicated Vital Sign Survey
- Manage reversible causes (i.e., blood glucose, arrythmia, hypoxia, hypo/hyperthermia) if found to be evident

- Consider IM naloxone in suspected opiate toxicity in consultation with on-call doctor
- Keep patient warm
- Secondary/CNS Survey, FAST, cranial nerve assessment
- Clinicians should have low threshold for calling the on-call doctor if there are no improvement to simple procedures and/or treatments

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider advanced airway where indicated
- Apply cardiac monitor
- Manage cardiac dysrhythmia
- A 12 lead ECG should be acquired in most, if not all these patients
- If indicated, gain vascular access
- Consider obtaining pre-hospital blood sample for i-STAT if time permits
- Consider IV fluids if indicated
- Consider IV naloxone, titrate to effect (no large boluses) in suspected opiate toxicity, if IM not previously given, in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider benzodiazepines (titrated to effect) if seizure activity evident in consultation with on-call doctor

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content: - ALOC may fluctuate with time and response to treatment

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
**Cerebrovascular Accident (Stroke)**
A stroke is a sudden neurological deficit caused by interruption of blood flow to the brain (ischemic stroke) or bleeding into the brain (haemorrhagic stroke). It is a medical emergency.

**Transient Ischemic Attack (TIA)**
A TIA is a temporary episode of neurological dysfunction caused by a brief interruption of blood flow to the brain, without permanent brain injury. Symptoms resolve within 24 hours, often within minutes. TIAs are a significant risk factor for future stroke.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Sudden onset of neurological symptoms, which may include:
  - Weakness, numbness or paralysis of the face (facial droop, asymmetry)
  - Arm or leg weakness or numbness (especially on one side of the body)
  - Speech difficulties (slurred speech, difficulty finding words, or inability to speak)
  - Vision changes (sudden loss of vision, double vision, or visual field deficit)
  - Balance or coordination problems (dizziness, vertigo, or ataxia)
  - Severe headache (especially sudden and severe, which may suggest haemorrhagic stroke)
  - Dysphagia, difficulty in swallowing
  - Altered level of consciousness (most common in haemorrhagic stroke due to increased ICP and brainstem involvement)

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Other conditions that can mimic a stroke must be considered:
  - Hypoglycaemia
  - Intracerebral lesions (tumours, abscesses)
  - Seizures and post ictal states
  - Hemiplegic Migraines
  - Electrolyte Abnormalities (hyponatraemia)
  - Syncope
  - Sepsis
  - Subdural Haematoma
  - Vertigo, Labyrinthitis, Vestibular Neuritis
  - Confusion/cognitive dysfunction

- Functional Neurological Disorder Syndrome

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Oxygen if indicated
- Consider patient position (45 degrees, head up)
- Complete Vital Sign Survey, especially BGL
- Conduct (BE)FAST assessment If
- FAST- but history is suggestive of TIA, the patient is at risk and should still be considered for off-site transfer and hospital assessment at an appropriate facility
- Consider cranial nerve assessment
- Consider RACE score
- FAST+, stroke is the likely diagnosis; conduct the RACE assessment tool to identify Large Vessel Occlusion
- Clinicians should have a very low threshold for calling the on-call doctor and alert RFDS as this cohort of patients will require transfer to a suitable acute stroke centre
- Keep patient warm and comfortable

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Cardiac monitoring (arrythmias), 12 LEAD ECG if concurrent chest pain and/or time permits
- Consider advanced airway if required
- Gain IV Access, 2 x large bore
- Collect pre-hospital blood sample for i-STAT testing where appropriate
- Consider IV fluids
- Consider benzodiazepines if actively seizing, (titrated to effect), in consultation with on- call doctor
- Consider prophylactic antiemetic to reduce aspiration risk
- Consider appropriate analgesia in consultation with on-call doctor

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Maintain a high index of suspicion of CVA in persons presenting with neurological symptoms over the past 24 hours, excluding obvious causes for these neurological symptoms such as hypoglycaemia

- Patients should have continuous limb lead cardiac monitoring throughout to capture arrythmias, specifically AF which increases the risk of stroke, do not delay transfer / transport to capture a 12 lead unless there are specific reasons such as concurrent chest pain
- The timing of the onset of symptoms needs to be clearly defined so that the suitability for IV thrombolysis (≤9 hours) or Endovascular Treatment (within 24 hours) can be determined

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Dizziness refers to a range of sensations including feeling light-headed, faint, woozy, unsteady or as if the surroundings are spinning. It is often caused by disruptions in the body’s balance system, which involves the inner ear, eyes, brain and nerves. In the prehospital setting, dizziness can be a symptom of a wide variety of conditions, from benign disorders to life-threatening emergencies. Common causes include dehydration, low blood pressure, inner ear disorders, medication side effects, and stress.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Sensations of spinning and visual disturbances
- Loss of balance, unsteady, weak
- Disorientation, confusion and difficulty concentrating
- Pallor and clamminess
- Nausea and/or vomiting
- Nystagmus

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Patients presenting with vertigo should be managed under that clinical guideline
- Dizziness can appear insignificant on presentation but may have a serious underlying medical condition. A thorough assessment should be undertaken on all presentations of dizziness
- Dizziness can be caused by some of the following and should be managed accordingly:
  - Vertigo or B.P.P.V/Labyrinthitis/Eardrum perforation/Meniere’s Disease
  - Stroke/T.I.A/migraine/brain tumours/Parkinson’s disease/multiple sclerosis
  - Cervicogenic issues (neck issues)
  - ACS/arrhythmias
  - Alcohol withdrawal
  - Medication side effects
  - Sepsis
  - Dehydration/heat related illness
  - Hypoglycaemia
- Anaemia
- Postural Issues
- Anxiety/stress/depression
- Recent trauma/pain Red flags
- Sudden onset of debilitating dizziness with associated neurological symptoms should raise the possibility of stroke
- Sudden onset of dizziness with associated hearing loss and/or tinnitus
- Sudden onset of dizziness with ear pain/discharge/trauma/barotrauma (please also contact the ENT registrar to advise patient will present to ED

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Position patient appropriately
- Oxygen if indicated
- Vital Sign Survey (Specifically pulse, blood pressure [postural BP], blood glucose and temperature)
- Conduct Secondary and neurological assessments (Cranial nerve assessment/CNS Survey/FAST)
- Urinalysis (specific gravity)

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Apply cardiac monitor
- Manage cardiac dysrhythmia
- 12 lead ECG should be acquired in most, if not all these patients
- Consider IV access if indicated
- Consider obtaining blood for i-STAT sampling (CG4+, CG8+)
- Consider fluid therapy
- Consider appropriate antiemetic

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content: - Some pre-hospital procedures such as injections may precipitate dizziness in susceptible patients. Patient position should be considered to avoid potential injury

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Headache is a generalised term given to any pain or discomfort in the region of the head, scalp, neck or face. The pain associated with a headache may vary by type and underlying cause, ranging from sharp, throbbing, dull, or vice-like in nature and may occur on one or both sides of the head, be isolated to single location, or extend as a band across the skull. Headaches are categorised as:
- Primary: Benign, with no structural cause (e.g., tension-type, migraine, cluster headaches)
- Secondary: Symptoms of underlying conditions (e.g., stroke, infection, haemorrhage, tumour)

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Tension-type: Bilateral, pressing pain (mild-moderate), without nausea or photophobia
- Migraine: Unilateral, throbbing pain (moderate-severe) with nausea/vomiting, photophobia, phonophobia, or aura
- Cluster: Severe orbital/temporal pain lasting 15–180 minutes, with autonomic features (lacrimation, nasal congestion)
- Other features: Neck stiffness, fever, confusion, weakness, or changes in consciousness (red flags for serious pathology) Green Flags Amber Flags Red Flags
- Symptoms associated with influenza like illnesses
- Previous history of headaches presenting with their usual symptoms and triggers
- Normal vital signs, normal assessment using the FAST technique and able to walk normally
- Worsening headache following trauma
- Headache/migraine different in presentation to usual
- Taking an antiplatelet /anticoagulant medication
- HTN during pregnancy
- Post partum
- Previous history of intercranial haemorrhage
- Onset during sexual activity
- Familial history of cerebral vascular abnormalities
- Thunderclap headache
- Focal neurological deficits
- Fever, rash or ALOC association
- Meningeal signs
- New onset of severe pain in greater than 50yrs
- Headaches worsening over time
- Headache associated with postural changes
- Aura that lasts longer than an hour
- Headache with ALOC
- Substance abuse with amphetamines or cocaine
- Headache + blood pressure ≥180 mmHg (systolic) or 120 mmHg (diastolic)

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Relief of symptoms with analgesia is not evident of a benign cause
- Consideration should be given before administration of narcotics to patients with a history of chronic headaches as it has not been proven to be a successful intervention and may potentially increase the risk of reliance on such medication
- Management of headaches that are considered red flags should be treated under any relevant CPG
- Differential diagnosis: Migraine, tension-type headache, cluster headache, subarachnoid haemorrhage, meningitis, temporal arteritis, intracranial mass, or medication overuse headache

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- FAST assessment
- Limb LEAD ECG
- Consider cranial nerve Assessment if appropriate
- Consider “over the counter” analgesia (paracetamol, ibuprofen, aspirin, diclofenac, Anagraine)
- Secondary Survey if applicable
- Consider cool compress
- Consider rest in a dark, quiet room if available

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- 12 LEAD ECG (Cranial changes to ECG are a late sign)
- Consider IV access
- Consider IV fluids
- Consider appropriate antiemetic (IM Metoclopramide in migraine) in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider further migraine related therapies in consult with on-call doctor, (triptans, IM metoclopramide)
- Consider further analgesia in consultation with the on-call doctor (Aspalgin, Ibudeine, Naproxen, Celecoxib, Indomethacin)
- Consider further parenteral analgesia, IM or IV if oral route not feasible in consultation with on-call doctor

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Clinical studies and systematic reviews have shown that parenteral metoclopramide (commonly 10 mg intramuscularly) can provide significant pain relief in acute migraine attacks, independent of its antiemetic effects
- Common migraine triggers can include Illness, poor sleep, exercise, menstruation, stress, sun glare, food (citrus, MSG, artificial sweeteners, nuts, onion, salty foods, caffeine, chocolate), skipped meals, missed mediation, medication overdose/overuse

**Headache patterns:**
  - Acute Recurrent: Migraine
  - Chronic Non-progressive: Tension type headache, anxiety, depression, somatisation
  - Chronic Progressive: Tumour, benign intracranial hypertension, brain abscess, hydrocephalus
  - Acute on chronic non-progressive: Tension headache with co-existing migraine
  - Migraine induced long QT changes should be considered before medication administration (triptans, ondansetron). This is often linked to autonomic nervous system dysfunction during the migraine. While usually resolving after the migraine, it's important to be aware of this potential association due to the risk of arrhythmia ECG Change Frequency During Migraine Attack Clinical Relevance Rhythm abnormalities ~30% Arrhythmia risk (AF, PVC, APC) Prolonged PR interval ~20% Conduction delay, arrhythmia Prolonged QTc interval ~40% Ventricular arrhythmia risk T wave inversion ~66% Repolarization abnormality ST-segment abnormalities ~40% Ischemic/repolarization change Increased QTc/P- wave dispersion Yes Predicts arrhythmia risk

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: The RACE scale is a tool used by paramedics and other prehospital personnel to quickly assess patients with suspected stroke and determine if they have a large vessel occlusion (LVO), a major cause of stroke. The scale includes five criteria, facial palsy, arm motor impairment, leg motor impairment, head and gaze deviation and aphasia-agnosia, The RACE scale has a higher sensitivity than other simpler scales.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
A seizure is abnormal electrical activity in the brain causing transient changes in behaviour, movement, sensation, or awareness. These discharges are typically spontaneous, uncontrolled and transitory. Seizures are a sign of an underlying disorder.
- Idiopathic (self-originating and without known cause, most epileptics)
- Symptomatic (known medical or traumatic cause) Status epilepticus (SE) is defined as:
  - A seizure lasting >5 minutes, or
  - Recurrent seizures without return to baseline consciousness between episodes.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Motor: Tonic-clonic movements (stiffening/jerking), focal limb twitching, head nodding
- Non-motor: Staring, confusion, lip-smacking, sensory hallucinations, unresponsiveness
- Autonomic: Tachycardia, hypersalivation, apnoea, incontinence
- Post-ictal phase: Fatigue, confusion, headache, amnesia
- Prolonged seizures or SE can be associated with hypoxia, hypercarbia, lactic/respiratory acidosis, hyperthermia, hypertension, tachycardia, hypo/hyperglycaemia, hyperkalaemia

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Anticipate potential airway obstruction; while tongue trauma typically poses minimal risk, dental fracture carries significant aspiration hazard
- Midazolam can be administered intravenously, intramuscularly, intranasally, or via the buccal routes
- Pharmacological management of non-convulsive or atypical seizure types requires consultation with the on-call doctor or RFDS medical support
- Implement environmental safety measures by removing hazards and providing head protection with padding. Apply physical restraint only when necessary to prevent immediate injury
- Continuous airway assessment and intervention are required throughout ictal and post- ictal phases to ensure patency and prevent aspiration
- Generalised seizure activity should be considered an emergency where:
  - The seizure is prolonged (> 5 minutes), or
  - Multiple seizures occur, or
  - The patient doesn’t regain consciousness

**Focal seizure activity associated where:**
  - The seizure is prolonged (> 5 minutes) and is associated with a GCS ≤ 12
  - Common causes: Epilepsy, hypoglycaemia, CNS infection, head trauma, drug toxicity, stroke, alcohol withdrawal
  - Red flags: First seizure, trauma during seizure, signs of infection (fever, rash), persistent post-ictal unresponsiveness

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Protect from injury whilst patient seizing
- Consider IM Midazolam in consultation with on-call doctor
- 5mg Intramuscular, intranasally or buccally if appropriate
- Complete Primary Survey
- Open (where possible), clear and maintain the airway. Never stick fingers in the mouth
- Suction if required and where possible
- Insert appropriate airway adjunct; consider NPA if trismus present (do not attempt to force an airway into the seizing patient's mouth)
- Lateral position if unresponsive (use left lateral position for pregnant women)
- Oxygen with assisted ventilation if indicated
- Identify and address cause where possible
- Full Vital Signs Survey, particularly GCS, BGL, Pulse Oximetry and Temperature
- Note length of time
- Complete Secondary Survey once seizure has terminated
- Limb LEAD ECG, being mindful of muscular artefact
- Consider urinalysis where appropriate, i.e., pregnant woman
- ESO’s, MESOs and other similar levels should have a low threshold for calling the on-call doctor

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Establish Vascular access
- Consider blood draw, where appropriate, for i-STAT testing: Chem8 (lactate and electrolytes)
- Consider IV Midazolam, titrated to effect, in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider IV glucose if related to low BGL

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Complications and side effects of seizures can include:
  - Incontinence
  - Secondary injury (including intra-oral trauma)
  - Aspiration
  - Hypoxia
  - Hyperthermia
  - Dysrhythmias
  - Brain damage
  - Death
  - ESO’s, MESOs and other similar levels should have a low threshold for calling the current poison permit holder or RFDS

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Syncope, also referred to as transient loss of consciousness (T-LOC), is defined as a rapid, self-limited loss of consciousness due to global cerebral hypoperfusion, resulting in spontaneous and complete recovery after the event. Syncope is a common prehospital presentation.
Presyncope describes symptoms such as dizziness, light-headedness, or weakness that may precede syncope but do not result in actual loss of consciousness.
Patients may describe syncopal episodes using terms such as “faint,” “black out,” “collapse,” or “passing out.” Syncope can be classified into the following major categories:
- Orthostatic syncope: Occurs secondary to postural changes, typically due to a failure of compensatory mechanisms to maintain blood pressure upon standing
- Reflex (neurally mediated) syncope: Results from an inappropriate autonomic nervous system response to a trigger or stimulus (e.g., vasovagal, situational, carotid sinus hypersensitivity)
- Cardiac syncope: Caused by a reduction in cardiac output due to arrhythmias or structural heart disease, leading to global cerebral hypoperfusion
- PLUS: SYNCOPE WITH additional provisional diagnosis i.e., syncope plus shortness of breath (PE), syncope plus abdominal or flank pain (AAA), syncope plus severe headache (Subarachnoid)

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Sudden, brief loss of consciousness with spontaneous return to baseline
- Rapid onset and short duration (typically less than a minute)
- Loss of postural tone (patient may fall)
- Prodromal symptoms: Light-headedness, dizziness, nausea, sweating, visual changes, or palpitations
- Post-event: Brief confusion or disorientation is common; most patients recover quickly and completely
- May complain of pain
- Injuries: May occur due to fall (e.g., head trauma, fractures)

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- A 12 lead ECG should be acquired in most, if not all these patients
- Syncope is most commonly caused by a transient reduction in systemic blood pressure, typically not due to cardiac pathology. Promptly positioning the patient supine is generally sufficient to restore cerebral perfusion and consciousness

- Sustained unconsciousness following an episode of fainting is not consistent with syncope. If a patient remains unresponsive, alternative or additional diagnoses must be considered and managed accordingly
- Maintaining a patient in an upright position after syncope can result in prolonged cerebral hypoperfusion, increasing the risk of brain injury or death. Therefore, supine positioning is strongly recommended
- Syncopal episodes occurring while the patient is supine or recumbent are highly suggestive of a cardiac aetiology and warrant urgent evaluation
- Persistent confusion lasting longer than 30 seconds post-event may indicate a postictal state, raising suspicion for seizure rather than syncope
- Patients over 40 years of age who present with a first episode of syncope require thorough assessment, as the likelihood of underlying pathology is increased
- The presence of abnormal vital signs following syncope, or associated symptoms such as chest pain, dyspnoea, severe headache, neurological deficits, or exertional onset, should prompt consideration of alternative or additional diagnoses beyond straightforward vasovagal syncope
- Although cardiac arrhythmias may be transient, clinical management should be guided by the patient’s condition rather than the specific rhythm identified on ECG
- While most cases of syncope (fainting) are not life-threatening, a small percentage (around 10%) are caused by serious underlying cardiac conditions
- Sudden severe blood loss; external or internal
- Postural hypotension (sometimes due to medication)
- Hypoglycaemia, (diabetic or due to low food intake)
- Cerebro-vascular Accidents (C.V.A.) or Transient Ischaemic Attacks (T.I.A.)
- T.I.A. resembles a C.V.A. (stroke), but is of short duration; even if recovered, always transport to medical care for assessment
- Can it be a seizure? Look for signs…

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey (correct any issues identified)
- Consider airway management if still unconscious, place patient in lateral position (left lateral position for pregnant women)
- If conscious, place supine
- Consider Oxygen if indicated
- Vital Sign Survey, particularly blood pressure and blood glucose
- Consider oral glucose intake if suitable, or IM glucagon if low BGL is a cause Limb LEAD ECG

- Secondary/CNS Survey, (BE)FAST, cranial nerve assessment, etc
- Clinicians should have low threshold for calling the on-call doctor

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Apply cardiac monitor
- Manage cardiac arrhythmia
- Consider establishing vascular access
- Consider blood draw for i-STAT: Chem8, check electrolytes, lactate, haemoglobin) where available
- Consider fluid therapy
- Consider appropriate antiemetic
- Consider IV glucose
- Consider analgesia if pain is a cause

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- The initial evaluation of patients with syncope should be focused on the early identification and management of serious and life-threatening non-cardiac or cardiac causes
- Non-lethal strangulation in domestic and family abuse may be a cause of unexplained syncope
- Determining the cause of a syncope can be complex in some patients and clinicians should have a low threshold for referring these patients for further medical review
- For some possible causes, consider AEIOUTIPS
- Alcohol, Arrhythmia, Epilepsy, Electrolytes, Insulin, Overdose, Oxygen, Opiates, Trauma, Temperature, Infection, Poisons, Psychiatric, Stroke, Shock, Syncope
- Patient Education: Advise on prevention of recurrence (e.g., avoid triggers, stay hydrated, rise slowly from sitting/lying)

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Unconsciousness refers to a state where a patient cannot be roused or do not respond to external stimuli, including sound, touch or other activities. Clinical unconsciousness can range from Syncope to coma. It is often associated with the patient having a Glasgow Coma Scale of 8 or less or being unresponsive on the AVPU scale. The condition requires immediate medical attention to address the underlying causes and stabilise the patient.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Unarousable and unresponsive: No purposeful response to verbal or tactile stimuli; patient may only exhibit reflex movements
- Absent or abnormal protective reflexes: Such as gag, cough, or swallowing
- Varied respiratory patterns: Normal, irregular or absent breathing
- Altered vital signs: May include hypotension, tachycardia, bradycardia, low or high blood sugar depending on underlying cause
- Possible associated features: Seizures, trauma, incontinence, tongue biting, abnormal posturing, or evidence of drug/toxin exposure
- Patients that cannot be roused or do not respond appropriately to external stimuli due to:
  - Hypovolaemia
  - Dysrhythmia
  - Hypoxia
  - Hypo/hyperglycaemia (hypoglycaemia may present as a focal neurological deficit or coma, whilst hyperglycaemia can lead to complications that can also result in unconsciousness.)
  - Toxins / Overdose
  - Hypo/hyperthermia
  - Sepsis
  - CVA / brain injury / infection / malignancy

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
**Scene Safety:**
  - Prioritise the safety of all responders and bystanders before patient assessment or intervention
  - Where feasible, establish the likely cause of unconsciousness before approaching, especially in confined spaces or hazardous environments. Atmospheric testing is recommended prior to entry if exposure to toxic gases, chemicals, or other environmental hazards are suspected

- Obtain a detailed history from bystanders or witnesses regarding the onset of symptoms, preceding events, and any relevant environmental factors
- Document current medications, recent drug or alcohol intake, and be alert to the possibility of polypharmacy or overdose
- Identify relevant medical history, including chronic conditions, allergies, and the presence of medical alert devices or identification
- Assess and manage airway compromise early
- Assess and manage airway compromise as a priority. Consider both acute obstruction and aspiration risk, particularly in patients with active vomiting, excessive secretions, or altered mental status
- All patients with an altered GCS (i.e. abnormal for the individual) should have a BGL test preformed and managed appropriately if symptomatic

**Common causes:**
  - Low brain oxygen: Hypoxia, cardiac arrest, respiratory failure
  - Cardiovascular: Arrhythmias, myocardial infarction, syncope
  - Metabolic: Hypoglycaemia, diabetic ketoacidosis, overdose, intoxication
  - Neurological: Stroke, seizure, head injury, brain tumour, infection
  - Potential for multi-system involvement: Trauma, intoxication, and medical conditions often coexist

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey with c-spine consideration
- Manage catastrophic haemorrhage
- Open, clear and maintain the airway
- Suction if required
- Insert oropharyngeal or nasopharyngeal airway (OPA/NPA)
- Consider breathing pattern
- Lateral position if unresponsive (use left lateral position for pregnant women). In unconscious trauma patients consider the Lateral Trauma Position
- Oxygen, with assisted ventilation if required (be aware of inadequate tidal volume)
- Full Vital Signs Survey, particularly GCS, BGL and Pulse Oximetry
- Limb LEAD ECG
- Complete Secondary Survey and manage injuries as required
- Keep patient warm/initiate cooling where appropriate
- Clinicians should have a low threshold for calling on-call doctor

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider advanced airway if tolerating OPA/NPA
- Apply cardiac monitor
- Manage cardiac dysrhythmia where possible and acquire 12 lead ECG
- Establishing vascular access
- Consider obtaining blood for i-STAT sampling if time permits. Chem8 in all patients where practicable and available

**Manage any abnormalities:**
  - Medication, drug or other toxin toxicity
  - Vital sign abnormalities
  - Consider IV fluids

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Some pre-hospital procedures such as injections may precipitate dizziness in susceptible patients. Patient position should be considered to avoid potential injury
- Unconsciousness of unknown cause should be presumed stroke until proven otherwise
- Beware of QTc intervals >460msec in polypharmacy overdose and TBI; can be an early warning sign of dangerous dysrhythmia
- See AEIOUTIPS for more information on possible cause

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Vertigo is a sensation of movement, often described as spinning or a feeling of being pulled in one direction, even when you are standing still. Vertigo is a symptom, not a diagnosis, and results from disturbances in the vestibular system. Vertigo is categorised into peripheral (inner ear or vestibular nerve involvement) and central (brainstem or cerebellum involvement). The main causes are benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV), vestibular neuritis and cerebrovascular accidents (CVA)

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
Vertigo Peripheral Central
- Spinning sensation (self or environment)
- Worsened by head movement
- Nausea/Vomiting
- Unsteadiness/gait disturbance
- Sudden onset
- Severe intensity
- Positional trigger (e.g., rolling over in bed)
- Horizontal nystagmus
- Hearing loss/tinnitus
- No neurological deficits
- Abnormal head impulse
- Gradual or abrupt onset
- Mild or constant intensity
- Focal neurological signs (e.g., diplopia, dysarthria, ataxia)
- Vertical or directional changing nystagmus
- No hearing involvement
- Unstable gait or inability to walk
- Slurred speech and double vision (diplopia)

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Sudden onset of severe vertigo with other neurological signs warrants immediate medical attention and should always be transport to further care
- The presence of other symptoms, such as hearing loss, tinnitus or neurological deficits can help in differentiating between different causes
- Hearing loss is a common feature vertigo. However, sudden hearing loss in acute vestibular syndrome can be a sign of an anterior inferior cerebellar infarct, due to involvement of the internal auditory artery. Hearing loss is not typical of vestibular neuritis
- A thorough medical history, including any previous head injuries or neurological conditions is important
- Patients whose vertigo is initiated by movement and only lasts less than 2 minutes at a time are most likely suffering from benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV) and would benefit from a Dix-Hallpike test followed by treatment with an Epley manoeuvre
- Reg flags: LOC or seizure, headache, neck pain or recent trauma, sudden persistent vertigo unrelieved by rest, visual disturbances
- Underlying causes of vertigo to be considered:

- Peripheral Vertigo, caused by BBPV, Meniere’s disease, vestibular neuritis/labyrinthitis
- Central Vertigo, which can have associated symptoms such as neurological deficits, ataxia and headache, stroke, brain tumour or multiple sclerosis

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Consider positioning for comfort
- Vital signs (postural BP included where appropriate, BGL)
- Complete Secondary Survey
- Consider FAST
- Consider HINTS (head impulse, nystagmus, and test of skew) exam
- Check for nystagmus
- Consider otoscope if trained and symptomatic
- Consider Dix-Hallpike test if appropriate (i.e., nil trauma, neck stiffness, etc.)
- Consider Epley manoeuvre if symptoms consistent with BPPV

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider ECG
- Consider vascular access if required, especially if central vertigo suspected
- Consider IV fluids if required
- Consider antiemetics
- Consider IM Prochlorperazine in consultation with current poison permit holder

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Patient education: advise around positional triggers and safety measures such as driving, working at heights, operating machinery, etc during episodes
- HINTS Exam (for Acute Vestibular Syndrome): Used to differentiate central vs. peripheral vertigo in continuous vertigo with spontaneous nystagmus:
  - Head-Impulse Test: Abnormal suggests peripheral
  - Nystagmus: Unidirectional = peripheral; bidirectional/vertical = central
  - Test of Skew, cover/uncover eye test: Eye misalignment suggests central
  - All 3 negative suggests peripheral. If 1 or more positive, treat as central 18 CARDIAC

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Acute Aortic Dissection is a rare but extremely serious vascular emergency that can be challenging to diagnose in the pre-hospital setting. Occurs when the innermost layer of the aorta tears, resulting in the separation of the vessel layers and creation of a false lumen in the aorta wall. Any abrupt chest, back or abdominal pain described as ripping or tearing in nature and that is associated with evidence of perfusion deficits or focal neurological signs (nerve, spinal cord, or brain) should be considered as an acute aortic dissection until proven otherwise. Chest pain in the presence of an upper limb weakness is aortic dissection until proven otherwise.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Sudden acute chest pain that is excruciating at onset and is often described as sharp, ripping or tearing in quality, but is sometimes tight in nature and can be difficult to distinguish from angina
- Pain is linked to the location of the dissection:
  - Anterior: ascending aorta
  - Neck / jaw: arch of aorta
  - Interscapular: descending aorta
  - Lumbar / abdominal: Subdiaphragmatic
  - Pulse deficits between heart beats
  - Significant systolic BP differences between limbs may be present
  - Pallor, vomiting and diaphoresis
  - Neurological deficits: limb weakness, numbness, tingling, pain or paraplegia
  - Chest pain with associated neurological symptoms
  - Syncope
  - Shock (hypotension, hypovolaemia, tamponade)

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Proximal acute aortic dissection may affect coronary arteries and potentially mimic an acute myocardial infarction (AMI)
- Differential diagnosis include AMI, pulmonary embolism, spontaneous pneumothorax, pleurisy, Pericarditis/Myocarditis, CVA, and acute abdomen
- Lethal complications associated with acute aortic dissection include aortic rupture, cardiac tamponade and visceral ischemia

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary survey
- Position patient appropriately
- Conduct vital sign survey, particularly bilateral BP
- Consider oxygen therapy if indicated
- Consider antiemetic
- Contact on-call Dr as soon as practicable

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Apply cardiac monitor
- Apply defib pads
- Establish large bore vascular access
- Consider fluid therapy if hypotensive
- Administer suitable pain relief as soon as practicable

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Increased risk in males, >50 years of age, history of hypertension, cardiac or valvular surgery, cardiac catheterisation, drug use, stress and blunt trauma
- Medical conditions affecting the connective tissue, for example, Marfan’s syndrome
- Increased risk in pregnancy
- Risk of rapid clinical deterioration
- Risk of cardiac arrest
- There is little direct evidence of the effectiveness of TXA for dissecting aortic aneurysm

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Acute coronary syndrome (ACS) represents a myriad of clinical presentations sharing common symptoms, ranging from worsening angina through to ST-elevation MI (STEMI).
Most patients present with prolonged (>10mins) or recurrent central or left sided chest discomfort described as tightness, heaviness, squeezing or crushing. The absence of these symptoms does not rule out ACS. Patients suffering from Acute Coronary Syndromes do not always present with chest pain and are subsequently frequently misdiagnosed.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**Signs & Symptoms Suggestive of ACS:**
- Chest pain or discomfort of presumed cardiac origin (pressure, tightness, heaviness, or burning sensation)
- Acute or subacute shortness of breath without clear alternative explanation (notably, up to one-third of ACS patients may present without chest pain)
- Referred pain, i.e., to the jaw/teeth, interscapular pain (pain between the shoulder blades), epigastric pain or discomfort
- Unexplained upper limb or neck discomfort (especially in female patients)
- Dizziness, light-headedness, or transient loss of consciousness
- Nausea, vomiting, or diaphoresis (excessive sweating) without obvious cause
- Palpitations or dysrhythmias (abnormal heart rhythms)
- Generalized weakness or malaise
- Right-sided chest pain (less common but possible)
- Feeling of impending doom

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Suspicion of acute myocardial ischemia (AMI) should be based on a thorough patient history and their clinical presentation. A normal or non-diagnostic ECG does not exclude ACS, and ongoing assessment is essential in patients with persistent symptoms
- Multiple 12-lead ECGs are recommended in patients with ongoing symptoms and high clinical suspicion for ACS. If the initial ECG is non-diagnostic, repeat ECGs should be performed at least every 15 minutes to capture evolving ischemic changes. The absence of ischaemic signs on the ECG does not exclude AMI. AMI is diagnosed by presenting history, serial ECGs and serial enzyme tests
- Early defibrillator pad placement is advised for all cases of confirmed STEMI to facilitate rapid defibrillation if required

- Right ventricular and inferior STEMIs (ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF) are commonly associated with significant hypotension and bradycardia. Glyceryl trinitrate (GTN) should be avoided unless specifically directed by the on-call medical officer or Royal Flying Doctor Service (RFDS). Early fluid resuscitation should be initiated if indicated
- Patients with a history of diabetes are at increased risk of atypical ACS presentations, including the absence of chest pain. High clinical suspicion is warranted in these patients, even in the absence of classic symptoms
- Glyceryl Trinitrate (GTN) Administration and Risk of Hypotension
- GTN administration can precipitate severe hypotension in susceptible patients, particularly those with right ventricular, inferior STEMI, bradycardia, though this is a rare event. Careful patient selection and monitoring are required
- New onset angina should be considered unstable until proven otherwise, and managed promptly with appropriate risk stratification and intervention
- Suspected ACS-related pain that has spontaneously resolved warrants investigation in hospital
- If within the window for reperfusion, rapid transport or evacuation should organised

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Reassurance (continuous)
- Consider Oxygen (titrate to 95%)
- Limit patients’ exertion as much as practically possible
- Vital Sign Survey (monitor regularly including pre-and-post intervention pain scores)
- 12 lead ECG where trained to do so
- If STEMI or STEMI Equivalent, consider placement of defibrillation pads
- Send ECG to on-call doctor for review
- Aspirin (300mg)
- Early Glyceryl Trinitrate (GTN) (if BP suitable)
- Consider Secondary Survey

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- 12 lead ECG
- Consider alternative views (inferior V4R, V5R, V6R) (posterior V7, V8, V9, MCL1) where indicated
- Gain vascular access, bilateral cannulas, 18g or wider bore Management Cont.
- Consider appropriate analgesia (Fentanyl preferred in consultation with on-call doctor)
- Consider antiemetic if required
- Consider Enoxaparin in consultation with on-call doctor

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- ESO’s should hold a low threshold for requesting further medical support
- The terminology used in this cohort of presentations continues to change with STEMI also known as STEACS (ST segment elevation acute coronary syndrome) and NSTEACS (non- ST segment elevation acute coronary syndrome)

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Acute Decompensated Heart Failure (ADHF) is the sudden or gradual worsening of signs and symptoms of heart failure, resulting in acute respiratory distress and/or systemic congestion. It often occurs in patients with pre-existing chronic heart failure but can also be the initial presentation of cardiac dysfunction from a myocardial infarction (MI) for example. ADHF can be a result of right (peripheral), left (pulmonary) or biventricular failure.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
Right Ventricular Failure Left Ventricular Failure Additional Signs & Symptoms
- Peripheral Oedema
- Hypotension
- Jugular vein distention
- Hepatomegaly (enlarged liver)
- Ascites (in advanced cases)
- Acute Dyspnoea (SOB)
- Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea
- Orthopnoea (worse lying flat)
- Tachypnoea
- Crepes on auscultation
- Hypoxia or low o2 saturation
- Pink frothy sputum
- Chest discomfort (MI, ischemia possible)
- Tachycardia
- Cool, clammy skin
- Cyanosis
- Altered mental status
- Cardiac Arrhythmia
- Lethargy

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Right sided failure: Avoid GTN (or other nitrates) as this can lessen preload and exacerbate symptoms
- Ensure oxygen is administered, even if the patient is trying to reject it as rejection may be due to hypoxia. Patience and coaching are essential in that circumstance
- Mental confusion or altered level of consciousness may occur due to hypoxia
- Ensure oxygen is administered, even if the patient tries to reject it; rejection may be due to hypoxia. Patience and coaching are essential in that circumstance
- Coughing is a frequent complaint that is suggestive of LVF
- Do not allow patient to walk
- Use clinical judgment to differentiate from sepsis, pneumonia, or other causes of dyspnoea

- Causes to consider include infarction, valvular regurgitation, myocarditis, cardiomyopathy, medication toxicity, PE, dysrhythmias, anaemia, drowning, high altitude or decompression illnesses, burns, pancreatitis, or pregnancy
- Regardless of the causes of pulmonary oedema (cardiogenic or non-cardiogenic), the presenting features of dyspnoea and tachycardia remain the same

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Open, clear and maintain the airway
- Administer oxygen with assisted ventilation if required or patient is exhausted
- If blood pressure permits, position sitting or semi-recumbent, preferably with legs hanging down
- Vital Signs
- Limb LEAD ECG

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- 12 LEAD ECG
- Establish large bore vascular access
- Consider fluid therapy if hypovolaemia is clearly indicated (cautiously administered)
- Consider Aspirin if cardiogenic cause such as MI
- Consider GTN in uncontrolled hypertension (acute cardiac pulmonary oedema) in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider frusemide in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider Clexane (enoxaparin) in consultation with on-call doctor
- Manage underlying condition if known as per relevant

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Cardiac arrhythmias are abnormal heart rhythms that occur when the electrical signals that coordinate the heart's beats do not work properly. The normal heart rate is usually between 60 and 100 beats per minute. These disturbances can cause the heart to beat too fast (tachycardia), too slow (bradycardia), or irregularly. Arrhythmias can be harmless or life-threatening, depending on their type and severity. Management strategies depend on the type and severity of the arrhythmia, including pharmacologic therapy, electrical cardioversion, or electrical pacing as examples.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**Unstable Bradycardia** (HR <60 bpm):
  - Signs: Hypotension (<90 mmHg), altered mental status, cool/pale skin, poor perfusion
  - Symptoms: Dizziness, chest pain, shortness of breath, fatigue, syncope

**Unstable Tachycardia** (HR >100 bpm, often >150 bpm):
  - Signs: Hypotension (<90 mmHg), signs of shock, altered mental status
  - Symptoms: Chest pain, palpitations, shortness of breath, syncope

**Supraventricular Tachycardia (SVT)**:
  - Regular, narrow complex tachycardia
  - Symptoms: Palpitations, lightheadedness, chest discomfort

**Atrial Fibrillation (AF)**:
  - Irregular, narrow complex tachycardia
  - Symptoms: Palpitations, fatigue, breathlessness, dizziness

**Broad Complex Tachycardia** (suspected VT):
  - Regular, wide complex tachycardia - treat as VT until proven otherwise

**Torsades de Pointes**:
  - Polymorphic VT, associated with prolonged QT interval

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Any of the following management should be made with consultation with either the current MinRes poison permit holder or with the Royal Flying Doctor Service (RFDS)
- All patients that present with bradycardia of cardiac aetiology must receive a comprehensive clinical assessment that includes dynamically assessing all pertinent vital signs, performing a 12 LEAD ECG and attaining a thorough patient history
- Bradycardia can be induced by numerous processes which include ACS, toxicology/toxinology exposure, sepsis, trauma, environmental exposure, electrolyte imbalance and endocrine disorders
- All anti-arrhythmic treatments (physical [vagal] manoeuvres, drugs, cardioversion, pacing) have the potential to make the rhythm worse rather than better, causing clinical deterioration. Furthermore, most anti-arrhythmic drugs cause myocardial depression, which may worsen heart failure or hypotension

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Continuous reassurance
- Limit patients’ exertion as much as is practicable
- Vital Signs (monitor regularly including pre-and-post intervention pain scores)
- Consider Oxygen if indicated
- ECG
- Consider Secondary Survey

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- 12 lead ECG
- Vascular access
- Consider blood draw for troponin if not time critical
- Consider appropriate analgesia
- Consider anti-emetic

**Unstable Bradycardia:**
  - Address reversible causes
  - If inferior/right ventricular MI with bradycardia and hypotension, consider Atropine and IV fluids for cardiogenic shock (in consultation with on-call doctor)
  - Manage hypovolaemia, hypoxia and toxins as indicated
  - Consider atropine and/or electrical pacing (in consultation with on-call doctor)

**Unstable Tachycardia:**
  - Consider patient in peri-arrest state; apply defib pads
  - Synchronised cardioversion with appropriate sedation (in consultation with on-call doctor)

**Supraventricular Tachycardia (regular, narrow complex):**
  - Modified Valsalva in the first instance where appropriate
  - Consider Metoprolol (in consultation with on-call doctor)
  - Consider Adenosine only in unstable patient (in consultation with on-call doctor)

**Narrow Complex Tachycardia (irregular):**
  - If Atrial Fibrillation, IV fluids may cause reversion to sinus rhythm
  - Consider Amiodarone (in consultation with on-call doctor)
  - Consider Metoprolol for rate control (in consultation with on-call doctor)
  - IV Magnesium may be considered (in consultation with on-call doctor)

**Broad Complex Tachycardia (regular):**
  - Synchronised cardioversion with appropriate sedation (in consultation with on-call doctor)
  - If cardioversion unsuccessful, consider Amiodarone (in consultation with on-call doctor)

**Torsades de Pointes:**
  - Consider Magnesium Sulphate (in consultation with on-call doctor)

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Resuscitation equipment should be at hand for any of the above presentations
- Consideration should be made that any unstable patient is peri-arrest and time critical.
- ESO’s should have a very low threshold for calling for help
- Clinical deterioration is likely
- Adenosine has a very short half-life. It should be administered rapidly, via a 3-way tap, followed by a saline flush, through a large bore IV (18 – 14g) as close to the heart as practicable, such as the cubital fossa

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Hyperkalaemia occurs when extracellular potassium increases, interfering with the normal action potential generation, having a detrimental effect in both skeletal muscle function and profoundly affecting normal cardiac function. Hyperkalaemia is defined as a serum potassium greater than 5.2 mmol (mEq/L) and can occur from any condition that causes an increase in extracellular potassium. Clinical effects depending on severity from mild, moderate to severe. Hyperkalaemia tends to be an incidental finding during examination and normally don’t manifest until there is moderate degree of hyperkalaemia (>6mmol/L).

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- General weakness and/or paraesthesia, muscle cramps, paralysis
- Lethargy and confusion
- Nausea, vomiting and/or diarrhoea
- Signs of underlying cause, i.e., renal impairment, burns, metabolic acidosis
- ECG changes consistent with hyperkalaemia

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- High-risk groups: Patients with chronic kidney disease, recent dialysis, crush injuries, prolonged down time, or those taking potassium-sparing medications (e.g., ACE inhibitors, potassium supplements)
- The definitive treatment for hyperkalaemia is dialysis (a consideration required when choosing receiving hospital)
- Calcium Gluconate and Sodium Bicarbonate must not be given together in the same line due to risk of precipitation

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- ECG
- Perform vital sign survey
- Secondary/CNS survey

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- 12 lead ECG (assess for changes associated with hyperkalaemia)
- Gain IV Access, ensure patency (2x large bore)
- Fluid resuscitation (unless overloaded)
- i-STAT testing (if timing permits, CHEM8+, K, reference range 2.0-9.0 mmol)
- Consider Salbutamol nebuliser in consultation with on-call doctor
- 5mg nebs once only
- Consider Calcium Gluconate 10% in consultation with on-call doctor
- 4.4mmols, repeated every 10 minutes to a max of 13.2mmols (3 doses)
- Consider Sodium Bicarbonate 8.4% in consultation with on-call doctor
- 100ml IV/IO once only
- Consider Insulin and Glucose in consultation with on-call doctor

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Calcium Gluconate provides immediate stabilisation of the myocardium; however, it does not reduce serum potassium levels
- Sodium Bicarbonate 8.4% will reduce serum potassium levels by 0.5 – 1 mmol/L and provide temporary effect whilst the underlying cause is treated
- Continuous salbutamol nebuliser reduces serum potassium levels by 0.5 – 1 mmol/L within 30 minutes
- A typical administration of 10 units of regular insulin can reduce serum potassium levels by 0.5 – 1 mmol/L within 10 – 20 mins, with effects lasting 4 – 6 hours

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Hypertension (high blood pressure) is defined as a persistent elevation of blood pressure (BP) in the arteries and is typically asymptomatic (“silent killer”). Hypertension can be primary (essential [90-95% of cases]) where it has no single identifiable cause and may develop due to genetic and lifestyle factors, or secondary which is caused by an underlying medical condition.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Headache, (especially occipital in the mornings)
- Visual disturbances, dizziness
- Chest pain, palpitations
- Shortness of breath
- Peripheral oedema
- Epistaxis (nose bleeds)
- ECG changes (hypertrophy, LBBB, etc)

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Some patients’ BP may be elevated in certain medical situations (“white coat”)
- Allow the patient to rest, seated or semi-recumbent for example, for 5 minutes before taking their BP
- Consider taking a manual BP first as a baseline
- Ensure medics are using the correct size BP cuff for the patient
- There is NO place for a systolic only BP measurement in this context
- Patients with severely elevated BP associated with life-threatening complications, myocardial or cerebral ischaemia for example, must be treated urgently
- Severely elevated BP (180/100 mmHg) without symptoms can be treated semi-urgently within days
- Hypertensive urgency, BP ≥ 180/110 mmHg with symptoms (headache/dizziness), should be treated within hours
- Hypertensive emergency, BP ≥ 220/140 mmHg, with or without symptoms, require urgent treatment
- Rapid reduction of a severely elevated BP or hypertensive urgency may cause more harm than good

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Vital Signs
- Take multiple BP readings, several minutes apart
- Limb LEAD ECG
- Consider Secondary Survey
- Consider urinalysis (specifically protein)
- Consider pregnancy test if females
- Consider GTN in hypertensive emergency in consultation with on-call doctor

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- 12 LEAD ECG
- Consider anti-hypertensive medication depending on cause in consultation with on- call doctor
- Amlodipine, Metoprolol, Frusemide
- Consider IV metoprolol in hypertensive emergency in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider analgesia if hypertensive secondary to pain

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Reference Ranges
- Normal: <120/80 mmHg
- Elevated: 120–129/<80 mmHg
- Stage 1 Hypertension: 130–139/80–89 mmHg
- Stage 2 Hypertension: ≥140/90 mmHg
- If available, patients can take a blood pressure machine away and record their own BP morning, noon and night for several days for a more accurate estimation of their BP 19 RESPIRATORY

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Dyspnoea refers to the sensation of shortness of breath or difficulty in breathing. It can be a subjective experience, meaning that it is based on the individual's perception of their breathing difficulty. Dyspnoea can range from mild to severe distress and can occur during rest or physical activity.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Distressed, anxious, fighting to breath, exhausted, altered level of consciousness
- Pale, sweaty and can be cyanosed
- Single words responses or unable to speak
- Stridor, crackles, wheeze, silent chest
- Breathing rate of < 6 or > 20
- Accessory muscle use, intercostal recession and/or tracheal tug, prolonged expiratory phases
- Tachycardia > 120 or bradycardia < 60

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Hypoxic aggression / irritability
- Oxygen rejection may be due to hypoxia
- Never cease oxygen administration if patient's level of consciousness or breathing deteriorates; oxygen almost never depresses breathing in the field and can usually be attributed exhaustion and fatigue (especially of the diaphragm). Exhaustion can depress breathing and/or level of consciousness, even if the patient is given oxygen
- Whilst hyperventilation is usually associated with severe emotional stress, do not over- diagnose "hyperventilation" in the field. Your patient could have a pulmonary embolus or other serious problem
- ACS can manifest as dyspnoea and may be the only indication of an acute myocardial infarction, therefore the need for a 12-Lead ECG should be considered
- Oedematous upper airway of rapid onset and airway obstruction due to neck trauma have a high potential to evolve into complete airway obstruction. Neck trauma can cause rapid oedema and complete airway obstruction
- Partial airway obstruction may progress to complete obstruction. Limit interventions to only those essential to maintain oxygenation. Always prepare for the management of a complete obstruction
- Causes to dyspnoea to consider are airway obstruction, cardiovascular compromise, neurological causes, respiratory compromise, thoracic musculoskeletal compromise

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Continuous reassurance
- Treat airway obstruction
- If respiratory distress is caused by exposure to any hazardous substance, remove substance or patient if safe to do so. Assistance from site-based Emergency Response Teams, Emergency Service (DFES) or other appropriate resource may be required
- Position patient appropriately (sitting patient upright or in a position of comfort; lateral position if unconscious, consider appropriate position in suspected spinal injury)
- Conduct Vital Sign Survey, particularly respiratory rate and pulse oximetry
- Consider oxygen therapy; titrate SpO2 using the appropriate mask to a target of 94– 98%
- Ventilate the patient if required
- Auscultate for diagnostic clues: Wheezing [asthma], crackles [infection, oedema], pleuritic rub, silent chest [severe asthma]
- Limb LEAD ECG
- Consider Secondary Survey

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Manage underlying condition if known
- Wheeze/silent chest: Asthma guideline
- Crackles/Pleuritic rub: Chest Infection guideline
- Pain/Trauma: Pain Management / Chest Trauma guideline
- Consider advanced Airway if indicated
- Consider IV access
- 12 lead ECG

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Asthma is a chronic inflammatory disease of the airways characterised by episodic and reversible airflow obstruction, bronchial hyperresponsiveness, and excessive mucus production. Acute exacerbations are marked by a rapid worsening of symptoms due to airway inflammation, smooth muscle contraction (bronchoconstriction), and increased mucus secretion, which together can progress to respiratory failure if untreated. During an asthma attack, the airways become inflamed, oedematous, and narrowed, often in response to specific triggers such as allergens, respiratory infections, or irritants. This pathophysiological process results in symptoms including wheezing, cough, chest tightness, and shortness of breath. In chronic or poorly controlled asthma, persistent inflammation may lead to airway remodelling and irreversible airflow limitation.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Tachypnoea, dyspnoea, wheeze, cyanosis
- Chest Tightness, accessory muscle use, tripod positioning
- Tachycardia, diaphoresis
- Shortening answers/replies to questions i.e., one-word answers

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- If doubt exists as to whether the patient is suffering from asthma or anaphylaxis, treat as anaphylaxis
- Acute Asthma is classified into three severity categories:
  - Mild/moderate: Patient is able to walk and speak in whole sentences in one breath
  - Severe: Unable to speak in complete sentences, visibly breathless, increased work of breathing, oxygen saturation 90-94%.
  - Life-threatening: Drowsy, collapsed, exhausted, cyanotic, poor respiratory effort, auxiliary muscle use, oxygen saturation <90%, altered mental status, bradycardia, quiet chest
  - Pulse oximetry is not a reliable indicator of asthma severity and cannot be used to determine improvement in the clinical condition
  - Respiratory symptoms such as dyspnoea and wheezing are non-specific indications of asthma. Differential diagnosis should be considered
  - Patients presenting with acute asthma may deteriorate rapidly without any warning of sudden clinical decline
  - If IPPV is required, care should be taken to ensure the patient is not over-ventilated by allowing for a prolonged expiratory phase

- Relievers are best given through a spacer, if available. Use 1 puff at a time and ask the patient to take 4 breaths from the spacer after each puff. Wait 4 minutes. If there is no improvement, give another 4 puffs. If little or no improvement, CALL on-call doctor IMMEDIATELY

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Position patient appropriately, sitting patient upright or in a position of comfort
- Conduct Vital Sign Survey, particularly respiratory rate and pulse oximetry
