- Consider oxygen therapy; titrate SpO2 using the appropriate mask to a target of 92– 95% for adults and at ≥ 95% for children
- Administer Salbutamol (MDI [via spacer]/Nebuliser)
- Give 4-12 puffs of a blue/grey reliever inhaler (puffer) via a spacer (1 puff – 4 breaths)
- If severe/life-threatening presentation, administer EpiPen

**Ventilate the patient if required:**
  - Extreme care should be taken when ventilating an asthmatic patient; gently and at a rate of no more than 4-6 breaths per minute. This allows for adequate exhalation and avoids air trapping which may otherwise lead to reduced blood pressure and cardiac arrest
  - Officers may assist the patient in the administration of their own medication where clinically indicated
  - Be alert for rapid deterioration

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider Ipratropium Bromide (nebulised) if indicated
- Consider IM Adrenaline for life-threatening asthma
- Consider Magnesium Sulphate for severe to life threatening asthma resistant to first-line treatments in consultation with on-call doctor
- Apply cardiac monitor
- Gain vascular access
- Consider Hydrocortisone in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider IV fluids to treat hypotension

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
**Differential diagnosis to be considered:**
  - Cardiac Failure
  - Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease
  - Foreign body
  - Smoke inhalation

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Foreign Body Airway Obstruction (FBAO), also known as foreign body aspiration, is a medical emergency that occurs when a foreign object becomes lodged in the airway, blocking the flow of air. FBAO is a serious medical emergency that requires prompt treatment. With a complete foreign body airway obstruction, unconsciousness can occur within 3 to 6 minutes, with death likely within 5 to 10 minutes if the obstruction isn't cleared.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
Mild (Partial Obstruction) Severe (Complete) Obstruction
- Effective Cough
- Audible breathing/air movement
- Ability to speak or vocalise
- Wheeze, stridor
- Dysphagia, drooling
- Anxiety, agitation, gasping, clutching at throat
- Paradoxical chest / abdominal movement
- Ineffective or absent cough
- Inability to speak or vocalise
- Silent Chest
- Cyanosis
- Clutching at throat
- Rapid deterioration in consciousness
- Apnoea or cardiac arrest if unresolved
- Difficulty when moving air during IPPV
- History of event that can cause FBAO, e.g., eating, or playing with smaller objects.

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- The severity of the airway obstruction can be determined by the patient’s ability to cough
- Effective cough indicates mild obstruction
- Nil cough indicates a severe obstruction
- Ensure airway suctioning devices are always readily available
- Abdominal thrusts are not recommended due to potential life-threatening complications
- Do not delay intervention for suspected severe FBAO as hypoxia can develop rapidly
- Do not leave the patient unattended at any stage

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey

**Effective cough:**
  - Place patient in a position of comfort
  - Encourage coughing
  - Continuous reassurance
  - Closely monitor for deterioration

**Sever obstruction:**
  - Conscious
  - 5 x Back blows (check obstruction has be relieved between each blow), if un
  - 5 x chest thrusts
  - If the obstruction is still not relieved and the person remains responsive, continue alternating 5 x back blows with 5 x chest thrusts. Repeat if necessary
  - Ensure ongoing assessment
  - Consider use of LifeVac if readily available
  - Unconscious
  - Commence CPR
  - Prepare for patient deterioration and cardiac arrest
  - Clinicians should have a very low threshold for consultation with on-call doctor

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider laryngoscope for removal under direct visualisation
- Consider Transtracheal Airway with insufflation device
- Consider Vertical Surgical Cricothyroidotomy

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content: - Commonly, a foreign body may be lodged at the top of the oesophagus mimicking an airway obstruction

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Pulmonary embolism (PE) is a life-threatening condition caused by obstruction of the pulmonary arteries by thrombi, commonly originating from deep vein thrombosis (DVT). Early recognition and prompt management can significantly reduce mortality and morbidity.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Acute onset dyspnoea (shortness of breath), often severe and unexplained
- Pleuritic chest pain (sharp, worse on breathing in)
- Tachypnoea (rapid breathing)
- Tachycardia (fast heart rate)
- Syncope or near-syncope
- Haemoptysis (coughing blood; less common but significant)
- Hypoxia (altered oxygen saturation, cyanosis)
- Signs of deep vein thrombosis (DVT), i.e., unilateral leg swelling, pain, redness, localised warmth
- Sudden hypotension/shock: indicating massive PE
- Anxiety or sense of impending doom
- Low-grade fever (occasionally)
- Jugular venous distension
- ECG changes: Sinus tachycardia (most common), (incomplete) right bundle branch block, right ventricular strain pattern, right axis deviation, right atrial enlargement (P-Pulmonale), S1Q3T3, clockwise rotation (late transition zone), atrial fibrillation/flutter

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Previous history of DVT, PE or Cancer
- Recent long-haul flights or prolonged periods of immobilisation
- Currently pregnant or post-partum
- Recent trauma or surgery
- Oral contraceptive use
- Non-Steroidal Anti-Inflammatory Drugs (NSAIDs) are generally avoided in PE due to risk of bleeding
- Suspect PE: chest pain + dyspnoea + tachycardia, syncope, new hypoxia, haemoptysis, unilateral leg swelling suggestive of DVT
- Differentials may include myocardial infarction, pneumonia, pericarditis, pleurisy, pneumothorax, pericardial tamponade, or complete heart failure
- Risk of sudden deterioration: Unstable PE may rapidly progress to cardiac arrest Antibiotic resistance: Overuse of antibiotics should be avoided; most cases are viral
- A 12 lead ECG can show changes relate to PE, listed in additional information

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Prioritise airway, breathing, and circulation
- Oxygen, high flow aiming for ≥ 94% saturation
- Position patient to optimise breathing (usually sitting upright)
- Vital Signs
- Over the counter pain relief
- Limb LEAD ECG
- Secondary Survey

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Gain IV access
- Consider small bolus dose fluids (250mls)
- Consider stronger analgesia if required
- Consider antiemetic if required
- Consider enoxaparin
- 12 LEAD ECG

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- D-dimer Test
- Most important blood test for suspected pulmonary embolism
- A D-dimer is a breakdown product of fibrin (a blood clot protein)
- Elevated D-dimer suggests recent or ongoing clot formation and breakdown
- Use: In patients with low or intermediate risk (assessed via Wells score or similar), a negative D-dimer (< age-adjusted threshold) effectively rules out PE, and no further imaging is generally needed
- Limitation: A positive D-dimer is not specific to PE and may be seen in infection, inflammation, malignancy, trauma, surgery, pregnancy, or increasing age. A positive result prompts imaging for confirmation
- No evidence suggests aspirin has short-term therapeutic benefit in acute PE:
Aspirin is crucial in managing acute coronary syndromes but plays no proven role in the immediate stabilization or outcome improvement in PE patients

**Possible ECG changes due to PE:**
  - Sinus tachycardia is the most common ECG abnormality, present in approximately 38–44% of patients with acute PE
  - T wave inversion in lead V1 is seen in 38% of cases; T wave inversions in the right precordial leads (V1–V4) and/or inferior leads (II, III, aVF) occur in 34–38% and are highly specific for PE when found together
  - ST elevation in lead aVR appears in around 36% of patients
  - S1Q3T3 pattern (deep S in LEAD I, Q in LEAD III, inverted T in LEAD III) is seen in about 20% of patients but is neither highly sensitive nor specific for PE
  - Right bundle branch block (RBBB)-complete or incomplete-shows up in about 18% of cases
  - Right ventricular strain pattern (T wave inversions, right axis deviation) is found in 16– 34%
  - Right atrial enlargement (P pulmonale) is noted in 9%
  - Atrial tachyarrhythmias are reported in 8% of patients
  - Non-specific ST segment and T wave changes: Up to 50% of patients may show these findings
  - The Pulmonary Embolism Rule-Out Criteria (PERC) is an eight-point checklist used to identify low-risk patients in whom pulmonary embolism (PE) can safely be ruled out without further testing if all negative criteria are met
  - PERC Criteria
  - Age < 50 years
  - Heart rate < 100 beats per minute
  - Oxygen saturation ≥ 95% on room air
  - No haemoptysis
  - No exogenous estrogen use (such as oral contraceptives)
  - No prior deep vein thrombosis (DVT) or pulmonary embolism
  - No unilateral leg swelling
  - No surgery or trauma requiring hospitalization in the previous 4 weeks
  - PERC should only be applied to those with a low clinical probability of PE. If any criterion is positive, further diagnostic testing should be considered 20 COMMON PRESENCE COMMONPRESENNCE

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Chest infection is a general term used to describe an infection that affects the lower parts of the respiratory system, specifically the lungs and airways (bronchi). Many different bacteria, fungi, and viruses can cause infection, but the two main types of chest infections are bronchitis (typically viral) and pneumonia (typically bacterial).

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Fever, Rigors
- a persistent cough, can be productive with yellow/green phlegm or coughing up blood
- Wheezing, breathlessness and/or rapid and shallow breathing
- Chest pain or tightness when taking a breath
- Feeling confused and disorientated

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Consider off site transfer (home) if suitable
- Tachycardia (≥100 bpm), Tachypnoea (≥22 rpm), reduced on air saturation (≤ 94%), hypotension (≤ 90mmHg), acute onset confusion should raise concern for sepsis
- Severe illness signs: Dyspnoea, chest pain, altered mental status, hypoxia, signs of sepsis or organ dysfunction and need urgent review and management off site
- Patient history of Asthma (or other COPD) and/or smoking is important for management, i.e., antibiotic choice +/- steroids +/- Ventolin
- It is perfectly reasonable to advise patients to purchase their own medications (paracetamol, ibuprofen, cold and flu, nasal sprays, antihistamines, etc.) from the village store where appropriate

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Vital Signs
- Auscultation
- Consider antipyretic medications only if temperature causing discomfort
- Consider “over the counter” analgesia
- Secondary Survey if required COMMONPRESENNCE

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider ECG
- Consider IV access
- Consider blood draw for i-STAT test in severe cases (Blood Urea Nitrogen, BUN and lactate where applicable)
- Consider IV fluids where required
- Consider oral antibiotics (Amoxicillin, Augmentin Duo Forte) only in consultation with on- call doctor
- Consider IM/IV antibiotics in severe cases (Ceftriaxone) only in consultation with on-call doctor

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Cough suppressants SHOULD NOT be supplied/administered if production is evident
- i-STAT Chem8+ BUN reference range 3-140 mg/dL (normal range 7-20mg/dL)
- i-STAT Lactate of > 2 mmol/L should prompt review
- The CRB-65 and CURB-65 scores are both used to assess the severity of community- acquired pneumonia (CAP). 1 point for each:
  - Confusion (new disorientation in person, place, or time, etc.)
  - BUN testing (where available)
  - Raised respiratory rate (30 breaths per minute or more)
  - Low blood pressure (systolic less than 90 mmHg or diastolic 60 mmHg or less)
  - Age 65 years or more COMMONPRESENNCE

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Constipation is a condition where bowel movements become infrequent and difficult, often characterised by passing fewer than three stools a week, or having hard, dry, or difficult-to-pass stools, or a sensation of incomplete evacuation.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Bloating, abdominal discomfort or pain
- Infrequent bowel movements (less than the patients’ normal regime)
- Straining to defecate, hard and/or dry stools
- Light-headedness, dizziness, syncope
- Rectal bleeding, nausea and/or vomiting, faecal impaction, haemorrhoids, abdominal mass

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Some medications can cause constipation, for example opioids, anticholinergics, calcium channel blockers, iron supplements, antidepressants, etc.
- Ozempic, Wegovy (semaglutide), Mounjaro all can cause constipation
- Failure to respond (void) after treatment warrants offsite referral for further review and management
- Red flags, severe unremitting abdominal pain, recurrent vomiting unresponsive to antiemetics
- It is perfectly reasonable to advise patients to purchase their own medications (paracetamol, ibuprofen, cold and flu, nasal sprays, antihistamines, etc.) from the village store where appropriate

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Vital Signs
- Secondary Survey
- Abdominal Assessment
- Abdominal Auscultation
- Consider laxatives and/or stool softeners if available (Lactulose, Docusate Sodium {Coloxyl, Coloxyl with senna}, etc.) COMMONPRESENNCE

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content: - Consider antiemetic (metoclopramide with consultation)

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content: - Diet and lifestyle factors can be discussed to reduce further incidences, consider consultation with Health and Injury Prevention Adviser COMMONPRESENNCE

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Dental pain (also known as odontogenic pain) refers to discomfort or pain originating from the teeth or their supporting structures. It is one of the most common reasons patients seek dental care and is a very common presentation to mine site clinics. Causes can include tooth decay, wisdom teeth eruption, dental abscess and gum disease.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Pain ranges from mild ache to severe, throbbing pain; may be sharp, constant, or intermittent.
- Sensitivity pain on exposure to hot/cold/sweet stimuli.
- Swelling of the gum or face near the affected tooth.
- Redness around the tooth or gums.
- Difficulty chewing and aggravation of pain with biting or pressure.
- Fever can sometimes be present with infection.
- Pus or bad taste indicates possible abscess or infection.
- Enlarged lymph nodes may be palpable in the jaw or neck region.

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Mine site clinics cannot provide effective dental care and gold standard care from a dentist (offsite) should be sought as first line. Medics can treat the symptoms in the first instance
- Any suspected dental abscess should prompt offsite treatment
- Consider utilising the Dentist in a Box Dental Emergency Kit if applicable/available for caries
- Advise patients who smoke to try and avoid it whilst afflicted
- Gargling salt water may provide some relief from mild toothache (don’t swallow)
- Codeine: there is no good evidence that codeine (even in doses of 60 mg) is any more effective than paracetamol or NSAIDs, or improves analgesia when combined with paracetamol or an NSAID
- It is perfectly reasonable to advise patients to purchase their own medications (paracetamol, ibuprofen, cold and flu, nasal sprays, antihistamines, etc.) from the village store where appropriate

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Vital Signs COMMONPRESENNCE

- Secondary Survey (intra oral exam)
- Consider over the counter analgesia if required (Paracetamol, Ibuprofen, benzocaine [Oral Eze])
- Consider cold compress on the side of the face if swollen

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider stronger analgesia (Oxycodone, Tapentadol) in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider antimicrobials (Metronidazole) or antibiotics (Amoxycillin, Augmentin Duo Forte), if infective, only in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider ECG if pain is atypical COMMONPRESENNCE

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Sometimes lying flat can increase dental pain, if this is the case, advise to rest propped up on a pillow
- Rarely, dental pain can be referred pain from other conditions such as sinusitis, temporomandibular disorders or cardiac pain, so reevaluate if symptoms are atypical

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Eye presentations in the remote and prehospital setting range from minor irritations to conditions requiring urgent medical review. Common non-emergent presentations include conjunctivitis (bacterial or viral), corneal foreign bodies, superficial corneal abrasions, subconjunctival haemorrhage, styes, blepharitis, and chemical or environmental irritants. Thorough assessment is essential to differentiate between minor, self-limiting conditions and those requiring escalation to the on-call doctor or urgent off-site transfer.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**Bacterial Conjunctivitis**
- Unilateral or bilateral red/pink eye
- Purulent (yellow/green) discharge, often crusting on lids
- Gritty or burning sensation; eyelids may be stuck together on waking
- Vision generally unaffected

**Viral Conjunctivitis**
- Typically bilateral (may start unilateral)
- Watery or serous discharge
- Associated with URTI, sore throat, lymphadenopathy
- Highly contagious

**Corneal Foreign Body / Abrasion**
- Sudden onset pain, photophobia, foreign body sensation
- Excessive tearing (epiphora)
- Blepharospasm (difficulty keeping eye open)
- History of trauma, grinding, or exposure to wind/dust
- May have visible foreign body on cornea or under eyelid

**Subconjunctival Haemorrhage**
- Painless bright red patch on the white of the eye
- No discharge, no change in vision
- Often noticed incidentally; may follow coughing, sneezing, or Valsalva

**Stye (Hordeolum)**
- Localised painful swelling on the eyelid margin
- May have central yellow/white point (abscess)
- Tenderness to palpation

**Blepharitis**
- Chronic bilateral lid margin inflammation
- Crusting, scaling, or greasy deposits at lash base
- Burning, itching, mild redness; worse in the morning

**Chemical / Environmental Irritation**
- History of exposure (dust, smoke, chemicals, UV light)
- Burning, stinging, redness, tearing

sections.title: Considerations & Red Flags
sections.content:
**Contact the on-call doctor for any of the following:**
- Significant reduction in visual acuity
- Severe eye pain not relieved with irrigation or topical anaesthetic
- Penetrating or perforating eye injury
- Chemical splash (strong acid or alkali) - irrigate first, then contact doctor
- Hyphaema (blood in anterior chamber)
- Corneal ulceration or suspected herpetic keratitis (dendrites on fluorescein)
- Pupil abnormalities (irregular, non-reactive, or unequal pupils)
- Proptosis or periorbital swelling suggestive of orbital cellulitis
- Recent intraocular surgery
- Photophobia with headache and neck stiffness (consider meningitis)

**Assessment Considerations:**
- Always check and document visual acuity (using Snellen chart if available, or a near-vision card)
- Examine both eyes systematically: lids, lashes, conjunctiva, cornea, anterior chamber, pupils
- Use a torch and, where available, fluorescein staining to identify corneal epithelial defects
- Evert the upper eyelid to check for sub-tarsal foreign bodies
- Ask about contact lens use - lens wearers with a red eye warrant heightened concern and on-call doctor contact
- Ascertain any history of trauma, chemical exposure, or recent URTI

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
**All Eye Presentations**
- Primary Survey
- Vital Signs (including temperature if infection suspected)
- Document visual acuity in both eyes
- Inspect eye(s): lids, lashes, conjunctiva, cornea, pupils - compare both eyes
- If contact lenses worn, remove prior to any examination or treatment

**Foreign Body / Superficial Abrasion**
- Irrigate the eye thoroughly with sterile normal saline or sterile water (minimum 250mL)
- Evert the upper eyelid and inspect for sub-tarsal foreign body
- If a visible, loose, superficial foreign body is present on the conjunctiva, attempt removal with a moistened cotton bud or gentle irrigation - do NOT attempt to remove embedded corneal foreign bodies
- Consider fluorescein staining to identify corneal abrasions or residual foreign body
- Advise the patient to avoid rubbing the eye

**Bacterial Conjunctivitis**
- Advise gentle cleansing of lid margins with clean water or saline-soaked gauze
- Consider chloramphenicol 0.5% eye drops - in consultation with on-call doctor
- Advise strict hand hygiene and avoidance of sharing towels or pillowcases

**Viral Conjunctivitis**
- Supportive care only; antibiotics are not indicated
- Cool compresses for comfort
- Advise strict hand hygiene; highly contagious

**Subconjunctival Haemorrhage**
- Reassure the patient - this is typically benign and self-limiting
- Resolves over 1–2 weeks without treatment
- Advise to return if vision changes, pain develops, or haemorrhage recurs frequently

**Stye (Hordeolum)**
- Advise warm compress 3–4 times per day for 10–15 minutes
- Advise against squeezing or attempting to drain
- Consider chloramphenicol eye ointment (topical) if spreading cellulitis - in consultation with on-call doctor

**Blepharitis**
- Advise warm compress and gentle lid margin hygiene (cotton bud with dilute baby shampoo)
- Long-term lid hygiene is the mainstay of management

**Chemical / UV Irritation**
- Immediate copious irrigation with sterile normal saline (minimum 1 litre for chemical exposure)
- Continue irrigation until pH of conjunctival sac is 7.0–7.4 (test with pH paper if available)
- Contact on-call doctor for all significant chemical splash injuries

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider topical anaesthetic (proxymetacaine / tetracaine) to facilitate examination - in consultation with on-call doctor; NOT to be used for ongoing pain relief (delays healing, masks worsening)
- Fluorescein staining to identify and document corneal epithelial defects
- Consider cycloplegic agent (e.g. cyclopentolate) for ciliary spasm pain - on-call doctor order required
- Consider oral analgesia (ibuprofen, paracetamol) for pain management
- Consider oral antibiotics for periorbital or orbital cellulitis - on-call doctor order required
- IV access and IV antibiotics may be required for severe orbital or periorbital cellulitis - on-call doctor order required
- Arrange off-site transfer where indicated (see Red Flags above)

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- **Chloramphenicol eye drops** are a broad-spectrum topical antibiotic for bacterial conjunctivitis. Supplied as 0.5% drops; instil 1 drop into the affected eye(s) every 2 hours while awake for the first 48 hours, reducing to 4 times daily thereafter for up to 5 days total. Requires on-call doctor authorisation.
- **Sodium fluorescein** is a diagnostic dye used topically to detect corneal epithelial defects. Apply 1 drop of 2% fluorescein solution or use a fluorescein-impregnated strip moistened with saline; view under cobalt blue light. Normal corneal epithelium does not take up the dye; defects (abrasions, ulcers, foreign body tracks, herpetic dendrites) fluoresce bright green.
- Do NOT patch corneal abrasions - current evidence does not support patching and it may increase infection risk.
- Contact lens wearers should not return to wearing lenses until fully healed and after review by a doctor or optometrist.
- Fluorescein will temporarily stain soft contact lenses; remove them before use.
- Document findings clearly: which eye(s), degree of visual acuity, nature of discharge, fluorescein findings, and any treatment administered.
- Encourage return to clinic if symptoms worsen or fail to improve within 48 hours.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Gastroenteritis (gastro) is an inflammation of the stomach and intestines that causes acute onset of diarrhoea, often with vomiting, abdominal pain, and sometimes fever. It is typically self-limiting. The most common causes are the virus’s (70%) norovirus and rotavirus, however, can be caused by bacteria (20%) or protozoa (10%).

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
Viral Bacterial Protozoal
- Prominent upper gastrointestinal symptoms
- Diarrhoea, vomiting and nausea
- Symptom onset is typically acute and resolves within 24 to 48 hours
- Fever
- Tenesmus
- Severe abdominal cramps
- Mucus and or bloody stool
- Symptom onset typically acute with typical duration of 4 to 7 days
- Abdominal distension
- Flatulence
- Nausea and anorexia
- Symptom onset is typically gradual (over several weeks) but can be acute
- Sudden onset of abdominal cramping, watery diarrhoea, nausea and/or vomiting
- Fever, rigors, headache, signs and symptoms of dehydration (tachycardia, hypotension, reduced skin turgor, sunken eyes)
- Malaise, muscle aches, weakness, loss of appetite
- Red flag symptoms: Bloody stools (dysentery) high fever (>38.5°C), signs of severe dehydration

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Gastroenteritis is a highly infectious and transmissible condition
- If tachycardia does not resolve with intravenous fluids, surgical or life-threatening causes should be considered
- Abdominal examination must be performed in all patients to ensure cause is not a surgical abdomen (colitis/diverticulitis/appendicitis)
- Inferior STEMIs can present with GI symptoms (consider ECG if cardiac risk factors are present)
- Anti-emetic and anti-diarrhoeal medication should be avoided in the first 24-48 hours unless necessary to retain fluids
- Consider any recent international travel
- Some causes and scenarios of gastro are a notifiable disease; mine site outbreaks are reportable to WorkSafe WA COMMONPRESENNCE

- Advise patients around proper hygiene
- Due to nature of a mine site, i.e., close quarters, shared dining, remote setting etc., the site should be considered “high risk” in relation to gastro. Patients treated as or suspected of gastro should not return to work until 48 hours after symptoms stop
- It is perfectly reasonable to advise patients to purchase their own medications (paracetamol, ibuprofen, cold and flu, nasal sprays, antihistamines, etc.) from the village store where practicable

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Vital Signs (including BGL)
- Secondary Survey if required
- Abdominal Assessment
- Abdominal Auscultation
- Advise rest
- Consider “over the counter” analgesia (Paracetamol, ibuprofen)
- Consider oral fluids with replacement electrolytes

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider ECG
- Consider IV access
- Consider IV fluids
- Consider Anti-emetic: Metoclopramide, Ondansetron, Prochlorperazine
- Consider Anti-diarrhoeal: Loperamide
- Consider antibiotics (Ciprofloxacin, Azithromycin), if possible bacterial cause, only in consultation with on-call doctor

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Bare below the elbows when treating patients with communicable illnesses
- Patient education on hygiene and room cleaning, changing bedding, etc.
- Bacterial gastroenteritis can cause some serious complications. Complications specific to the cause of acute gastroenteritis such as Haemolytic Uraemic Syndrome (HUS) (E coli, Salmonella) and Guillain-Barré (Campylobacter) COMMONPRESENNCE

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Influenza-like illness (ILI) describes a set of symptoms that resemble those of influenza (the flu) but can be caused by a variety of respiratory viruses and, less commonly, bacteria. ILI is typically characterised by the sudden onset of fever, cough, and often other symptoms such as sore throat, muscle aches, fatigue, and headache, usually lasting for several days.
Common viral causes include influenza viruses, respiratory syncytial virus (RSV), rhinovirus, adenovirus, parainfluenza viruses, and human coronaviruses.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content: Symptoms Cold Influenza COVID19 Symptoms begin gradually acutely within 14 days ofexposure Symptoms last 4-10 days 5-7 days Varies by person Body aches ✓ ✓ sometimes Chills less common ✓ sometimes Dry cough ✓ ✓ ✓ Exposure to germs ✓ ✓ ✓ Fatigue/weakness ✓ ✓ ✓ Fever less common ✓ ✓ Headaches less common ✓ sometimes Itchy eyes - - sometimes Nasal congestion ✓ ✓ sometimes Nausea/vomiting/diarrhoea sometimes sometimes sometimes New onset of loss of smell and/or taste sometimes sometimes ✓ Rigors (shivering) sometimes sometimes sometimes Runny nose ✓ ✓ sometimes Sneezing ✓ ✓ sometimes Sore throat ✓ ✓ sometimes Shortness of breath less common ✓ ✓ Symptom progression/get worse - ✓ ✓ COMMONPRESENNCE

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- It is perfectly reasonable to advise patients to purchase their own medications (paracetamol, ibuprofen, cold and flu, nasal sprays, antihistamines, etc.) from the village store where practicable
- Do not rule out influenza in patients with a current history of seasonal influenza vaccination
- Several cardiac conditions can present with symptoms that overlap with those of influenza. For example, myocarditis often mimics the flu, producing fever, fatigue, and muscle aches. Other heart conditions, such as heart failure and myocardial infarction (heart attack), can also cause symptoms like shortness of breath, chest pain, and fatigue, which may be mistaken for influenza
- Most influenza infections are uncomplicated and self-limiting, typically lasting less than 7 days. However, complicated illness (e.g., sepsis, pneumonia, acute respiratory distress syndrome [ARDS]) or atypical presentations (e.g., neurological manifestations, myositis) without significant fever or respiratory features can occur
- Hydration status is important information, used to guide further management.
Tachycardia, increased resp rate, decreased urine output - important to determine if IV hydration is required, also the urgency to evacuate (logistics)
- Influenza is a reportable illness
- Antibiotics have no benefit in treating viral illnesses
- Severe Symptoms Requiring Urgent Assessment:
  - Hypoxia (SpO₂ <94%)
  - Tachypnoea (>22 breaths/min)
  - Haemodynamic instability (e.g., hypotension)
  - Altered mental status
  - Chest pain or persistent vomiting
  - Cyanosis or respiratory distress COMMONPRESENNCE

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Appropriate PPE
- Primary Survey
- Vital Signs
- Secondary Survey if required
- Auscultation
- Hydration testing
- Consider RAT, Influenza A/B testing where available
- Provide ILI information sheet where appropriate

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider ECG
- Consider IV access
- Consider IV fluids where required
- Consider antiemetic where required
- Consider analgesia where required
- Consider Ritonavir (COVID) only in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider Oseltamivir (Tamiflu)(Influenza) only in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider antibiotics where bacterial infection may be the cause, only in consultation with on-call doctor

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
**Patient education:**
  - Wear a mask where required or appropriate
  - Take away meals preferred where available
  - Viral infections of an ILI nature tend to last for 7-10 days
  - Viral coughs can last longer than the original virus, sometimes up to 16 weeks
  - Studies have shown that individuals are significantly more likely to experience a heart attack within a week of a confirmed flu infection, particularly in older adults and those experiencing their first heart attack so having a baseline ECG to compare to could prove beneficial COMMONPRESENNCE

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Otitis externa, also known as “swimmer’s ear”, is an inflammation or infection of the external ear canal, the pathway from the outer ear to the eardrum. It is often caused by bacterial or fungal infections and can be triggered by prolonged moisture in the ear canal, such as after swimming.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Earache
- Discharge
- Itching
- Hearing difficulties (due to canal obstruction)
- Feeling of fullness/blocked sensation in the ear
- Swelling/redness
- In acute diffuse otitis externa, the external ear canal (if visible) appears inflamed, with erythema and oedema
- In otomycosis, fungal debris is typically visualised in the canal on otoscopy (having the appearance of wet newspaper)

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Persistent symptoms despite adequate topical treatment may indicate complications such as cellulitis
- Red flags can include severe symptoms > 48 hours despite treatment, cranial nerve involvement, facial paralysis, granulation, immunosuppression, diabetes
- Consider fungal (Otomycosis) or resistant bacteria in non-resolving symptoms. Fungal infections are more likely to present with itch and a blocked ear (from fungal debris) than from pain
- Avoid ear drops containing an aminoglycoside (e.g., Framycetin, Neomycin) in a tympanic membrane that is known or suspected to be perforated. Instead consider oral therapies
- Follow up 48-72 hours
- Imaging must be consented to
- It is perfectly reasonable to advise patients to purchase their own medications (paracetamol, ibuprofen, cold and flu, nasal sprays, antihistamines, etc.) from the village store where practicable

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Assess vital signs COMMONPRESENNCE

- Assess around outer ear for erythema, swelling, discharge, oedema and signs of periauricular cellulitis
- Otalgia (ear pain) can be elicited by tugging the pinna to retract it

**Perform bilateral otoscopy, looking for:**
  - Tenderness and pain on advancing speculum (care to be taken)
  - Swelling, oedema, moisture (may not be able to visualise External Auditory Canal)
  - Debris and discharge (fungal causes can be distinguished)
  - Obstruction
  - If possible, and with consent, perform imaging for comparison or review by current poison permit holder
  - Consider “over the counter” analgesia (Paracetamol, Ibuprofen)
  - Consider over the counter topical treatment, e.g., Aqua Ear

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider analgesia
- Consider topical antibiotics (Ciprofloxacin [Ciproxin], Kenacomb Otic) only in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider ear wick if available, but must be removed after 72 hours
- Consider Oral antibiotics (Augmentin Duo Forte) in severe cases or no improvement after 48 hours of topical treatment only in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider removal of discharge or other debris by dry aural toilet (cotton wool on a probe) if trained to do so. The aim of aural toilet is to facilitate ear drop administration.
Do not syringe the ear with water or other fluids

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Patient education to finish complete course of antibiotics, avoid scratching, red flags, avoid inserting anything into ear (cue tip, ear plugs for example), keep ears dry and clean
- If images are taken, ensure they are added to the patient’s medical record for comparison on review
- Fungal infection (otomycosis) occurs less frequently than bacterial infection
- Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Staphylococcus aureus are the most common causes of acute diffuse otitis externa; other bacterial causes are much less common
- Otocomb/Otodex/Sofradex are not effective against Pseudomonas infection. Hence a better choice for ear drops is Ciprofloxacin COMMONPRESENNCE

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Otitis media, also known as a middle ear infection, is an inflammation or infection of the middle ear, the space behind the tympanic membrane (eardrum), often follows a cold or upper respiratory infection and caused by fluid buildup. There are two main types:
acute otitis media (a sudden, painful infection) and otitis media with effusion (fluid in the middle ear without signs of infection, sometimes called "glue ear").

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Earache, headache
- Fever
- Fluid draining, feeling of fullness in the ear, hearing difficulties
- Nausea/Vomiting, Vertigo

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Ear effusion, also known as glue ear or otitis media with effusion (OME), is a condition where fluid builds up in the middle ear, often after an ear infection, and can cause temporary hearing loss or other symptoms. Watch and wait is a good approach
- It often starts with a cold and will usually go away by itself without antibiotics
- Signs of runny nose, sore throat can suggest patient may have a cold resulting in OME
- Complications include tympanic membrane perforation, mastoiditis, cholesteatoma, hearing loss and meningitis
- Risk factors: recent URTI, smoking, eustachian tube dysfunction, allergies
- A watch and wait approach is suitable in mild cases, must be reviewed within 48-72 hours
- Red flags can include severe pain despite treatment, dizziness and vertigo, facial droop/ weakness, mastoiditis (pain over the mastoid bone), underlying immunosuppression or diabetes, sudden hearing loss or purulent ear discharge
- It is perfectly reasonable to advise patients to purchase their own medications (paracetamol, ibuprofen, cold and flu, nasal sprays, antihistamines, etc.) from the village store where practicable COMMONPRESENNCE

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Assess around outer ear, including mastoid
- Redness
- Size, shape, colour
- Feel mastoid for tenderness, redness, swelling or affect ear position
- Assess sinuses for tenderness

**Perform bilateral otoscopy, looking for:**
  - Redness, bulging membranes, loss of landmarks
  - Fluid behind the tympanic membrane
  - If possible, and with consent, perform imaging for comparison or review by on-call doctor
  - Consider “over the counter” analgesia (paracetamol, Ibuprofen)
  - Consider topical analgesia (Auralgan)

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider analgesia
- Consider antiemetic
- Consider IM Stemetil if indicated in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider oral antibiotics (Amoxycillin, Augmentin Duo Forte, Doxycycline) only in consultation with on-call doctor only

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Patient education to finish complete course of antibiotics, red flags, avoid inserting anything into ear (cue tip, ear plugs for example)
- If images are taken, ensure they are added to the patient’s medical record for comparison on review

**Patient education:**
  - Otorrhoea is suggestive of tympanic membrane perforation and middle ear effusion, provided that acute diffuse otitis externa has been excluded. In such cases, the patient may use a rolled tissue spear or similar device to gently dry mop the ear canal every six hours until the external ear canal is dry
  - Advise patient not to use any ear drops, in particular, Sofradex, Otodex, and Otocomb
  - Aminoglycoside-containing ear drops should be avoided because of safety concerns, in particular the risk of auditory and vestibular toxicity COMMONPRESENNCE

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Gastroesophageal reflux (GER) involves the involuntary retrograde flow of gastric contents into the oesophagus, which may cause symptoms or complications. Common causes can include, delayed gastric emptying, obesity, pregnancy, medications or hiatal hernia.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Heartburn, regurgitation, vomiting
- Belching, acid taste in mouth, tooth decay
- Chest pain
- Cough (that can increase at night), wheezing, hoarseness, increasing/worsening Asthma symptoms
- Feeling a lump in the or and/or sore throat

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Chest pain as the primary symptom, difficulty and/or painful swallowing, gastrointestinal bleeding anaemia and/or persistent vomiting should be considered as red flags and warrant further offsite investigation
- Consider cause from cardiac origin
- It is perfectly reasonable to advise patients to purchase their own medications (paracetamol, ibuprofen, cold and flu, nasal sprays, antihistamines, etc.) from the village store where practicable

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Vital Signs
- Secondary Survey: auscultation, intra oral exam where required
- Consider antacids: Mylanta, Gaviscon

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- ECG
- Consider “Pink Lady” *
- Consider Proton Pump Inhibitors (PPI) (Esomeprazole, Pantoprazole) in consultation with on-call doctor COMMONPRESENNCE

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- When symptoms or mucosal injury are more severe it’s termed gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD)
- Some patient medication can worsen symptoms of GER, discuss with on-call doctor if concerned
- Reflux symptoms are commonly induced by high-fat meals, alcohol, coffee, chocolate, citrus fruit, tomato products, spicy foods and carbonated beverages
- Patient education on possible lifestyle modifications, i.e., weight loss, avoiding dietary triggers, avoiding meals 2-3 hours before bedtime, smoking cessation, avoiding tight clothing
- Pink Lady can be made from 1:1 up to 1:3 but if unsure, speak to the on-call doctor
- 1:1 Viscous Xylocaine 15mls : Gastrogel/Mylanta 15mls
- 1:3 Viscous Xylocaine 10mls : Gastrogel/Mylanta 30mls COMMONPRESENNCE

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Sinus infections (acute rhinosinusitis) are common, generally self-limiting upper respiratory conditions but may become significant in remote environments where access to further healthcare is delayed.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Facial pain or pressure (particularly over the sinuses-forehead, cheeks, behind eyes)
- Nasal congestion or obstruction
- Purulent (green/yellow) nasal discharge
- Fever (low-grade, sometimes absent)
- Reduced or lost sense of smell
- Maxillary tooth pain
- Postnasal drip and/or sore throat
- Headache, especially worse when leaning forward
- Fatigue, malaise

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Risk of progression to severe infection: Rare complications include orbital cellulitis, intracranial infection (meningitis, abscess)
- Antibiotic resistance: Overuse of antibiotics should be avoided; most cases are viral
- Environmental hazards: Dust, irritants, or extreme dryness contribute to chronic or recurrent symptoms
- Red flag features (suggesting complication or alternate diagnoses-immediate escalation required):
  - Severe headache unresponsive to analgesia
  - Periorbital swelling or erythema
  - Visual changes (blurred, double vision, loss of vision)
  - Altered mental status or persistent vomiting
  - High fever and neck stiffness
  - Focal neurological deficits
  - It is perfectly reasonable to advise patients to purchase their own medications (paracetamol, ibuprofen, cold and flu, nasal sprays, antihistamines, etc.) from the village store where appropriate COMMONPRESENNCE

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Vital Signs
- Secondary Survey
- Assess for red flag features
- Consider warm compress to sinuses
- Consider over the counter analgesia (Paracetamol, Ibuprofen)
- Consider nasal sprays where available (for example, oxymetazoline 0.05% nasal spray)
- Consider antihistamines if allergy related
- Consultation with on-call doctor for advice and review if further management is required

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider stronger analgesia if required
- Consider Antibiotics only in consultation with on-call doctor COMMONPRESENNCE

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Sore throat refers to pain, discomfort, or scratchiness in the throat, often worse on swallowing. It can involve the pharynx, tonsils, or larynx and is common in both adults and children. Sore throat has several causes, not limited to rhinovirus, coronavirus, adenovirus, influenza, herpes simplex, strep throat, candida, allergies, irritants, reflux and vocal strain.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Sore or scratchy throat, pain with swallowing (odynophagia)
- Red, inflamed pharynx and/or tonsils
- Hoarseness if larynx involved, bad breath
- Swollen lymph nodes around area
- Fever, rigors, headache, malaise, fatigue, tonsillar exudate

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Otoscopy and sore throat are related because a sore throat can sometimes be a symptom of an ear infection, particularly otitis media (middle ear infection)
- Distinguishing between viral and streptococcal pharyngitis and tonsillitis based on clinical features can be challenging
- Red flags: Difficulty in breathing or swallowing, drooling, trismus, stridor, neck swelling and tightness, signs of dehydration
- Suspected peritonsillar abscess (“quinsy”) should prompt urgent offsite ENT referral
- It is perfectly reasonable to advise patients to purchase their own medications (paracetamol, ibuprofen, cold and flu, nasal sprays, antihistamines, etc.) from the village store where practicable

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Vital Signs
- Secondary Survey
- Intra oral assessment
- Otoscopy
- If any abnormalities detected, gain imaging, with consent, for the doctor to review
- Advise rest and fluids COMMONPRESENNCE

- Consider throat lozenges
- Consider warm saltwater gargle
- Consider Cepacaine/Betadine throat gargle
- Consider “over the counter” analgesia

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider aspirin and water gargle
- Consider stronger analgesia (if in scope) otherwise in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider antibiotics (Phenoxymethylpenicillin, Amoxycillin, Cephalexin) only in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider Prednisolone in consultation with the on-call doctor (patients with severe symptoms, sever pain, dysphagia, drooling)

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Severe cases should be referred offsite at next available opportunity
- Complications of untreated Group A Strep: Rheumatic fever (rare in developed nations), post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis, peritonsillar/parapharyngeal abscess
- Patient education: Most sore throats resolve in 5–7 days without antibiotics; antibiotics do not shorten duration for viral infections
- If images are taken, ensure they are added to the patient’s medical record for comparison on review
- Viral pharyngitis and tonsillitis are strongly suggested by the presence of any one of the following clinical features: cough, hoarse voice, conjunctivitis, nasal congestion, anterior stomatitis, viral exanthema and diarrhoea
- Streptococcal pharyngitis and tonsillitis are difficult to diagnose based on clinical features alone. The clinical features traditionally associated with streptococcal infection are abrupt onset of symptoms, fever (above 38Â°C), tender cervical lymphadenopathy, tonsillar exudate, and the absence of cough, rhinorrhoea or nasal congestion COMMONPRESENNCE

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: A urinary tract infection (UTI) is an infection of the urinary system, most commonly caused by bacteria (such as Escherichia coli). It can occur in any part of the urinary tract, including the bladder (cystitis), urethra (urethritis), or kidneys (pyelonephritis).

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Fever, chills, rigors

**Lower UTI:**
  - Dysuria (painful or burning urination)
  - Increased frequency and urgency of urination
  - Suprapubic discomfort or pain
  - Cloudy or malodorous urine
  - Occasionally haematuria (blood in urine)
  - Some patients may present without any lower UTI symptoms

**Upper UTI:**

**Above symptoms PLUS:**
  - Fever and chills
  - Flank or costovertebral angle pain
  - Nausea and vomiting

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- More common in women than in men
- Risk factors can include sexual activity, use of spermicides, pregnancy, postmenopausal status, diabetes, urinary catheters, urinary tract abnormalities
- Always consider sexually transmitted infections
- Consider referral of pregnant women who are symptomatic or bacteriuric, or patients not responding to first line treatment for further review and management
- Urinary alkalinising agents (Ural) significantly reduce the antimicrobial effect of Nitrofurantoin
- It is perfectly reasonable to advise patients to purchase their own medications (paracetamol, ibuprofen, cold and flu, nasal sprays, antihistamines, etc.) from the village store where practicable COMMONPRESENNCE

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Vital Signs
- Urinalysis: Look for leukocyte, nitrites, protein, blood
- Consider antipyretic medication only if temperature is causing discomfort
- Consider “over the counter” analgesia if required (Paracetamol, Ibuprofen)
- Consider Ural
- Advise ample oral hydration
- Secondary Survey if required

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Considered appropriate antiemetic if required
- Consider antibiotics only in consultation with on-call doctor
- Nitrofurantoin (now first line as of May 2025), Cefalexin, Trimethoprim
- If septic, treat as per that guideline

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Symptoms should improve within 48–72 hours; lack of improvement warrants reassessment
- Cystitis in males is uncommon; its prevalence increases with age and is often related to abnormalities of the urinary tract, prostatic disease
- The efficacy of urinary alkalinising agents for the symptomatic treatment of acute cystitis has not been established in trials, but some patients find them useful for symptom relief

**Patient education:**
  - Educate where necessary around appropriate hygiene practices (wiping front to back, urinating after intercourse, avoiding irritating feminine products, etc.)
  - Advise all male patients to follow up with their GP for further testing (ultrasound/STI/Urine Culture and Sensitivity test)
  - Advise patient to complete the full course of antibiotics even if symptoms resolve early 21 ENVIRONMENTAL

title: Hyperthermia / Heat Stroke / Heat Related Sickness
synonyms: Hyperthermia / Heat Stroke / Heat Related Sickness

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
RELATED SICKNESS

Heat stroke is an excessive elevation of core body temperature above the normal range due to failure of thermoregulation. Heat stroke should be suspected if symptoms do not dissipate when the affected person lies down and rests when removed from contributing factors.
Heat stroke can be induced by environmental factors, such as excessive heat absorption, hot and humid climates, excessive physical exertion and/or additional underlying factors, such as metabolic reactions, drugs, infection.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Heat Exhaustion (core temperature 37°C to less than 40°C)
- Severe headache and/or dizziness (especially postural)
- Diaphoresis, nausea and vomiting
- Tachypnoea, tachycardia, hypotension (especially postural)
- Muscle pain, fatigue, cramps
- Heat Stroke (core temp ≥ 40°C)
- CNS dysfunction with bizarre behaviour, seizures and altered level of consciousness
- Extreme fatigue, headache and/or syncope
- Facial flushing, vomiting and diarrhoea
- Skin hot, possibly dry or nil diaphoresis
- Dysrhythmia and hypotension
- Tachypnoea and acute respiratory distress syndrome
- Hypoglycaemia and hyperkalaemia

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Consider using any facilities that are on scene to help rapidly cool the patient before transporting to hospital
- In the initial stages of heat stroke, CNS dysfunction is observed. These are the first signs of thermoregulatory failure
- Improvements in the patients’ conscious state is the most promising sign that active cooling is working but may happen slowly
- The young and elderly are more prone to heat induced illness
- Tympanic temperature readings may not provide a true representation of a patient’s core body temperature in heat extremes
- Assess patients left in cars for heat induced illness
- Cardiac dysfunction and tachyarrhythmias generally resolve with cooling
- Hyperthermia is a medical emergency with the degree and duration being a predictor of the outcome
- Patients can become hypothermic when cooled too rapidly. If the patient begins shivering, cool to touch or peripherally shut down, cooling should discontinue
- Avoid the patient shivering as this will increase core body temperature
- Where appropriate, unlimited salted foods with cool water
- Do not use antipyretic medication for temperature control

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- When safe to do so, move the patient into a cool environment or shade
- Position patient supine if appropriate
- Vital Sign Survey (closely monitor patients’ temperature and BGL)
- Consider Oxygen if indicated
- Provide water to drink if fully conscious
- Urinalysis (myoglobinuria)

**Apply critical treatment points:**
  - Strip clothing off patient
  - Soak or spray the patient with tepid water
  - Fan the patient to promote heat loss
  - Apply ice packs to neck, groin and armpits
  - Cover the patient with wet cold cloths and continue to fan.
  - Consider delaying transport to ensure the patient is adequately cooled (approximately 15 minutes)
  - Consider antiemetics
  - Continue cooling the patient during transport

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- 12 lead ECG
- If unconscious, consider advanced airway management
- Establish vascular access
- Consider fluid therapy (administering cool IV fluids if possible)

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Seizures are common in patients suffering from hyperthermia
- Mortality > 60%, permanent neurological deficit – 7-15% Condition Cause Heat Syncope Fainting due to peripheral vasodilation secondary to high ambient temperature Heat Cramp Muscle cramping occurring during exercise in heat, which is related to salt deficiency and is usually benign Heat Exhaustion Occurs when an individual develops salt and/or water depletion, dehydration and hypovolaemia from heat stress. Nausea, vomiting, fatigue and excessive sweating are typical. Core temperature may or may not be raised and tissue damage does not occur Heat Stroke Hyperthermia with neurological dysfunction due to the failing thermoregulatory system

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Hypothermia occurs when the body loses heat faster than it can produce it causing the body temperature to drop below 35°C.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
Mild hypothermia (32-35°C): Moderate hypothermia (30-32°C):
Severe hypothermia (<30°C):
- Decreased respiratory rate
- Lethargy
- Weakness
- Slurred speech
- Ataxia
- Shivering may cease
- Muscle rigidity
- Poor reflexes
- Dilated pupils
- Hypotension
- Bradycardia
- Flaccid muscles
- Fixed pupils
- Arrhythmias
- Cardiac arrest

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Avoid sudden movements (may trigger ventricular arrhythmia in moderate to severe hypothermia)
- Hypothermia generally develops more rapidly in immersion and in patients where thermo-regulation is impaired (i.e., spinal cord injuries, TBI’s, diabetics, etc.) Osborne (J) waves may be present. An Osborne wave is a slow, positive deflection at the end of the QRS complex, most prominent in lead II and V3-V6. These can resolve as the patient is rewarmed
- The onset and duration of medications is prolonged during hypothermia. In cardiac arrest if the patient has a temperature <30°C, the interval between doses of adrenaline or amiodarone is doubled
- Atrial arrhythmias, bradycardias or A-V blocks will generally resolve on rewarming.
Antiarrhythmic medications or trans-thoracic pacing are usually not required unless decompensation has occurred
- Potential major trauma patients should receive thermal management under this guideline, irrespective of their temperature
- Shivering peaks at around 34°C and is minimal/stops around 31-32°C
- Rubbing the skin causes vasodilation and my increase heat loss

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- When safe to do so, remove the patient from the cold environment
- Position patient recumbent if appropriate or a position of comfort
- Vital Sign Survey (closely monitor patients’ temperature and BGL)
- Consider Oxygen if indicated
- Provide warm oral fluids to drink if appropriate
- Remove wet/cold clothing
- Passively re-warm with blankets and warm surroundings
- The target temperature for the patient compartment of the ambulance for patients suffering or at risk of hypothermia is 24°C or higher.
- Secondary Survey

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Gain IV access
- 12 LEAD ECG

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Cardiac arrest secondary to hypothermia (hypothermic cardiac arrest guideline):
  - Hypothermia in Western Australia as a cause of cardiac arrest is extremely rare and mostly accidental e.g. locked in a cool room. If you suspect that the cardiac arrest was secondary to hypothermia, the emphasis is on high performance CPR and transport
  - Defibrillation and cardioactive medications may not be effective at temperatures < 30°C. VF may resolve spontaneously upon rewarming
  - DO NOT STOP RESUSCITATION IN THE FIELD UNLESS TOLD TO BY A DOCTOR
  - Foil blankets radiate heat and as such are only useful in mild cases
  - Some foil blankets may only have one reflective surface so must be placed the right way round

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Marine envenomation can result from stings due to superficial contact or penetrating injuries from spiny marine creatures.
- Stings:
  - Box Jellyfish
  - Irukandji
  - Blue bottle and other jellyfish
  - Blue Ringed Octopus
- Penetrating injuries:
  - Venomous fish
  - Stingray
  - Sea Urchin

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Box Jellyfish
- Severe Localised Pain
- Adherent tentacles and associated lesions
- Cardiovascular collapse can occur within 20 – 30 minutes which can be life- threating if ACLS is not initiated
- Irukandji
- The sting initially appears minor. Approximately 30 minutes following a sting, symptoms appear (generalised pain, agitation, vomiting and diaphoresis)
- The sting is associated with the release of endogenous catecholamines leading to sympathomimetic toxicity where symptoms could include:
  - Hypertension, tachycardia, arrythmias and chest pain. Severe cases may cause MI
  - Agitation, anxiety, seizures, hyperreflexia
  - Dilated pupils
  - Hyperthermia, sweating
  - Goosebumps, rhabdomyolysis
  - ECG changes can occur which can reflect myocardial injury
  - Clinical features of dysphoria, severe generalised pain, sweating, hypotension and pulmonary oedema in the absence of major dermal findings is pathognomonic of Irukandji syndrome
  - Blue Bottle and other minor jellyfish
  - Immediate localised pain that can last several hours with associated linear eruptions
  - Mild systemic presentations with nausea, vomiting and malaise
  - Blue-ringed Octopus
  - Saliva contains tetrodotoxin which is injected with bite

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Most bites occur when the octopus is handled
- Do not apply a pressure immobilisation bandage (PIB)
- Vinegar will deactivate non-discharged nematocysts but not reduce the pain of venom already injected
- Patients initially stable but suffering severe symptoms within 30 minutes may have an Irukandji sting and require immediate medical attention
- Irukandji syndrome should be considered in any patient presenting with clinical features during or shortly after swimming in tropical coastal waters

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Keep patient at rest and limit movement where possible
- Assist ventilations as required
- Manage cardiac arrest as per guidelines
- Observe patient closely for signs of anaphylaxis; if identified, treat accordingly
- Refer to treatment for (Non)Tropical Jellyfish sting or Venomous Fish below
- Use cold compress to reduce pain and swelling at the site if specific treatment cannot be carried out
- Conduct Vital Sign Survey
- Tropical Jellyfish
- Soak area with vinegar for 30 seconds to neutralise visible stinging cells
- If no vinegar is available: remove tentacles with gloved hands and wash well with sea water, NOT fresh water
- Antivenom is available for some box jellyfish
- Non-Tropical Jellyfish
- Remove any visible tentacles and rinse well with sea water, NOT fresh water
- Where possible, place the affected area in water as hot as the patient can comfortably tolerate for 20 minutes
- If pain not relieved by heat or hot water unavailable, apply cold pack
- Venomous Fish
- Treat for haemorrhage or impaled object if required
- If stung on a limb, immerse stung area in water as hot as the patient can comfortably tolerate

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Apply cardiac monitoring
- Consider advanced airway where required
- Gain IV Access
- Consider blood draw, troponin if indicated
- Consider pain relief
- Relative to pain score, (1-3: Paracetamol, Ibuprofen) (4-6: Panadeine Fort, Oxycodone, Methoxy) (7-10: Fentanyl 1mg/Kg to max 100mcg, Methoxy)
- Consider antiemetic
- Consider prophylactic antibiotics only in consultation with on-call doctor

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Caring for the patients in the hours following envenomation (e.g. including inter-hospital transfer care) will include monitoring for signs of coagulopathy (bleeding from bite site or cannula) and renal impairment. This may include measuring urine output where possible
- Pain relief works best with a multimodal approach

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Snakes produce venom in modified salivary glands, administering this through fangs which pierce the skin. Many snakes in Australia (including sea snakes) can deliver doses of toxin lethal to humans and due this potential, all patients should be managed with a time critical, robust approach regardless of clinical appearance
- Life-threatening effects of toxins include:
  - Neurotoxic muscle paralysis leading to respiratory arrest
  - Coagulation failure leading to excessive bleeding
  - Muscle damage which leads to kidney failure
  - Several snake species in WA pose a threat to humans. Tiger, Coastal Taipan, King Brown, Dugite and Death Adder snakes are all highly venomous

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Puncture mark(s) or scratch
- Bite may be painless, without any visible marks (localized bruising or redness is uncommon in Australian snake bites)
- Headache, nausea/vomiting, abdominal pain
- Swollen or tender lymphatic glands at groin/axilla of bitten limb
- Symptoms can resemble dysfunction of the CNS:
  - Confusion
  - Collapse
  - Visual disturbances
  - Drooping eyelids
  - Difficulty speaking
  - Swallowing or breathing
  - Weakness/paralysis
  - Respiratory weakness/arrest
  - Seizure

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- The size of the patient in relation to the volume of venom will be determinants in predicting the rate of envenomation
- All potential snake bite victims should be transported to a medical facility for assessment

- Most venom reaches the blood stream via the lymphatic system. Research by the ARC indicates little venom reaches the circulation, even after several hours, if a Pressure Immobilization Technique is applied immediately, and maintained. These patients are NOT considered time critical, unless signs of envenomation are evident
- Anti-venom is available for all venomous Australian snake bites
- Paralysis may be long lasting and where possible treatment for respiratory or cardiac arrest should continue until arrival at an appropriate medical facility
- The aim over the next 12 hours (post bite) is to determine if the patient has been envenomed and if so, which snake is responsible. This is based on the history, examination and investigations. This can only be done in hospital

**Do not:**
  - Walk the patient
  - Apply a Pressure Immobilisation Technique if the bite did not occur on a limb
  - Cut, wash or suck the site of the bite
  - Utilise an artery tourniquet
  - Trap/catch or kill the snake. Medical services do not rely on visual identification for anti-venom selection

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Assist ventilations as required
- Manage cardiac arrest (basic life support)
- Keep patient at rest and prevent movement (immobilise the patient, in bed)
- Continuous reassurance
- Apply Pressure Immobilisation Technique to bitten limb as per clinical skill, local pressure if on the torso
- Monitor closely for respiratory depression
- DO NOT remove bandages or splints prior to arrival at a suitable hospital unless authorised by the on-call doctor

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider 12 lead ECG
- Consider vascular access
- Transport all suspected snake bites to hospital

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content: - Caring for the patients in the hours following envenomation (e.g. including inter-hospital transfer care) will include monitoring for signs of coagulopathy (bleeding from bite site or cannula) and renal impairment. This may include measuring urine output where possible

title: Spider and Other Insect Bites & Stings
synonyms: Spider and Other Insect Bites & Stings

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Spider bites are common and rarely result in a serious reaction unless the patient has an underlying allergy to the venom.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- In susceptible individual’s allergy/anaphylaxis may occur. Watch for respiratory and gastrointestinal symptoms and treat for anaphylaxis if identified
- Intense localised pain, redness and swelling at the site. Pain may become intense and spread
- Nausea, vomiting and abdominal pain
- Tender glands in the groin or armpit of envenomed limb

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Venom of the Redback spider can pose a threat to life in children
- Venom from spider and insect bites spreads slowly and may take up to 3 hours to develop
- Pressure at the site of the bite will increase pain
- Although not prevalent in Western Australia, treat any large black spider bite as a funnel-web spider bite

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Keep patient at rest and prevent movement
- Conduct Vital Sign Survey, be alert for rapid deterioration
- Management of sting: bee/wasp sting, remove sting as quickly as possible, to prevent further venom injection through the sting
- Use cold compress to reduce pain and swelling at site
- Consider “over the counter” pain relief (Paracetamol, Ibuprofen) if indicated
- Consider antihistamine (Loratadine, Fexofenadine, Phenergan)
- Consider lignocaine gel (SOOV)

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider ECG
- Consider IV access
- Observe patient closely for signs of anaphylaxis; if identified, treat accordingly
- Consider antibiotics (Dicloxacillin, Flucloxacillin), if infective, only in consultation with on- call doctor
- Consider opiate based pain relief for Red Back spider bites where required
- For large local reactions, consider Prednisolone 25mg (once per day for 3 days) in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider Tetanus

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Children who suffer from anaphylaxis from bee stings may present with diarrhoea and vomiting as the presenting symptom
- Pressure Immobilisation Technique is not to be used on spider bites, except the Australian Funnel-Web Spider
- The Australian funnel-web spider is the only spider in Australia which can cause a threat to life in adults but is not known to be found in WA

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Ticks are parasites that feed on animal and human blood. There are two families of ticks found in Australia: hard ticks and soft ticks. The most common hard ticks encountered in Western Australia (WA) are the ornate kangaroo ticks and the brown dog tick. Soft ticks more commonly parasitise birds, marsupials and horses, rather than people.
Peak tick season in WA runs from September to April.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
Tick Bite Reactions
- Localised reactions, redness, itchiness
- Anaphylaxis can occur but this is not common
- Small itchy, bruise like mark after removal Rickettsial Disease
- Fever
- Rash
- Myalgia
- Headache
- Fatigue
- Eschar (dead tissue at the tick bite site)

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Tweezers and/or solvents of any kind should never be used to remove a tick. Allergic reactions are more likely to occur if the tick is disturbed by trying to remove it with tweezers or applying methylated spirits or kerosene, as this causes the tick to inject more allergen containing saliva
- If signs and symptoms of rickettsial infection, fever, headache, and a characteristic rash, often accompanied by an eschar (a small, black sore) at the bite site do not disturb the tick(s)
- Tick paralysis requires that the tick be attached for 4 to 5 days and is therefore rare; it takes a few days to manifest, presenting as an ascending paralysis, initially with leg weakness and an unsteady gait

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Keep patient at rest and prevent movement

**Management of sting:**
  - if no history of allergy, remove immediately (Tick Removal Skill)
  - If known allergy, tick must remain in place to prevent further envenomation occurring on removal. The hospital will kill the tick in position prior to removal
  - Conduct Vital Sign Survey
  - Observe patient closely for signs of anaphylaxis; if identified, treat accordingly
  - Permethrin cream (used for scabies treatment) available on site can be used to remove tick, pour onto tick

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider ECG
- Consider IV access
- Consider alternative pain relief if cold compress ineffective
- Manage anaphylaxis as per CPG

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- There is no current evidence that Australian tick bites can cause Lyme Disease-like syndromes
- If the patient is asymptomatic 20 mins after tick removal, then advise patient to re- present or seek medical advice if condition changes 22 MEDICAL

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Abdominal pain is a very common symptom experienced by patients. In the majority of patients, the cause is benign and/or self-limiting. However, many causes can be time critical and may require urgent intervention.
- Time critical abdominal emergencies can include:
  - Ectopic pregnancy
  - Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurism (AAA)
  - Peritonitis and sepsis
  - Testicular/ovarian torsion
  - Uncontrolled GI bleeding
  - Bowel obstruction
  - Acute pancreatitis

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Severe abdominal pain and/or tenderness suggestive of peritonism
- Abdominal distention
- Constipation
- Altered Vital Signs, fever, diaphoresis, pallor, signs of sepsis
- Nausea and/or vomiting haematemesis, melena
- History of haematemesis and/or melaena or haematochezia
- Vaginal discharge/bleeding

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Cardiac pain can mimic gastro-oesophageal pain. Any patient with pain above the naval should get a 12 lead ECG, consent not withstanding
- The presence of normal vital signs does not preclude serious abdominal disease
- AAAs may masquerade as renal colic pain, especially in older patients

- Red Flags Requiring Immediate Attention
- Haemodynamic instability (shock, hypotension)
- Rigid abdomen (peritonitis)
- Severe, sudden-onset pain ("worst ever")
- Gastrointestinal bleeding (haematemesis, melena, haematochezia)
- Persistent vomiting or signs of bowel obstruction
- Positive pregnancy test with abdominal pain (risk of ectopic pregnancy)
- Known AAA with abdominal/back pain

**Conditions by region could be:**

**Right Hypochondriac:**
Gallstones or cholecystitis, hepatitis or liver abscess, duodenal ulcers, kidney stones or infection, trauma (#’s)

**Epigastric:**
Gastroesophageal reflux, gastritis, gastric ulcers, pancreatitis, cholecystitis, overeating

**Left Hypochondriac:**
Splenic enlargement or rupture, gastric ulcers, pancreatitis, kidney stones, trauma (#’s)

**Right Lumbar:**
Kidney stones or pyelonephritis, appendicitis (retrocecal), colon disorders (e.g., Crohn’s)

**Umbilical:**
Umbilical hernia, early appendicitis, gastroenteritis, small bowel disorders including obstruction or inflammation, AAA

**Left Lumbar:**
Kidney stones or pyelonephritis, diverticulitis, constipation or impaction

**Right Iliac Fossa:**
Appendicitis, Crohn’s disease, ovarian issues (e.g., cysts, torsions or ectopic pregnancy), hernias

**Hypogastric:**
Cystitis, pelvic inflammatory disease (PID), endometriosis, ectopic pregnancy, bladder distension

**Left Iliac Fossa:**
Diverticulitis, irritable bowel syndrome (IBS), pelvic inflammatory disease (PID), ovarian issues (e.g., cysts, torsions or ectopic pregnancy), hernia

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Vital signs
- Keep patient at rest and prevent movement, allow patient to find comfortable position
- Secondary Survey (including distal colour, temp, sensation, movement and pulses/cap refill)
- Abdominal auscultation, palpation, percussion
- Consider “special signs” (Murphy’s, McBurney’s, Rovsing, Psaos)
- Consider “over the counter” pain relief (Paracetamol, Ibuprofen)
- Urinalysis and pregnancy test where appropriate

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider IV access
- Consider fluid resuscitation
- Consider appropriate analgesia
- 12 lead ECG

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- The target of fluid resuscitation in a dissecting or ruptured AAA, GI Bleed or ruptured ectopic pregnancy is to maintain perfusion to vital organs. Radial pulses can be used as a marker of perfusion
- Patients who have a dissecting AAA are usually in severe pain and require IV narcotic analgesia which should not be delayed or withheld
- Cardiac ischemia may present with abdominal pain, therefore all patients with abdominal pain should have a 12 lead ECG acquired and interpreted for risk stratification
- The assessment of abdominal pain is complex and requires multi-modal investigation
- Patient education: Explain warning signs that require emergency care (e.g., worsening pain, bleeding, fever). Advise follow up for further review and management

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Back pain is a prevalent clinical complaint, frequently associated with benign, self-limiting, or chronic non-emergent conditions. Non-traumatic back pain refers to spinal discomfort without recent physical injury, typically arising from musculoskeletal strain, degenerative conditions, nerve compression, or systemic illnesses. It accounts for 74.4% of back pain cases presenting to emergency medical services. However, prompt recognition and intervention are critical, as certain spinal emergencies may rapidly progress to irreversible neurological impairment, including permanent sensory and motor deficits, loss of sphincter control, and sexual dysfunction.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Recent onset of pain without trauma or lifting
- Severe pain at rest or progressively worsening pain
- Bilateral sciatic nerve pain
- Obvious structural deformity
- Urinary retention and/or incontinence
- Bowel incontinence
- Lower extremity neurological deficit
- Fever, chills, night sweats or recent weight loss
- Perineal, perianal or saddle sensory loss

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- New onset, pre-existing or chronic back pain patients can all develop spinal emergencies, therefore a current diagnosis regarding back pain does not rule out a spinal emergency
- Age <20 years or > 50 years, history of cancer, recent infection or immunosuppression, IV drug use or recent steroid therapy
- A Radiculopathy, often referred to as a "pinched nerve," is a condition where a nerve root in the spinal column is compressed or irritated, leading to pain, numbness, tingling, or weakness in the area supplied by that nerve
- Cauda Equina compression: altered bladder and/or bowel function (e.g., urinary retention, faecal incontinence). Reduced sensation or numbness in the saddle area.
Persistent or progressive bilateral foot or leg weakness
- Visceral disease (e.g., pancreatitis, aortic aneurysm [leak or rupture]) should be suspected if a patient presents sudden, unexplained onset of pain with an absence of aggravating features (e.g., pain not aggravated by spinal movement), associated collapse or hypotension and/or abdominal pain radiating to the back

- Any evidence of serious pathology, spinal infection (osteomyelitis, epidural abscess), vertebral fracture, malignancy, or visceral disease should be treated under those guidelines
- All health care providers should consider cardiac causes in non-traumatic back pain

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Vital Signs
- Conduct secondary survey
- Utilise heat therapy
- Consider OTC pain relief (paracetamol, NSAIDS)
- Consider inhaled analgesia if necessary (Methoxyflurane)
- Position for comfort (e.g., "knees-to-chest" for discogenic pain)
- Ensure Health and Injury prevention team/physiotherapist are involved from the earliest stage practicable to optimise recovery, minimise the risk of long-term complications and have the opportunity to provide education, assessment, and guidance for safe return to work duties

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider IV access
- Consider Fluids (KVO) if necessary
- Consider antiemetic (Ondansetron, Metoclopramide)
- Consider further analgesia (Codeine containing medication, NSAIDS Celecoxib, Ketorolac, Naproxen, Fentanyl in severe pain)
- Consider ECG

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- The assessment of back pain is complex and requires multi-model investigation
- When assessing a patient with back pain, it is important to gather a full medical history including progression of symptoms and any changes in symptom presentation from pre- existing or chronic conditions

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Cellulitis is a localised, bacterial infection of the skin that involves the dermis and subcutaneous tissue, most commonly caused by Staphylococcus aureus and Streptococcus.
