It usually results from a break in the skin such as a scratch, abrasion, laceration, or insect bite. Cellulitis is common in certain remote settings as a result.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Marked area of erythema with associated warmth, swelling and/or tenderness
- Reported rapid spread or erythema in the affected area, lymphangitis (red streaks spreading from the lesion), regional lymphadenopathy (swollen lymph nodes)
- Discharge of clear or yellow fluid and/or exudate
- Fevers, chills, rigor, evidence of sepsis (typically a late sign)

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Presence of high-risk comorbidities such as diabetes, lymphoedema, immunosuppression, peripheral vascular disease, cirrhosis increase likelihood
- Previous history of cellulitis
- Evidence or deficits to the skin such as animal, bites, human bites, eczema, ulcers, psoriasis
- Social factors such as inability of selfcare, intravenous drug use, obesity, poor personal hygiene
- Cellulitis involving the face (especially periorbital region) or joints requires urgent evaluation and transport

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Vital signs (specifically temperature, heart rate, BSL, blood pressure, Spo2)
- Secondary Survey
- Consider outlining the affected area to monitor potential spread
- Consider images, with consent, for review by on-call doctor
- Consider oxygen (if septic and indicated)
- Consider antipyretic only if temperature causing discomfort (paracetamol, ibuprofen, aspirin)

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider IV access
- Consider blood draw and i-STAT (CG4+) use
- Consider fluid resuscitation if indicated
- 12 lead ECG if indicated
- Consider antibiotics (Bactrim, Cephalexin, Clindamycin, Dicloxacillin, Flucloxacillin, Metronidazole [penicillin allergy]) only in consultation with on-call doctor
- If septic, follow sepsis pathway

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Erysipelas is a superficial infection of the upper dermis; the affected area is raised, with a clear line of demarcation between infected and noninfected tissue. It classically involves the facial skin in a butterfly pattern, but also commonly affects a lower limb.
Cellulitis is an infection extending further into the dermis than erysipelas and involves subcutaneous tissue
- There are many conditions that can mimic cellulitis and should be considered
- Continual pruritus (itch) and no pain
- Contact dermatitis
- Allergic reaction
- Cellulitis can affect any part of the body but most commonly presents on the
- Lower extremities
- Feet and toes
- Hands and arms
- Face, particularly around the eyes
- Any images taken must be consented to. Any images must be uploaded to the patient’s medical record for the use of comparison and further management

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: An acute dystonic reaction (ADR) is an extrapyramidal side effect characterised by sudden, involuntary abnormal postures or repetitive movements, resulting from sustained contractions of opposing muscle groups. It is caused by dopamine receptor blockade in the basal ganglia, which leads to a relative excess of cholinergic activity. ADR typically occurs within hours to days after the initiation or dose increase of causative medications, such as antipsychotics or antiemetics.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Laryngospasm (Potentially life-threatening) with stridor, dysphonia, throat pain, and/or dyspnoea
- Deviated eye gaze (upward/sideways), with or without eyelid spasm
- Torticollis (neck tightness and/or tilting/twisting)
- Opisthotonus (extreme back arching) with Arm flexion, leg extension and/or arched back
- Macroglossia, where the tongue feels enlarged, but is not, and protrudes from the mouth
- May present with trismus, dysarthria (slurred or slowing speech) and/or grimacing
- May involve hips, pelvis and abdominal wall muscles (tortipelvic crisis)
- Spasticity (softening and contraction) of trunk and limbs
- The patient will have normal mental status

**Other features may include:**
  - Anxiety
  - Agitation
  - Diaphoresis
  - Tachycardia
  - Tachypnoea

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Often an unusual reaction, but more common in young males, especially if prior history
- Can onset shortly after administration of medication but usually onsets hours to days later
- ADR may mimic several other conditions such as seizures, meningitis or hyperventilation, for example and is often misdiagnosed as anaphylaxis or seizures; focus on medication history and characteristic spasms
- History of Antipsychotic medication (haloperidol, risperidone and especially when used with young males), antiemetics (metoclopramide, prochlorperazine) antibiotics (erythromycin), antihistamines (ranitidine) or antidepressants (SSRI’s)

**Classifications to consider:**
  - Focal: Sudden involuntary contractions in one region (e.g., eyes, neck)
  - Segmental: Simultaneous involvement of two or more adjacent regions
  - Generalised: Widespread involvement; multiple body parts affected

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Position for airway protection (upright, head tilted back, chin lifted, if laryngospasm is suspected)
- Consider oxygen
- Be prepared to assist ventilations if required
- Limb LEAD ECG (be aware of muscular artefact)

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider vascular access
- Consider fluid resuscitation
- Consider IM/IV Benzatropine (1-2mg) in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider promethazine (25 mg to 50 mg IM) in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider benzodiazepines in for persistent spasms in consultation with on-call doctor

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Focal: Blepharospasm, Oculogyric crisis, spasmodic torticollis (wry neck)
- Segmental: Hemiballismus
- Generalised: Chorea, Tardive Dyskinesia

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
MURRAY VALLY)

Encephalitis is inflammation of the brain and can result from various causes including viral, bacterial, fungal, parasitic, post-infectious or auto-immune triggers. It can cause a range of symptoms, including headaches, seizures, and mental confusion. In severe cases, encephalitis can lead to brain damage, stroke, or death. Some of the causes include viruses, bacteria, fungi, parasites, post infection and auto immune conditions. Some recent mosquito related causes in Western Australia include:
- Japanese Encephalitis Virus (JEV)
- Flavivirus Encephalitis
- Murray Valley Encephalitis Virus (MVEV) JEV, MVEV and Flavivirus are rare but serious forms of encephalitis. JEV and MVEV are types of flavivirus but not all flavivirus’ are JEV or MVEV. The focus in the prehospital setting is on treating the symptoms.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Headache
- Fever
- Decreased consciousness, confusion or altered mental state
- Neurological deficits, paralysis
- Seizures or convulsions, coma, death
- Photophobia or neck stiffness (less common)

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Travel in rural areas and undertaking outdoor activities are associated with an increased risk of mosquito bites (such as camping and hiking), or staying in accommodation without air-conditioning, screens or bed nets
- Most people with JEV will be asymptomatic, however those with severe infection may experience neck stiffness, coma, and more rarely, permanent neurological complications or death
- Herpes Simplex Virus can be a cause with Herpes simplex encephalitis being the commonest cause in Australasia. Acyclovir should be started as soon as the diagnosis is suspected
- Infection during pregnancy can result in an increased risk of miscarriage and other complications
- Early recognition is critical. Site based treatment in the worst-case scenarios will be around management of symptoms
- Maintain a low threshold for urgent evacuation

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Insert appropriate airway if required
- Consider Oxygen
- If actively seizing, and trained to do so, administer IM midazolam
- Administer in an emergency and consult with current poison permit holder or RFDS after
- Consider OTC pain relief if able to be taken orally
- Consider antipyretics if able to be taken orally

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider IV access
- Consider fluids if necessary
- Consider IV Midazolam titrated to effect
- Consider analgesia
- Consider Acyclovir, if herpes simplex is a likely diagnosis, in consultation with the on-call doctor
- Consider IV Antibiotics only in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider ECG

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- JEV is a vaccine-preventable disease. Persons who are at risk and that are eligible for vaccination are encouraged to get vaccinated
- JEV and MVEV are reportable conditions. Staff must report this via department of health

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Gastrointestinal Bleeds are categorised by their location. Upper gastrointestinal bleeds (UGIB) originate from the oesophagus, stomach, or duodenum, while lower gastrointestinal bleeds (LGIB) stem from the small intestine, colon, rectum, or anus. They can present with similar symptoms however the type of blood (bright red vs. dark) and its location (vomiting, stool) can provide clues to its origin.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Abdominal Pain (example, UGI – Epigastric, LGI – hypogastric)
- Dizziness, syncope
- Dyspnoea
- Hypotension, pallor tachycardia, (signs of hypovolaemic shock)
- Fatigue, weakness
- Upper GI
- Nausea
- Coffee ground/frank blood vomit (Haematemesis)
- Dark tarry like stool (Melena)
- May have evidence of blood in and/or around mouth
- Lower GI
- Bright red blood from rectum (Hematochezia)
- Maroon coloured stools (seen with slow bleeding from the colon)

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- All patients must receive a thorough clinical assessment that determines the following pertinent information:
  - Upper GI
  - History of liver or renal disease
  - Chronic alcohol consumption
  - Recent use of aspirin, ibuprofen, or other non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs
  - Previous history of pylori infection
  - Previous endoscopy or surgical investigation
  - History of gastric or duodenal ulcers or previous episodes of variceal bleeding

- Lower GI
- History of Diverticulitis
- History of inflammatory bowel diseases, example, Crohn’s disease, Ulcerative colitis
- History of Angiodysplasia
- History of haemorrhoids (more commonly a PR bleed as opposed to GI bleed)
- All patients that present with evidence of a suspected UGIB must be transported to hospital for further examination. This includes instances where the patient is not actively bleeding on examination
- Risk factors can include NSAID use (aspirin, ibuprofen), anticoagulant therapy (warfarin, clopidogrel), liver disease, history of ulcers, or recent GI procedures or surgery
- Some country hospitals may not have the facilities to receive a patient suffering UGIB and may require RFDS transport to definitive care however clinical judgement around stabilisation, if necessary, is paramount

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Vital signs, monitor heart rate and BP, including postural BP, often
- Have a low threshold for calling the on-call doctor
- Position patient for comfort, generally upright, if possible, to avoid aspiration
- Secondary Survey (abdominal assessment, including palpation, percussion and auscultation

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Gain large bore IV access
- Consider Fluids if necessary (titrate to >90mmHg)
- Consider antiemetic
- Consider analgesia
- Consider prophylactic antibiotics only in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider administration of PPI in consultation with on-call doctor
- Apply ECG

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- All patients with suspected UIGB must remain nil by mouth

- Where practical, patients should be positioned upright
- Acute variceal bleeding is associated with high rates of infection due to bacterial translocation and prophylactic administration of antibiotics should be considered
- Currently, there is not enough evidence or study into the benefits of administering tranexamic acid in this cohort of patients, however, can be discussed with on-call doctor or RFDS
- As these patients require emergency review by a gastroenterologist, consideration should be given if the sites local hospital facility can receive UGIB patients

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Hyperglycaemia, also known as high blood sugar, is a condition where the level of glucose (sugar) in the blood is elevated beyond the normal range (4mmol - 9mmol). This often occurs when the body doesn’t have enough insulin, or when the body can’t use insulin effectively. In the prehospital setting, it is most commonly encountered in patients with diabetes mellitus (DM) and may be associated with diabetic emergencies such as diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) or hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state (HHS).

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- General: Polyuria, polydipsia, polyphagia, fatigue, malaise, blurred vision
- Gastrointestinal: Nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain (tetany, particularly in DKA)
- Neurological: Confusion, drowsiness, altered mental state, weakness
- Respiratory: Kussmaul respirations (deep, rapid breathing in DKA), fruity, acetone breath
- Severe (BSL >15mmol/L)/Complicated: Dehydration, hypotension, tachycardia, shock, coma

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Common causes of hyperglycaemia can include inadequate levels of insulin or incorrect doses of diabetes oral medications, infections, excess carbohydrate intake, and stressful situations
- HHS usually presents in older patients with type 2 diabetes mellitus and carries a higher mortality rate and is less common than DKA
- Ketoacidosis occurs mainly in patients with type 1 DM and is not normally seen with type 2 diabetic patients as they have some insulin production preventing severe lipolysis.
However, it can happen in patients with type 2 DM, particularly when taking sodium- glucose co-transporter 2 (SGLT2) inhibitors (examples below) and may occur without hyperglycaemia
- Dapagliflozin (Forxiga)
- Empagliflozin (Jardiance)
- Ertugliflozin (Steglatro)
- If the patient has any of the following, they must be transported to hospital
- Newly diagnosed diabetes
- Suspected cause of hyperglycaemia is due to an illness that requires further review and management
- Inability to keep oral fluids down
- Moderate to severe dehydration
- Pregnant

- Treatment for severe dehydration may be required; however correcting fluid deficits too quickly can cause cerebral oedema

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Position patent to maintain airway if indicated
- Consider Oxygen
- Vital Sign Survey (monitor regularly including pre-and-post intervention blood glucose levels)
- Monitor closely BGL, GCS, SpO2, and Ketones
- Assess hydration where appropriate
- ESO’s should have a low threshold for consulting with on-call doctor

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- 12 lead ECG
- Establish vascular access
- Correction of fluid loss with aggressive intravenous fluid replacement (500ml).
Consultation with on-call doctor after initial fluid stat bolus dose to guide fluid replacement
- Consider subcutaneous insulin administration (0.2 IU/kg) in consultation with on-call doctor (must be self-administered unless patient is physically or visually impaired)

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- DKA is more common in type 1 diabetes; HHS is more common in type 2 and elderly
- Both conditions are medical emergencies with high morbidity and mortality if untreated

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
There is no universally accepted definition of hypoglycaemia (low blood glucose).
Clinically significant hypoglycaemia is confirmed by the presence of a blood glucose level (BGL) low enough to cause signs and/or symptoms of impaired brain function and neurogenic response, generally a BGL <3.3–4.0 mmol/L. In clinical practice, any patient with symptoms and a BGL <4.0 mmol/L should be considered hypoglycaemic and treated accordingly.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Weakness, trembling, shaking
- Diaphoresis
- Headache
- Abnormal behaviour
- Altered conscious state
- Tachycardia
- Seizure

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Relative hypoglycaemia occurs when a patient with chronic, poorly controlled diabetes (for example) reports hypoglycaemic symptoms, but the blood glucose remains above 3.8 mmol/L (70 mg/dL) due to altered glucose threshold adaptation.
This still requires treatment. Patients with diabetes presenting with altered mental status should be presumed to have hypoglycaemia until proven otherwise
- Patients on Acarbose (Glucobay) therapy may require larger glucose doses for hypoglycaemic correction, as acarbose delays carbohydrate absorption and does not stimulate pancreatic insulin release; however, oral glucose may be less effective, and IV dextrose is preferred if available
- The underlying cause of hypoglycaemia should guide therapeutic selection, for example, glucagon administration is ineffective if hepatic glycogen stores are depleted
- Following initial treatment, ongoing clinical assessment and management are essential to ensure resolution and prevent recurrence of hypoglycaemia
- In cases of persistent hypoglycaemia secondary to insulin overdose (accidental or intentional), repeated IV glucose boluses may be necessary to achieve and maintain normoglycaemia; it is important to note that neurological recovery (as indicated by GCS) may be delayed despite correction of blood glucose levels
- Patients experiencing recurrent or prolonged episodes of hypoglycaemia may develop hypoglycaemia unawareness, resulting in diminished or absent warning symptoms
- Conversion of Blood Glucose: 1 mmol/L = 18 mg/dL, 1 mg/dL = 0.056 mmol/L

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Ensure airway patency by inserting an appropriate airway adjunct and providing assisted ventilations as clinically indicated
- Administer supplemental oxygen if indicated
- Vital Sign Survey (monitor regularly including pre-and-post intervention blood glucose levels)

**If conscious (GCS 15):**
  - Administer oral glucose in the form of a sugary drink (e.g., fruit juice, non-diet soft drink) or carbohydrate-rich food (e.g., toast with jam, biscuit)
  - Consider administration of Glucose Oral Gel
  - Reassess GCS and BGL after 10 minutes to evaluate response to treatments
  - Provide a complex carbohydrate meal (e.g., sandwich) if further carbohydrate supplementation is required

**If Impaired Conscious State (GCS <15):**
  - Withhold oral intake if patient is unable to maintain airway protection
  - Administer intramuscular glucagon
  - If within scope, consider advanced management
  - Monitor closely and reassess conscious state and BGL following intervention

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Obtain intravenous (IV) access if readily achievable
- Confirm IV-line patency by flushing with an increased volume of normal saline prior to glucose administration
- Administer intravenous glucose (5% or 10% dextrose solution), titrating the dose to clinical effect
- Reassess GCS and BGL after 10 minutes
- If the BGL is still low and the patient is symptomatic encourage oral intake of complex carbohydrates if appropriate or administer repeat dose of intravenous glucose 10% /repeat dose of intramuscular glucagon (consult with on-call doctor)
- Consider benzodiazepines if seizing
- Consider cardiac monitoring

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Causes to consider in patients who present with hypoglycaemia without the history of DM:
- Exogenous drugs (e.g., insulin, oral hypoglycaemics, ethanol intoxication, quinine, chloroquine, beta-blocker overdose, valproate overdose, salicylate overdose, pentamidine)
- Pituitary insufficiency
- Post-prandial hypoglycaemia (e.g. ’late dumping’ after gastric surgery)
- Liver disease (e.g., hepatocellular cancer, hepatitis and rare genetic defects)
- Addison’s disease
- Islet cell tumours (e.g. insulinomas)
- Immune hypoglycaemia (e.g. anti-insulin receptor antibodies in Hodgkin’s disease or anti-insulin antibodies that release insulin when insulin levels are relatively low)
- Infection (e.g. severe sepsis, malaria)
- Non-pancreatic neoplasms (e.g., fibromas, sarcomas, mesotheliomas, and small cell carcinomas that produce IGF-2; extensive metastases that overwhelm the body’s ability to produce glucose)
- Nesidioblastosis or noninsulinoma pancreatogenous hypoglycaemia (NIPH) syndrome (islet cell hyperplasia, which can be congenital or acquired, e.g. post- gastric surgery)
- Starvation and malnutrition
- Hypothyroidism (myxoedema coma)

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
BACTERIAL & VIRAL MENINGITIS

Meningococcal septicaemia is a severe bloodstream infection caused by the bacterium Neisseria Meningitidis. When the bacteria enter the blood stream they multiply rapidly, damaging blood vessel walls and causing bleeding into the skin and organs. This condition can progress quickly and is life-threatening if not treated promptly.
Bacterial Meningitis is inflammation of the meninges (brain and spinal cord membranes) due to bacterial infection, most commonly caused by Streptococcus pneumoniae, Neisseria meningitidis, and Haemophilus influenzae.
Viral Meningitis is an inflammation of the meninges caused by viral infection. It is the most common type of meningitis and is generally less severe than bacterial. Causes can come from the viruses that cause herpes simplex, shingles, measles, mumps, and gastroenteritis.
Bacterial & Viral meningitis both prevent with similar symptoms and thus should be treated as bacterial in first instance.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- The following conditions cannot be distinguished on clinical grounds alone and diagnosis requires lumbar puncture with cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis, however they may present with:
Meningococcal Septicaemia Bacterial Meningitis Viral Meningitis
  - Rapid onset of fever, malaise, myalgia, headache, vomiting
  - Non-blanching, purpuric rash, especially in young adults
  - Tachycardia, tachypnoea, hypotension, poor perfusion, altered mental state (septic shock)
  - Joint pain, cold extremities, respiratory distress
  - Same as septicaemia, but also
  - Fever, headache, neck stiffness (nuchal rigidity)
  - Photophobia, vomiting, lethargy, irritability
  - Altered mental state, confusion, seizures
  - Kernig’s or Brudzinski’s signs (if assessed)
  - Nausea/Vomiting are often prominent
  - Fever, headache, neck stiffness
  - Photophobia, nausea, vomiting
  - Generally milder symptoms and self- limiting (compared to bacterial meningitis)
  - No rash or shock (unless co-infection or underlying condition)
  - Usually follows viral infections (enteroviruses, herpesviruses)

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- The presence of rapid onset symptoms of sepsis +/- rash may be a sign of meningococcal septicaemia
- Meningococcal is transmitted by close personal exposure to airway secretions / droplets
- Viral Meningitis does not require antibiotics, generally only supportive care, however, discuss with the on-call doctor around the prophylactic administration if unsure of cause
- Meningitis can be Bacterial or Aseptic. Aseptic: meningeal inflammation with negative bacterial cultures and the most common cause is viral (enteroviruses)
- Ensure face mask protection especially during intubation / suctioning
- All patients suspected of meningitis from any cause must be transported to further care

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Consider oxygen
- Vital signs (especially BP, SPO2 and temp)
- Consider intramuscular antibiotics (Benzylpenicillin, Ceftriaxone) as soon as suspected only in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider pain relief if suitable
- Clinicians should have a low threshold for calling current poisons permit holder or RFDS

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Gain IV access
- Administer intravenous fluids, monitor for overload
- Administer intravenous antibiotics (Benzylpenicillin, Cefotaxime, Ceftriaxone, Vancomycin) only in consultation with on-call doctor (if IM administration not already given)
- Administer antiemetic if required
- Consider further analgesia
- Apply ECG

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Ensure medical follow up for staff post exposure
- Ensure public health notification is complete
- Vancomycin is not considered a first-line treatment for bacterial meningitis in most cases. It is primarily used as an alternative or adjunctive therapy under specific circumstances particularly when there is suspicion or confirmation of infection by highly penicillin- or cephalosporin-resistant strains of bacteria such as certain Streptococcus pneumoniae

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Vomiting, or emesis, is the forceful expulsion of gastric contents from the stomach.
Nausea is often described as a stomach unease and is the unpleasant sensation that often precedes vomiting and can have the following characteristics.
- Nausea can occur with or without vomiting.
- Both nausea and vomiting can be signs of various underlying conditions.
Determination of cause requires careful assessment of symptoms.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Common features include: hypersalivation, retching, clamminess, seating, pallor, or abdominal discomfort
- Associated symptoms include headache, dizziness, fever, diarrhoea or abdominal pain
- Possible causes to consider: Gastroenteritis, gastritis, cyclical vomiting, pregnancy, pregnancy induced hyperemesis, bowel obstruction, migraine, vertigo, opioid induced, medication side effects [SSRI’s, chemotherapy/radiation], subarachnoid haemorrhage/space occupying lesion)
- Red Flags: Intractable vomiting (uncontrolled), haematemesis, ALOC, bilious (green) or faecal vomit (obstructions), signs of dehydration

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Low- level evidence suggests that Ondansetron can prolong the Q-T interval, with a subsequent risk of VT. If Long Q-T Syndrome is known or suspected, then Ondansetron should not be administered
- Administration of ondansetron to patients in their first trimester is controversial, with contemporary literature indicating administration is associated with cardiac and cleft palate defects. Administration of ondansetron should only be considered in extreme and uncontrolled hyperemesis where other therapies aren’t an option
- The preferred treatment for nausea and vomiting in the pregnant patient with signs of dehydration is fluid rehydration where appropriate
- Pregnant patients suffering from uncontrolled hyperemesis are not suitable to remain on site
- Nausea and vomiting are common adverse effects of many drugs (e.g., opioids, tramadol, oestrogens, metformin, antibiotics such as erythromycin and metronidazole)
- Ondansetron should not be given to a patient who feel nauseated / are vomiting due to tramadol administration
- Prochlorperazine must only be given via the IM route
- If antiemetics are required for isolated symptoms, oral wafer is preferred
- If the cause of symptoms cannot be determined and is undifferentiated, the presence of serious medical cause must be considered
- Consider and ask if patients are taking Wegovy, Ozempic, Mounjaro
- Patients who have a history of diabetes should be monitored closely and adjusting their insulin dosage may be required (by the patient). Most of these patients will be well versed with their regime, however, consultation with the on-call doctor may be required in these circumstances

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Provide reassurance
- Complete vital sign survey
- Particularly heart rate, blood pressure, temperature and blood glucose in all patients
- Consider cause and appropriate antiemetic if within scope, else try olfactory distraction with alcohol swabs for short term relief of nausea as per additional information below
- Consider urinalysis if fluid intake has been reduced due to symptoms testing specific gravity
- Consider oral rehydration and electrolytes if required
- Consider analgesia where appropriate and tolerated
- Secondary survey where appropriate
- Consider call to on-call doctor if IV fluids are required and not within scope

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider IV access
- Consider IV fluids (1ltr over 1 hour)
- Consider ECG
- Consider i-STAT bloods where appropriate (e.g., troponin, if undifferentiated)
- Consider other causes and treat accordingly
- Transport off site for further review and management if any red flags evident

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Approximately 1 in 2,500 patients will have Long Q-T Syndrome, whether diagnosed or not. If VT (including Torsade de Pointe) follows Ondansetron administration, Amiodarone should NOT be administered as it can further prolong Q-T. Treatment should be focussed on transport with cardioversion or (if unconscious or pulseless) defibrillation
- Ondansetron is ineffective for vestibular causes of nausea and vomiting
- Repetitive smelling of isopropyl alcohol from a swab can be initiated as a primary adjunct. This should be initiated by holding the swab 2.5cm under the nose and taking deep inhalations for 60 seconds
- Undifferentiated nausea and vomiting may include but is not limited to:
  - secondary to Acute Coronary Syndrome
  - secondary to opioid analgesia
  - secondary to cytotoxic drugs or radiotherapy
  - severe gastroenteritis 23 MENTAL HEALTH

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
High-risk mental health conditions are severe psychological states or acute crises where there is an imminent risk of harm to self or others, loss of capacity, or profound disturbance of reality. Conditions commonly include:
- Suicidal ideation or suicide attempt
- Active or recent self-harm
- Psychosis (hallucinations, delusions, severe thought disorganisation)
- Severe mania or agitation
- Acute behavioural disturbance or aggression
- Severe depressive episode with loss of function
- Patients in a state of mental health crisis, including those at risk of violence, exploitation, or unable to care for themselves

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Expressing intent to self-harm or commit suicide
- Acts of self-injury or overdose
- Delusions, hallucinations (auditory, visual), paranoia, or thought disorder
- Severe agitation, restlessness, or unprovoked aggression
- Disorganised, confused, or bizarre behaviour
- Gross neglect of basic needs (food, shelter, hygiene)
- Unco-operative, withdrawn, or catatonic presentation
- Lack of insight into illness
- Recent marked deterioration in mood or function

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Personal Safety: Prioritise your own safety and that of bystanders; do not place yourself in danger
- Assess for weapons, hazardous materials, or escalating behaviours
- Triage early if assistance from other agencies may be required. They may take time to get on scene
- Always address organic causes for behavioural presentations, e.g., CVA, Hypoglycaemia, Hypoxia, TBI
- Be aware of local mental health legislation enabling involuntary transport if the patient lacks capacity or poses risk
- Only the police and Mental Health Transport Officers have legislated authority to apprehend persons in mental health circumstances (section 156 (1) Mental Health Act 2014)
- Section 243 of Criminal Code (7.4.2) states it is lawful for any person to use such force as is necessary to prevent a mentally impaired person from doing violence to any person or property
- The patient’s dignity should be maintained during the period of restraint and, where possible, the method of restraint should be individualised so that dignity can be respected, and the amount of force/restriction applied should be no more than is necessary
- Do not allow police or security officers to handcuff a patient to the stretcher. Clearly document reasons if this cannot be achieved and the service number of the Police Officer
- Evaluate whether the patient can understand, retain, weigh, and communicate decisions regarding care
- Respect privacy but prioritise safety and share information with relevant professionals as permitted
- Where possible, recognise cultural factors influencing expression, stigma, and support
- Record all risks, capacity, assessment, actions taken, and collateral information provided
- If emergency treatment (sedation) is unnecessary, do as little as possible except to reassure while transporting. Be very aware of the patient’s personal space
- Positive RASS score does not automatically infer a need to sedate
- Sedation is HIGH RISK and must only be carried out after careful deliberation between medic and the on-call Doctor and must not be based primarily at the request or influence of other staff members/agencies on scene
- Have a low threshold for contacting the on-call doctor, Mind Matters team (especially if on site) or EAP
- All personnel must be fully aware of, and operate strictly within, the boundaries of their professional scope of practice when involved in mental health-related cases. While it is both appropriate and important to provide initial support, perform basic assessments, and assist with triage, the offering of advice, diagnoses, or interventions that exceed formal qualifications or competencies must be avoided. Acting outside one's scope of practice can lead to unintended harm and may compromise the safety, well-being, and trust of the individuals seeking help

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider and address organic causes for disturbed behaviour as soon as practicable (including but not limited to TBI, hypoxia, hypoglycaemia and overdose)
- Sedation requires sound judgment on a case-by-case basis and should normally align with post de-escalation RASS score and must be discussed with the on-call doctor
- Consider Olanzapine for RASS = 1-3
- Consider Droperidol for RASS = 1-3
- Consider Midazolam for RASS ≥ 3 in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider Ketamine for RASS = 4 (where available)
- Sedation requires sound judgment on a case-by-case basis and should normally align with post de-escalation RASS score and must be discussed with the on-call doctor Note: IM Midazolam is not indicated for primary sedation. It is only to be used IV for maintenance of sedation unless directed otherwise by the on-call doctor. These patients should be sedated with ketamine where available

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Ensure Safety: Remove hazards, engage additional emergency services as required (police, specialist teams)
- Primary Survey, including BSL and Spo2 where possible to rule out organic causes
- Initial De-escalation, offering reassurance, maintain a non-threatening posture, utilise de-escalation techniques. Approach calmly, use clear and simple language, avoid confrontation or escalation
- Conduct RASS Score Assessment frequently Restrain only if essential to protect yourself and the patient using the minimum force necessary
- Restraint may be verbal and/or physical depending on the individual circumstances
- If appropriate, enquire sensitively about possible depression, helplessness and thoughts of suicide
- If patient expresses suicidal thoughts, do not leave patient alone
- Always garner additional support from the on-call doctor
- Consider completion of Suicidal Risk Tool where applicable

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Initiate de-brief with Mind matters team or EAP where relevant and appropriate. Be aware that these pathways are also bound by patient confidentiality
- Example causes for high-risk presentations:
Individual Relationship Social
  - Job loss/unemployment
  - Legal/criminal issues
  - Financial issues
  - Stressful life events
  - Previous suicide attempts
  - Serious illness
  - Mental health illness
  - Substance misuse
  - Social isolation
  - Relationship break-up or loss
  - Bullying
  - Exposure to violence
  - Adverse childhood events
  - Family history of suicide
  - Barriers to accessing health care
  - Suicide cluster
  - Stigma associated with seeking help
  - Access to lethal means Red bolded items are seen regularly within remote mining settings

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Low-risk mental health conditions are mild to moderate psychological or emotional disturbances that do not usually present immediate risks of harm to self or others. These conditions commonly include:
- Adjustment disorders
- Mild anxiety or depressive symptoms
- Situational stress reactions
- Low-grade mood disturbance Patients typically maintain functional capacity with some distress, but without psychosis, suicidality, or immediate risk of violence

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Increased anxiety or irritability
- Difficulty concentrating or decision making
- Changes in appetite or sleep patterns (insomnia, hypersomnia)
- General lack of motivation or withdrawal from social interactions
- Feelings of sadness, hopelessness, or low mood
- Mild somatic complaints such as headaches or fatigue
- Often oriented, cooperative, and able to communicate concerns coherently

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Safety first: Exclude any immediate risk factors (suicidal ideation, self-harm intent, aggression, hallucinations). If present, escalate care appropriately and refer to High Risk Mental Health Presentation CPG
- Confidentiality and rapport: Establish a trusting relationship; provide non-judgmental, empathetic support
- Holistic assessment: Consider physical causes (intoxication, infection) or medication effects
- Cultural sensitivity: Recognise cultural differences in expression of distress and stigma surrounding mental health
- Capacity and consent: Assess decision-making capacity, especially if referral or transport is required
- Support systems: Identify protective factors such as family or community supports

- Documentation: Record findings, assessments, and advice given carefully
- All personnel must be fully aware of, and operate strictly within, the boundaries of their professional scope of practice when involved in mental health-related cases. While it is both appropriate and important to provide initial support, perform basic assessments, and assist with triage, the offering of advice, diagnoses, or interventions that exceed formal qualifications or competencies must be avoided. Acting outside one's scope of practice can lead to unintended harm and may compromise the safety, well-being, and trust of the individuals seeking help

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Psychological support: Provide reassurance, active listening, and validation of feelings
- Environment: Reduce sensory stimuli, provide calm, private surroundings if possible
- Self-care advice: Encourage routines, balanced diet, sleep hygiene, and gradual return to social activities
- Referral: Provide information on MinRes provided Employee Assistance Program (Assure), Mind Matters team where available or follow-up pathways
- Consider advising patient to lean on work group/supervisor where appropriate
- Vital Sign Survey, including BSL
- Consider escalation or further advice from the on-call doctor where required
- Complete the MinRes “Mental State Examination Tool”
- Consider on-call doctor for further advice where required

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content: - Consider oral diazepam for acute situational anxiety (if within SASA) otherwise consult with on-call doctor

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Refer to Mental Health Triage TARP for quick reference
- Initiate de-brief with Mind matters team or EAP where relevant and appropriate. Be aware that these pathways are also bound by patient confidentiality

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
(RASS)

The Richmond Agitation-Sedation Scale (RASS) is a 10-point observational scale used to assess and guide the depth of sedation in critically ill patients, ranging from +4 (combative) to -5 (unarousable).

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: An ectopic pregnancy occurs when a fertilised egg implants outside the uterine cavity, most commonly in the fallopian tube (~95% of cases). Less commonly, it may occur in the ovary, cervix, or abdominal cavity. Ectopic pregnancies are non-viable and potentially life-threatening due to the risk of tubal rupture and internal haemorrhage. A suspected ruptured ectopic pregnancy is considered a medical emergency.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Abdominal pain (in 80-90% of women)
- Vaginal bleeding (in 75% of women)
- Amenorrhoea

**Other symptoms may include:**
  - Abdominal pain may radiate to the neck or shoulder (Kehr’s Sign)
  - Episodes of syncope or presyncope in early pregnancy
  - Lower abdominal tenderness or palpable mass
  - Common non-specific symptoms include vomiting and diarrhoea, mimicking gastroenteritis
  - Tachycardia/ pallor/ hypotension/ shock/ syncope/ orthostatic hypotension
  - Patient in hypovolaemic shock that is disproportionate to visible blood loss

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Clinicians should consider a differential diagnosis of ectopic pregnancy in all women of reproductive age who present with abdominal pain, with a high suspicion when is accompanied by vaginal bleeding
- Clinical manifestations generally occur 6-8 weeks after last normal menstrual period
- Consider a pregnancy test in women of childbearing age who are symptomatic of gastroenteritis
- Any patient with an index of suspicion should not be allowed to transport themselves unless the patient has specifically refused treatment and/or transport
- Risk factors: Previous ectopic pregnancy, pelvic inflammatory disease (PID), tubal surgery, intrauterine device (IUD) in situ, assisted reproductive technologies (e.g., IVF), endometriosis

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Support and reassurance
- Perform vital sign survey
- Consider oxygen
- Secondary / CNS Survey (as required)
- Consider pregnancy test where appropriate
- Liaise with the on-call doctor or RFDS and pre-alert local hospital in suitable country areas, recording all advice in the patients record

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- ECG
- IV access (2x large bore cannulas)
- Fluid resuscitation with emphasis on permissive hypotension
- Consider analgesia in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider antiemetic in consultation with on-call doctor
- Ensure patient is kept warm if signs of shock are present

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- The available evidence for the use of tranexamic acid in management of ruptured ectopic pregnancy is weak
- Ensure patient has offer of support from mind matters and/or EAP

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: In Australia miscarriage is defined as the spontaneous loss of a foetus before 20 weeks’ gestation. 1 in 4 pregnancies would have PV bleeding and out of those with PV bleeding 30-50% of pregnancies with first-trimester bleeding do unfortunately end in miscarriage with the majority being in the first 8 weeks.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Vaginal bleeding (light to heavy)
- Lower abdominal pain or cramping
- Passage of tissue or clots
- Loss of pregnancy symptoms (e.g. breast tenderness, nausea)
- In missed miscarriage: often asymptomatic
- Hypotension and tachycardia suggest significant blood loss, whereas,
- Hypotension and relative bradycardia may indicate cervical shock
- In septic miscarriage: fever, offensive discharge, tachycardia, hypotension

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Early pregnancy loss cannot be prevented
- Early pregnancy bleeding does NOT always result in miscarriage, and some can go onto have a normal pregnancy
- Ectopic pregnancy should be suspected in ALL sexually active women presenting with any of the associated features, i.e., significant abdominal tenderness is suggestive of ectopic pregnancy
- Prehospital diagnosis of miscarriage can be difficult, particularly when the products of conception (POC) are not obvious
- If hypotensive and bradycardic - it would need removal or POC from Cervix (Cervical shock)
- Risk factors can include advanced maternal age, previous miscarriage, smoking, alcohol and/or drug use, infections or chronic illnesses (e.g., diabetes)

**Important questions to ask:**
  - Last menstrual period to ascertain possible gestation
  - If patient has had an ultrasound confirmation of intra-uterine pregnancy (as otherwise ectopic needs to be ruled out as priority)
  - Blood group (consideration for anti-D)
  - If a miscarriage happens later in gestation the foetus may be well developed.
Acknowledging/referring to this as the mother’s baby may help with mum’s psychological care

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Offer continuous support and reassurance
- Vital Sign Survey
- Consider Oxygen if indicated
- Always offer engagement with Mind Matters or EAP
- Have a low threshold for calling the on-call doctor
- If a foetus is passed out of the vagina later in gestation, the placenta may not separate.
In this case, cut and clamp the cord, wrap the pre-term infant and allow mother to hold baby if she wishes

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- ECG
- Ensure constant reassurance
- Consider IV access
- Consider blood draw for i-STAT testing. Chem8 (HB) if bleeding where practicable and available
- Consider IV fluids if clinically indicated
- Consider pain relief
- Consider anti-emetic
- Consider antibiotics only in consultation with on-call doctor if suspecting uterine infection (septic abortion)
- Manage suspected sepsis as per that guideline

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- The only definitive way to diagnose miscarriage is by confirming passage of POC or by ultrasound
- If possible, all tissues and large clots should be retained and transported with the patient
- Miscarriage can have a profound effect on the patient’s mental health. Clinicians must be sensitive in their approach
- Patient education: Bleeding and cramping are expected but should ease

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Postpartum Haemorrhage (PPH) is defined as blood loss > 500ml after childbirth / or blood loss that significantly compromises the mother anytime up to 6 weeks after birth.
There is very low to no risk of site medics attending a primary or secondary PPH on site however there may be a memorandum of understanding with our state emergency services that may require a response to any medical emergency.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
Primary PPH Secondary PPH
- Blood loss of ≥500 mL (vaginal birth) or ≥1000 mL (caesarean birth) within the first 24 hours after delivery

**Severity:**
  - Minor: 500–1000 mL
  - Major: >1000 mL (with further subclassification as severe if >2000 mL)

**Common Causes (the "4 Ts"):**
  - Tone: Uterine atony (most common)
  - Tissue: Retained placental tissue
  - Trauma: Lacerations, uterine rupture, episiotomy
  - Thrombin: Coagulopathies
  - Definition: Abnormal or excessive bleeding after 24 hours and up to 6 weeks postpartum

**Causes:**
  - Retained products of conception (RPOC)
  - Infection (endometritis)
  - Subinvolution of the uterus
  - Vascular abnormalities (e.g., arteriovenous malformations)
  - Lower abdominal or pelvic pain
  - Signs of shock (hypovolaemic or septic); tachycardia, hypotension, fever, confusion, pallor

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Blood loss from PPH is frequently underestimated
- Consideration should be given when elevating the patients’ legs in cases of hypotension as this may conceal further haemorrhage
- Due to the physiology of pregnancy and increased maternal circulation, patients can tolerate larger volume losses before declining rapidly and as such, signs of haemodynamic compromise do not normally show until significant blood loss has occurred

- Atony may be present if you fail to locate the fundus, or it feels soft
- Risk factors can include retained products of conception (POC), infection, subinvolution, trauma, coagulopathy, history of PPH, or C-section
- Be aware that the on-call doctor may ask you to perform bi-manual compression, however if you are not trained to do so you can say no

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Continuous support and reassurance
- Keep the patient warm
- Perform vital sign survey
- Consider oxygen
- Apply direct pressure to any visible bleed, such as tear of perineum
- Secondary / CNS Survey (as required)
- Collect any passed clots or tissue for hospital evaluation

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- ECG
- Gain IV access, 2 x large bore cannula where possible
- Consider blood draw for i-STAT testing. Chem8 if practicable and available
- Fluid resuscitation with normal saline
- Consider TXA in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider oral (Augmentin Duo) or intravenous (metronidazole) antibiotics only in consultation with on-call doctor
- Erythromycin is suitable for penicillin sensitive patients only in consultation with on- call doctor
- Consider external aortic compression
- Monitor patient persistently, recording full observations every 10 minutes

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- It is a clinician’s objective to manage tone, trauma, tissue, temperature and/or thrombin’s
- Blood flow to the placental bed is approx. 750ml/min

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Anaphylaxis is a severe, acute, and potentially life-threatening systemic allergic reaction, characterised by rapid onset and multisystem involvement. It can present with a wide range of clinical features, including respiratory (bronchospasm or upper airway obstruction), cardiovascular (tachycardia, bradycardia, hypotension), cutaneous (urticarial rash or erythema/flushing, and/or angioedema), and gastrointestinal symptoms (diarrhoea, vomiting).

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**Cutaneous:**
  - Swelling of lips, face, or eyes
  - Urticaria (hives)
  - Itching and welts (weal-like swellings)
  - Flushed skin

**Oral:**
  - Tingling sensation in the mouth
  - Gastrointestinal (Note: These symptoms, while listed here, may indicate more severe reactions, especially in insect or medication allergies.):
  - Abdominal pain
  - Nausea and/or vomiting

**Respiratory:**
  - Chest tightness
  - Subjective airway impairment (swollen tongue, laryngeal oedema, stridor)
  - Swelling or tightness in the throat
  - Difficult or noisy breathing
  - Difficulty talking and/or hoarse voice
  - Wheeze or persistent cough
  - Runny nose

**Neurologic:**
  - Decreased mental status

**Cardiovascular:**
  - Hypotension (may present as isolated hypotension in patients with known allergies)
  - Tachy/Bradycardia

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Ascertain whether a non-sedating or sedating antihistamine was administered before arrival. If a non-drowsy antihistamine was given and the patient exhibits decreased alertness, consider anaphylaxis as a potential diagnosis
- Maintain a high index of suspicion for anaphylaxis in patients with unexplained hypotension, particularly when non-traumatic and non-cardiogenic causes have been excluded

- In adults, hypotension is defined as a systolic blood pressure below 90 mmHg or a decrease of more than 30% from the patient’s known baseline and patients without respiratory distress should be positioned supine if possible
- Anticipate and prepare for the possible need for advanced airway management early during treatment
- Do not mobilise patients until the patient has been thoroughly assessed and circulatory stability can be ascertained. Assessment should not occur until at least one hour after a single dose of adrenaline, or four hours if more than one dose of adrenaline has been administered
- Position the patient to facilitate dependent drainage of secretions, typically in the lateral position. Avoid the sitting position unless clinically necessary; consider a semi- recumbent position if required. Ensure legs are outstretched (not flexed in a chair), and do not allow the patient to stand or walk, even if symptoms appear to resolve
- Multiple doses of adrenaline may be required due to biphasic reactions and thus, all patients should be transported for further review and management
- Injection sites should not be close together. Consider rotating injection sites

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Administer Adrenaline via Auto-Injector if available
- Primary survey
- Position patient appropriately (i.e. lateral position, unless respiratory distress predominates)
- Left lateral position for pregnant patients
- Remove trigger / injection mechanism if possible
- Conduct vital sign survey, particularly respiratory rate, blood pressure and pulse oximetry
- Consider oxygen
- Limb LEAD ECG
- ESO’s should have a low threshold for contacting the on-call doctor

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Administer intramuscular adrenaline 500mcg (anterior lateral thigh) and repeat if required
- Apply cardiac monitoring, if peri-arrest, apply defib pads
- Obtain large bore vascular access in all patients
- Consider fluid therapy if required for treatment of anaphylactic shock
- Consider Salbutamol if required for persistent wheeze (lower airway, not to be used as first line)
- Consider Hydrocortisone if persistent wheeze
- If peri-arrest / life-threatening and unresponsive to 3x IM Adrenaline doses and fluid therapy, consider intravenous Adrenaline infusion (the correct IV presentation is important, please ensure prepared appropriately)
- If angioedema / stridor / airway obstruction (upper airway) unresponsive to 2x IM Adrenaline doses, consider nebulised Adrenaline
- If persistently hypotensive OR unresponsive to adrenaline, consider Glucagon IV. Only in consultation with on-call doctor, RFDS or another specialist

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content: - Patient should be taken to a medical facility where they can be monitored for 4 hours after the last dose of adrenaline

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Cardiogenic shock is a severe haemodynamic syndrome marked by inadequate cardiac output, resulting in persistent tissue hypoperfusion and a high risk of multiorgan failure and mortality. It is clinically defined by persistent hypotension lasting more than 30 minutes, with a systolic blood pressure below 90 mmHg or a reduction in mean arterial pressure (MAP) of more than 30 mmHg from the patient’s baseline resulting from cardiac causes such as but not limited MI, Myocarditis, or Arrythmias.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Patient History and Risk Factors
- Patient in shock, all other causes ruled out, and identification of predisposing conditions such as acute myocardial infarction, cardiomyopathy, or prior cardiac events is essential
- Respiratory Findings
- Tachypnoea and/or hypoxia
- Pulmonary congestion
- Cardiovascular Manifestations
- Sustained hypotension
- Jugular venous distension
- Signs of Poor Perfusion
- Poor peripheral perfusion
- Oliguria
- Other Features
- Peripheral oedema
- Chest pain or discomfort

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Patient management should focus on cardiorespiratory support
- Monitor for and address potential complications such as arrhythmias, worsening hypotension, or respiratory failure
- The risk of developing cardiogenic shock during or after AMI is higher in older adults, diabetics, those with previous AMI, and those presenting with hypotension
- Titrate fluid to manage symptomatic hypotension and do not fluid overload

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Position patient appropriately
- Consider IIPV where indicated
- Titrate Oxygen
- Vital Sign Survey and close haemodynamic monitoring (recording vital signs at least every 5 minutes)
- Apply Limb LEAD ECG, monitor for arrythmia
- Consider Aspirin
- Monitor for deterioration
- Consider other causes of shock

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Perform 12-lead ECG
- If due to arrythmia, follow that guideline
- If due to ischemic, follow that guideline
- Establish vascular access, large bore if possible
- Consider IV fluid (generally not effective due to pump failure)
- Ensure defib pads are attached to patient
- Consider Adrenaline bolus in consultation with on-call doctor

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- A high percentage of this cohort of patients end up in cardiac arrest

**Cardiogenic Shock can be caused by:**
  - Impaired contractility
  - Myocardial ischemia and complications
  - Myocarditis
  - Myocardial contusion – Trauma
  - Takotsubo Cardiomyopathy
  - Septic shock
  - Dysrhythmia
  - Tachycardia
  - Bradycardia
  - Valvular dysfunction
  - Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HOCM)

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Hypovolaemic shock is a life-threatening condition resulting from a significant reduction in circulating blood volume, leading to inadequate tissue perfusion and oxygen delivery.
It is most commonly caused by acute blood loss but can also result from severe fluid losses due to burns, vomiting, diarrhoea, or third-space fluid shifts. Early detection is imperative.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Early (Compensated)
- Tachycardia
- Tachypnoea
- Pale, cool, or clammy skin
- Weak or thready pulse
- Mild hypotension (may be absent initially)
- Delayed capillary refill (>2 seconds)
- Anxiety or confusion (especially in the elderly or children)
- Late (Decompensated)
- Marked hypotension (systolic BP <90 mmHg or >30% below baseline)
- Altered mental status (lethargy, confusion, unconsciousness)
- Oliguria or anuria
- Profound pallor
- Peripheral cyanosis
- Marked tachycardia or bradycardia (terminal stages)
- Metabolic acidosis (elevated lactate, base deficit)

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Level of consciousness is a good indicator of cerebral perfusion
- Hypotension is a late sign of shock
- Beta blockers blunt cardiovascular responses to hypovolaemia
- Aggressive fluid management may dislodge clots from micro circulation
- In non-traumatic hypovolaemic shock (e.g., dehydration), aggressive fluid resuscitation is indicated (e.g., 60 mL/kg in the first 15 minutes in severe cases)
- Minimise on scene time as much as is practical with ongoing management undertaken during transport
- Remember: Blood pressure and heart rate may not always reflect the severity of shock, especially in young or fit individuals, with hypotension being a late sign
- Fluid loss can be from excessive diaphoresis (heat related), diabetic ketoacidosis, burns, pancreatitis, sepsis, anaphylaxis (third spacing)

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Stem any life-threatening bleeding, consider CT tourniquet where appropriate
- Primary Survey
- Position patient appropriately, supine with raised legs, elevate affected area where possible
- Consider Oxygen
- Vital Sign Survey
- Secondary Survey
- Consider “triad of death” (hypothermia + acidosis + coagulopathy) and keep patient warm
- Be prepared for deterioration and possible cardiac arrest

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider advanced airway where required
- Establish IV vascular access, 2 x large bore
- Consider blood draw (lactate)
- Consider fluid therapy, targeting 90mmHg (haemorrhagic shock), 110mmHg for head injury
- Administer pain relief as soon as practicable
- Consider antiemetic
- Transport Priority 1 to hospital if patient time critical AND the time taken to arrive would be quicker than calling and transporting via RFDS, pre-notifying receiving facility

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Monitoring urine output is important
- Hypovolaemic shock is a medical emergency requiring rapid recognition and intervention. Early control of bleeding, judicious fluid resuscitation, and prompt transfer to definitive care are essential for optimal outcomes
**Detrimental effects of hypovolaemia:**
  - Oliguria à Acute renal failure
  - Cerebral Ischemia à confusion à decreased LOC à infarction
  - Myocardial ischemia à cardiogenic shock à heart failure
  - Gastrointestinal ischemia, hepatic failure, limb ischemia or lactic acidosis

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Neurogenic shock is a distributive shock resulting from acute spinal cord injury (SCI) or central nervous system (CNS) trauma, leading to loss of sympathetic tone, systemic vasodilation, and impaired haemodynamic stability. It is characterised by hypotension, bradycardia, and warm peripheries due to unopposed parasympathetic activity.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Hypotension: Systolic BP <90 mmHg or >30% below baseline
- Bradycardia: Heart rate <60 bpm (unlike hypovolemic shock, which typically causes tachycardia)
- Warm, dry and flushed skin due to peripheral vasodilation, especially below level of injury)
- Possible hypothermia due to vasodilation
- Respiratory distress (if injury involves cervical spine or brainstem)
- Altered mental status (confusion, lethargy)
- Priapism (in males) or loss of reflexes

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Consider other forms of shock, cardiogenic, hypovolaemic
- Be aware of respiratory depression
- Patients may appear "well perfused" peripherally but are at high risk of central hypoperfusion (e.g., cerebral, renal ischemia)
- Beware of fluid overload
- Any hypotension in a trauma patient should be assumed to be hypovolaemic in origin until proven otherwise, even in a patient with an overt spinal injury. Sources of bleeding must be aggressively sought and controlled. Neurogenic shock is rare in comparison
- Neurogenic shock must be differentiated from "spinal" shock as their managements differ
- Spinal shock: Flaccid paralysis and areflexia below the injury level
- Neurogenic shock: Haemodynamic instability (hypotension + bradycardia)
- As the likely cause on site would be from injury, it is important to ensure spinal precautions are taken
- Vagal stimuli, such as suctioning, nasogastric tube insertion, or intubation may exacerbate neurogenic shock

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Consider IPPV if necessary
- Consider Oxygen
- Maintain spinal precautions and immobilisation
- Vital Sign Survey
- Ensure patient is kept warm
- Consider Secondary/CNS Survey
- Keep patient warm and comfortable (as temperature regulation may be impaired)
- Consider early consultation with RFDS for evacuation

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Apply cardiac monitor/defibrillator
- Establish vascular access
- Administer IV fluid cautiously (aiming for 90mmHg)
- Consider atropine in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider prophylactic antiemetic
- Consider analgesia
- Consider adrenaline use in persistent hypotension only in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider atropine (0.6mg IV boluses)
- If available and trained to do so, insert indwelling catheter

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content: - Ensure patient is correctly packed to be received by RFDS, i.e., Patient is immobilised within a vacuum mattress where available

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Sepsis is a life-threatening organ dysfunction due to a dysregulated host response to infection. Septic shock is a subset of sepsis and is a severe and potentially life-threatening condition that occurs as a result. Septic shock is an underlying circulatory and cellular/metabolic abnormalities are profound enough to substantially increase mortality.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**General:** Fever or hypothermia, chills, rigors, malaise, altered mental status (GCS <15, confusion, lethargy, coma)
**Cardiovascular:** Hypotension (systolic BP <100 mmHg or MAP <65 mmHg), tachycardia, weak or thready pulses, delayed capillary refill, cool or mottled extremities
**Respiratory:** Tachypnoea, hypoxia, dyspnoea, possible respiratory distress or failure
**Renal:** Oliguria or anuria
**Metabolic:** Elevated lactate, metabolic acidosis
**Other:** Evidence of infection (local or systemic), possible skin mottling, cyanosis, or non- blanching rash

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Identify source of infection: Pneumonia, urinary tract infection, intra-abdominal infection, skin/soft tissue infection, indwelling devices, or toxin-mediated sepsis

**People with impaired immune systems:**
  - people being treated for cancer with chemotherapy
  - people with diabetes, people who have had a splenectomy, or people with sickle cell disease, ongoing illnesses or misuse drugs intravenously
  - people taking long-term steroids
  - people taking immunosuppressant drugs to treat non-malignant disorders such as rheumatoid arthritis
  - people who have had surgery, or other invasive procedures, in the past 6 weeks
  - people with any breach of skin integrity (for example, cuts, burns, blisters or skin infections)
  - Pregnant women who have given birth, had a termination of pregnancy or miscarriage, or have continued vaginal bleeding or offensive discharge (consider prolonged rupture of membranes, retained products of conception, caesarean section, etc.)
  - People who have or have been in close contact with people with group A streptococcal infection, for example, scarlet fever
  - Monitor for rapid deterioration: Septic shock can progress quickly; frequent reassessment is critical

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Oxygen as indicated
- Vital Sign Survey, with frequent reassessment of A to E
- Utilise qSOFA tool (additional information)
- Minimise on scene time
- Be prepared for deterioration and possible cardiac arrest

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Obtain IV access, 2x large bore
- Collect pre-hospital blood sample where appropriate
- Measure lactate via i-STAT CG4+* where applicable and if not time critical (*see addition information)
- Consider fluid therapy if indicated, reassess fluid therapy requirements often
- Consider early IV antibiotics only in consultation with on-call doctor
- Apply cardiac monitoring
- Consider adrenaline for hypotension only in consultation with on-call doctor (by infusion pump only were applicable)

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- The qSOFA (quick Sequential Organ Failure Assessment) score is a bedside tool used to rapidly identify patients with suspected sepsis who may be at risk of a poor outcome.
The qSOFA score is based on three clinical criteria, each worth one point:
1. Respiratory Rate: A respiratory rate of 22 or more breaths per minute
2. Altered Mental Status: Measured by a drop in the Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) score of 1 or more points
3. Systolic Blood Pressure: A systolic blood pressure (SBP) of 100 mmHg or less
- A score of 2 or more points suggests a higher risk of sepsis and may prompt further investigation and intervention
- The majority of the sepsis 6 protocol can be initiated on site (blood cultures excepted):
1. Administer oxygen
2. Blood cultures*
3. Administer broad spectrum intravenous antibiotics
4. Administer intravenous fluids
5. Measure lactate
6. Measure urine output

- I-STAT Lactate Testing (reference range 0.30 to 20.00 mmol/L):
  - Normal Range: Less than 1.0 mmol/L.
  - Hyperlactatemia (elevated lactate) 2.0 to 4.0 mmol/L: amber flag, review by on- call doctor.
24 TOXINS

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Benzodiazepines (BZD) are sedative agents which act by potentiating the effects of the inhibitory neurotransmitter GABA within the central nervous system. They are one of the most common prescription drugs used in self-poisoning.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Symptoms usually manifest within 4-6 hours:
  - Ataxia
  - Drowsiness
  - Slurred Speech
  - Decreased GCS
  - Hypotension, bradycardia and hypothermia are possible with large ingestions

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Investigating why/how the overdose occurred is important to rule out self-harm
- For isolated BZD overdoses, full recovery is expected when good supportive care is provided
- Older persons, history of cardiorespiratory comorbidity and ingestion of other CNS depressants suggest a higher risk of complications
- Illicit or non-prescription benzodiazepines are most dangerous and often required intubation when overdosed
- Profound come suggests co-ingestion of a CNS depressing agent, e.g., alcohol, as they may compound toxicity
- It is not uncommon for a BZD overdose to present with hypoglycaemia thus ensure a BGL is acquired

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Offer continuous support and reassurance if applicable
- Position patient appropriately
- Consider Oxygen if indicated
- Vital Sign Survey
- Have a low threshold for calling on-call doctor

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider advanced airway
- Consider IPPV if required
- Apply cardiac monitor, 12 LEAD ECG
- Consider IV access
- Consider fluids if indicated
- Consider glucose if indicated
- Consider anti-emetic
- Consider Flumazenil in consultation with on-call doctor (Alprazolam, illicit benzodiazepine, latrogenic causes)

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
**Examples of BZD’s include:**
  - Alprazolam (Xanax)
  - Clonazepam
  - Diazepam (Valium)
  - Midazolam
  - Nitrazepam
  - Oxazepam
  - Temazepam
  - Lorazepam (Ativan)
  - Flumazenil administration carries the risk or precipitating a toxidrome or a seizure in someone who has either taken a mixed overdose or has a history of epilepsy or chronic benzodiazepine use. If the patient has a seizure, you are now unable to give a benzodiazepine to terminate the seizure
  - Illicit non-prescription benzodiazepines are especially dangerous because the actual content and strength of the drug are often unknown and can vary widely.
Novel/designer benzodiazepines can be much more potent than prescription versions, increasing the risk of overdose, severe sedation, amnesia, and withdrawal. These types of benzodiazepines may contain other drugs or toxic substances, including synthetic opioids
  - Always offer engagement with Mind Matters or EAP if applicable

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Beta blockers, also known as β-blockers, are a class of medications primarily used to manage heart and circulatory system conditions. They work by blocking beta-adrenergic receptors, which reduce the effects of stress hormones like adrenaline and noradrenaline on the heart and blood vessels. Toxicity from accidental or intentional overdose is not uncommon, causing bradycardia that is associated with significant mortality. Commonly used beta blockers include Bisoprolol, Atenolol, Metoprolol, Propranolol and Sotalol.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**Cardiovascular effects:**
  - Bradycardia
  - Heart block, sinus node dysfunction
  - Hyperkalaemia
  - Hypotension
  - Cardiogenic shock
  - Other effects can include, hypo/hyperglycaemia, pulmonary oedema, seizures, coma

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Investigating why/how the overdose occurred is important to rule out self-harm
- Onset of toxicity is often delayed in modified release preparations
- Propranolol and Sotalol are particularly toxic in overdose and can result in:
  - Propranolol: Seizures, coma and QRS widening
  - Sotalol: QT prolongation which can lead to Torsades De Pointes
  - Ingestion of 2g of Propranolol are associated with severe toxicity
  - Effects can be compounded when co-ingested with other cardiac drugs
  - Have IV fluids, atropine and adrenaline prepared and at hand
  - Increased volumes of IV fluids can lead to pulmonary oedema; hence these patients require inotropes (adrenaline) to manage shock

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Offer continuous support and reassurance if applicable
- Position patient appropriately
- Vital Sign Survey (especially BGL)
- Consider Oxygen if indicated
- Consider activated charcoal
- Have a low threshold for calling current poison permit holder or RFDS

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Apply cardiac monitor
- Gain IV access in all patients
- Consider fluids if indicated, given in 250ml boluses to avoid overload and pulmonary oedema
- Consider Atropine in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider Adrenaline in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider Sodium Bicarbonate (if QRS widening) in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider Glucose if indicated
- Consider transcutaneous pacing, with appropriate sedation, in consultation with on- call doctor
- Consider Magnesium Sulphate (Torsades de Pointes) in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider anti-emetic, being aware of QT prolongation in cases of Sotalol toxicity

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Clinical features typically manifest within 4 hours of ingestion
- Activated Charcoal: Mixed data on its efficacy; however, considered low harm intervention for potential benefits
- Atropine: Atropine alone is usually not enough for reversal in beta blocker overdose
- Always offer engagement with Mind Matters or EAP if applicable
- Vasoplegia, also known as Vasoplegic Syndrome, is a condition characterised by abnormally low systemic vascular resistance (SVR) and profound vasodilation leading to hypotension despite a normal or increased cardiac output

- Glucagon is still considered an antidote in beta blocker overdose. Glucagon activates cardiac glucagon receptors, which are coupled to Gs proteins and thereby increase intracellular cyclic AMP (cAMP) independent of beta receptor activity. This results in positive inotropic and chronotropic effects, helping to counteract the negative cardiac effects of beta blockers. However, it is unlikely that any site will carry enough Glucagon to follow this treatment through as a large volume is needed as an initial bolus dose, 5-10mg over 10 minutes and then an additional 1-5mg per hour infusion

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Calcium channel blockers (CCBs) are a class of medications that lower blood pressure by reducing the amount of calcium entering cells of the heart and blood vessels. They work by blocking calcium from entering through specific channels in these cells, which relaxes blood vessels and reduces the heart's workload. Overdose is associated with vasoplegia and cardiovascular collapse. Overdose can lead to potentially life-threatening cardiovascular, metabolic, and neurological effects.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Hypotension, bradycardia, AV block or other arrhythmias, shock (cardiogenic, distributive)
- Altered mental state, seizures (rare, usually secondary to hypoperfusion)
- Pulmonary oedema, respiratory depression
- Nausea and vomiting, decreased bowel sounds/ileus
- Hyperglycaemia, lactic acidosis, warm, flushed skin

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Investigating why/how the overdose occurred is important to rule out self-harm
- CCB toxicity is potentially life-threatening, particularly if Verapamil or diltiazem is taken.
These are more cardio selective
- Those with underlying cardiovascular disease are more at risk of severe toxicity
- Ingestion of other cardiac medications, such as ACE inhibitors, beta blockers or digoxin can cause severe CCB toxicity if taken together

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Offer continuous support and reassurance if applicable
- Position patient appropriately
- Consider Oxygen if indicated
- Vital Signs, BSL
- Consider activated charcoal if presented within first hour of ingestion
- Have a low threshold for calling on-call doctor

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider advanced airway
- Consider IPPV if required
- Apply cardiac monitor, 12 LEAD ECG
- Gain IV access
- Consider Blood draw for i-STAT, check potassium and acid base (CG8+) where applicable
- Consider fluids if indicated
- Consider calcium gluconate in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider Atropine (often ineffective) in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider Adrenaline in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider insulin/glucose bolus dose if indicated (and available) in consultation with on- call doctor
- Consider sodium bicarbonate (if metabolic acidosis evident) in consultation with on- call doctor
- Consider transthoracic pacing with adequate sedation in consultation with on-call doctor

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
**Examples of CCB’s include:**
  - Amlodipine, Nifedipine, Felodipine, Verapamil, Diltiazem
  - Glucagon promotes calcium entry into cells via stimulation of a receptor that is considered to be separate from adrenergic receptors. Glucagon can be considered (in consultation only) in the treatment of CCB for this reason; however, it is unlikely that any site will carry enough Glucagon to follow this through
  - Always offer EAP services where necessary

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Carbon monoxide (CO) is a poisonous, colourless, odorless, and tasteless gas produced by the incomplete combustion of fuels such as gas, wood, coal, and, in some cases, plastics. CO binds to haemoglobin, reducing oxygen delivery to tissues and impairing cellular respiration, which causes tissue hypoxia. This can result in acute organ injury and, in some cases, chronic neurological dysfunction through inflammatory and oxidative mechanisms. Common hazardous sources of CO include contained fires, car exhausts, operating petrol-powered machinery, industrial exposure, and working in confined spaces (where increased CO generation and poor ventilation increases the risk of exposure).

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Headache, confusion, dizziness, syncope
- Tachycardia, palpitations, arrythmia, hypotension, myocardial ischemia
- Pulmonary oedema, dyspnoea
- Nausea/vomiting
- Fatigue, malaise, ataxia
- Seizure, coma, death

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Gas detection must take place prior to entering any area suspect of being contaminated with any hazardous atmosphere
- Investigating why/how the overdose occurred is important to rule out self-harm
- The classic “red-faced” presentation is very rarely seen except in severe/late poisoning
- SpO2 readings can be unreliable in CO poisoning due to the increased affinity when binding with haemoglobin (pulse oximetry gap)
- Patients with underlying cardiovascular disease are particularly at risk
