- Given the long-term neurological conditions and delay in presentation of symptoms, it is important to have a low threshold for suspicion
- All patients need a blood gas, venous or arterial, and must be transported to hospital if there is suspected exposure

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey (Responder safety is paramount)
- High flow Oxygen through a non-rebreather mask, 15L per min
- Manage airway if required
- Remove patient from hazardous atmosphere as soon as is safe to do so
- Vital Sign Survey
- Secondary Survey

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider advanced airway if required
- 12 LEAD ECG
- IV Access
- Consider blood draw for i-STAT testing (CG8+: Carboxyhemoglobin) and/or Troponin if cardiac symptoms present, where applicable
- Consider anti-emetic
- Manage emergent presentations as per that clinical guideline

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content: - Up to 40% may develop delayed neuropsychiatric syndrome (DNS) days to weeks after exposure-memory loss, personality change, movement disorders, depression

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Corrosive agents are substances that cause tissue damage upon contact. Ingestion, inhalation, or dermal exposure to these agents can lead to significant morbidity and mortality. Corrosive agents are chemicals (acids or alkalis) that can cause immediate and progressive damage to tissues on contact. Contamination can occur as a result of accidental occupational exposure or deliberate exposure/ingestion for self-harm.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Ingestion
- Intra oral, oesophageal and/or abdominal pain, burns and/or ulcerations
- Drooling, dysphagia
- Nausea/vomiting
- Risk of perforation and sepsis
- Inhaled
- Dyspnoea, stridor, wheeze, cough, hoarse voice, pulmonary oedema
- Topical Exposure
- Localised pain, burning, blistering, numbness
- Skin discolouration (redness, severe cases may blacken or char skin), rash, cracked, dry or peeling skin, swelling
- Eye exposure
- Burning, stinging, redness, visual disturbance/loss, orbital swelling, lacrimation

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Responder safety is paramount. Do not approach an unsafe scene
- One or more patients may indicate a hazardous atmosphere. Do not approach until the scene is declared safe by the ESO/ERT after gas monitoring has been conducted
- Investigating why/how the overdose occurred is important to rule out self-harm
- The absence of lip or oral burns does not exclude significant gastro-oesophageal injury
- The extent of a direct chemical injury depends on the pH, concentration and volume
- In cases of ingestion, do not induce or encourage vomiting
- Oxygen administration should be withheld in paraquat toxicity unless patient is hypoxic.
Aim for 88%-92% saturation

- All patients with exposure to hydrofluoric acid must be transferred to hospital and not managed on site passed initial treatment. This applies to both inhalation and dermal exposure due to dermal injury ranging from nil visible injury to full thickness burns and must be observed/treated in hospital

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Offer continuous support and reassurance
- Position patient appropriately
- Consider Oxygen if indicated
- IPPV if required
- Remove any clothing and constrictive jewellery
- Consider Diphoterine if available and close by (not for use on hydrofluoric acid)
- Initiate irrigation of the affected area for minimum 20 minutes. Continue during transport
- Vital Sign Survey
- Secondary Survey
- Have a low threshold for calling on-call doctor

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider advanced airway
- Apply cardiac monitor, 12 LEAD ECG
- Consider IV access
- Consider fluids if indicated
- Consider antiemetics
- Consider IV analgesia in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider calcium gluconate gel/solution/nebuliser depending on exposure route of hydrofluoric acid
- Consider prophylactic antibiotics only in consultation with on-call doctor

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content: - Always offer engagement with Mind Matters or EAP if applicable

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Cyanide is a rapidly acting and potentially fatal chemical that inhibits cellular respiration by blocking the mitochondrial enzyme cytochrome c oxidase. This leads to histotoxic hypoxia, where cells cannot utilise oxygen, despite its adequate supply. The most likely situation on a MinRes site to result in cyanide toxicity will be from a fire (up to and including 2025). Cyanide is produced from melting plastics and those enclosed in fires are at considerable risk.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Headache, confusion, altered mental state
- Tachycardia, hyperpnoea & hypertension in early stages, bradycardia, apnoea & hypotension in late stages
- Chest pain, cardiovascular collapse, arrythmia
- Nausea and/or vomiting
- Mydriasis (dilated and unreactive pupils)
- Metabolic acidosis
- Seizure, coma, death

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Gas detection must take place prior to entering any area suspect of being contaminated with any hazardous atmosphere
- Investigating why/how the overdose occurred is important to rule out self-harm
- Co-exposure with carbon monoxide is a risk factor for severe toxicity, i.e. in a fire
- May present with a bitter almond smell to the breath
- All patients in this cohort must be referred off site for further review and management

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Offer continuous support and reassurance if applicable
- If non-life-threatening exposure (skin or hair contact), decontaminate (remove clothing and wash with soap and water for 20 minutes)
- Position patient appropriately
- High flow oxygen through a non-rebreather mask whether conscious or unconscious for at least 30 minutes or until asymptomatic

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider advanced airway
- Consider IPPV if required
- Apply cardiac monitor, 12 LEAD ECG
- Consider IV access
- Consider blood draw for i-STAT (CG4+: Lactate) testing where applicable
- Consider fluids if indicated
- Consider antiemetic
- Consider Hydroxocobalamin in consultation with on- call doctor
- Consider Sodium Bicarbonate, in severe metabolic acidosis, in consultation with on-call doctor

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Caution is advised if the cyanide compound is acetonitrile (found in industry), life- threatening toxicity can be delayed up to 24 hours later
- Patients need to be tested (urine sample for thiocyanate) for chronic cyanide toxicity 2 -3 days after exposure so arrangements for this should be made
- Always offer engagement with Mind Matters or EAP if applicable

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Vital Sign Survey
- Secondary Survey
- Contact poisons information/toxicologist on 131126
- Have a low threshold for calling on-call doctor for all cases

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Drug Induced Hyperthermia refers to elevated body temperature (>38.5°C or >101.3°F), resulting from pharmacologic agents that disrupt normal thermoregulation through increased heat production, reduced heat dissipation, or altered central control mechanisms, examples of which can be found in the additional information section of this guideline.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Elevated core body temperature (>38.5°C; often >40°C in severe cases)
- Tachycardia, hypertension, tachypnoea
- Altered mental status (agitation, confusion, delirium)
- Diaphoresis or hot, red, dry (in anticholinergic syndrome)
- Seizures, myoclonus, tremors, rigidity, or clonus (depending on syndrome)
- Rhabdomyolysis, renal failure, DIC, multiorgan failure (late/severe stages)

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Investigation why/how the overdose occurred is important to rule out self-harm
- Objective is early identification, temperature control and supportive care
- Antipyretics are ineffective in drug-induced hyperthermia (mechanism is non- pyrogenic)
- Airway protection may be needed in severe agitation or seizure activity
- Delay in cooling increases mortality initiate active cooling early

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Offer continuous support and reassurance if applicable
- Position patient appropriately
- Consider Oxygen if indicated
- Vital Sign Survey (continuous pulse oximetry)
- Undress patient and initiate cooling (spray water over bare skin, fan, ice packs to neck, axilla and groin)
- Consider cool oral fluids if tolerated (and no risk of aspiration)
- Have a low threshold for consulting with on-call doctor

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider advanced airway where required
- Consider IPPV if required
- Apply cardiac monitor, 12 LEAD ECG, continuous monitoring
- Gain IV access
- Cooled IV fluids (1ltr normal saline) where practicable
- Consider Midazolam in consultation with on-call doctor

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
Syndrome Causative Drugs Serotonin Syndrome SSRIs, SNRIS, MAOIs, tramadol Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome Antipsychotics (haloperidol), antiemetics (metoclopramide) Malignant Hyperthermia Inhaled anaesthetics (methoxyflurane) Sympathomimetic Toxicity Cocaine, amphetamines, MDMA, synthetic cathinones (bath salts) Anticholinergic Toxicity Antihistamines, tricyclic antidepressants, atropine, scopolamine Salicylate Toxicity Aspirin
- Always offer engagement with Mind Matters or EAP if applicable

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Opiate toxicity (or opioid overdose) occurs when the ingestion or administration of opioids exceeds the individual's tolerance, leading to life-threatening central nervous system (CNS) and respiratory depression. Respiratory failure may lead to death. The aim of the responder is to ensure good supportive care (+/- naloxone) as this will ensure survival.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Hypoxia, cyanosis, bradypnoea, apnoea
- Hypotension, bradycardia
- Miosis (constricted pupils)
- Slurred speech
- Confusion, sedation, coma
- Hypothermia
- Pulmonary oedema (late stage or severe cases)
- Decreased bowel sounds
- Seizures (Tramadol toxicity)
- Serotonin toxicity in tramadol and oxycodone

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Investigating why/how the overdose occurred is important to rule out self-harm
- In the apnoeic patient, ensure the patient is well oxygenated prior to Narcan (Naloxone) administration to avoid confusion and agitation
- If giving Narcan intravenously, ensure it is titrated to effect (no large doses) to avoid acute opioid withdrawal
- Paraphernalia may give early clues to the cause
- The effect of Narcan can wane after a short period of time. All Opioid effected patients should be referred to further care and management, consent not withstanding

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Position patient appropriately
- Maintain and manage airway as required
- IPPV with oxygen where necessary (SpO2 <92% indicates inadequate ventilation)
- Consider intramuscular Narcan (Naloxone) if IPPV is not effective
- Vital signs including continuous pulse oximetry (early respiratory depression indicator) and BGL
- Consider secondary survey
- Have a low threshold for contacting on-call doctor

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider advanced airway
- Consider IV access
- Consider IV fluids if indicated
- Consider titrated IV Narcan if no previous IM dose given and no response to adequate ventilation
- Consider ECG

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Although highly unlikely to be encountered on site, methadone can cause QT prolongation
- If required, ensure patient is given details for Mind Matters or EAP services

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Organophosphates are a common cause of poisoning within the developing world and result in a cholinergic crisis. Organophosphates can be found in readily available preparations such as insecticides, surface and room sprays, head lice shampoo, fumigation and crop protection

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- SLUDGEM: salivation, lacrimation, urination, defaecation, emesis, miosis
- DUMBBELS: diarrhoea, urination, miosis, bronchorrhea, bradycardia, emesis, lacrimation, salivation
- Agitation, seizure, coma

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Responder safety is paramount. Do not enter any area that risks the safety of the responders
- Universal precautions such as gowns and gloves are suitable
- Inhalation or dermal exposure is rarely life-threatening, and the smell of an agent does not indicate exposure. The smell is usually the solvent and poses no toxicity risk to responders
- Investigating why/how the toxicity occurred is important to rule out self-harm
- Oral consumption produces multi-system toxicity. Respiratory muscle weakness and bronchorrhea causes early deaths. Atropine is your first line
- Onset of symptoms can be delayed up to 12 hours

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Offer continuous support and reassurance if applicable
- Position patient appropriately
- Consider Oxygen if indicated
- Remove contaminated clothing
- Wash/Shower with soap where appropriate
- Vital Sign Survey
- Have a low threshold for calling on-call doctor

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider advanced airway
- Consider IPPV if required
- Gain IV access
- Consider IV atropine in consultation with the on-call doctor (1-2mg, repeated every 5 minutes)
- Apply cardiac monitor, 12 LEAD ECG
- Consider fluids if indicated
- Consider antiemetic

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content: - Always offer engagement with Mind Matters or EAP if applicable

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Serotonin syndrome is a potentially life-threatening condition caused by increased serotonergic activity in the central nervous system often due to the use or interaction of certain medications or recreational drugs. It presents as a triad of autonomic instability, neuromuscular abnormalities, and altered mental status.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Confusion, altered mental status
- Abdominal pain
- Agitation, restlessness, clonus (ankles), hyperreflexia, incoordination, ataxia, rigidity
- Tachycardia, hypotension/hypertension
- Tachypnoea
- Dilated pupils, ocular clonus
- Fever, flushing
- Seizure, coma, death

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Investigating why/how the overdose occurred is important to rule out self-harm
- Typically manifests via changes in mental status, autonomic hyperactivity and neuromuscular excitation
- More severe serotonin toxicity with symptoms of progressive hyperthermia, rigidity and seizures is life-threatening. Sedation and rapid cooling are the care priorities
- Citalopram and escitalopram can also affect the QT interval and increases the risk of Torsades De Pointes (TdP)

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Offer continuous support and reassurance if applicable
- Position patient appropriately
- Consider Oxygen if indicated
- Initiate cooling if patient is hyperthermic

- Vital Sign Survey
- Have a low threshold for calling on-call doctor

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider advanced airway
- Consider IPPV if required
- Gain IV access
- Consider fluids if indicated
- Consider Midazolam in consultation with on-call doctor
- Apply cardiac monitor, 12 LEAD ECG for QTc prolongation (be aware of tremor causing artefact)

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content: - Always offer engagement with Mind Matters or EAP if applicable

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Tricyclic antidepressants (TCAs) are used to treat depression, chronic pain, and other psychiatric or neurological conditions and be common medication. Examples include Examples include amitriptyline, nortriptyline, imipramine, and clomipramine. TCA toxicity most commonly results from intentional overdose and can be a life-threatening medical emergency due to its cardiotoxic and neurotoxic effects.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Arrhythmias (tachycardia, heart blocks, VF, VT, asystole), ECG changes, Hypotension
- Reduced level of consciousness, agitation, confusion, nystagmus
- Dry, warm, flushed skin, hyperthermia, dilated pupils
- Hyperreflexia, seizure, coma, death

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Investigating why/how the overdose occurred is important to rule out self-harm
- 10mg/kg or more can potentially be life-threatening
- Large overdoses/toxicity can lead rapidly into seizures, coma and death
- Tricyclics + Alcohol ≈ Torsade De Pointes
- QRS widens due to fast sodium channel blockade, >100ms is predictive of seizures and >160ms is predictive of ventricular tachycardia
- It is unlikely that defibrillation will work, amiodarone and beta-blockers are contraindicated. Anti-arrhythmic drugs should be avoided

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Offer continuous support and reassurance if applicable
- Position patient appropriately
- Consider Oxygen if indicated
- Vital Sign Survey
- Consider activated charcoal if presenting within one hour of ingestion and patient is GCS 15
- Have a low threshold for calling on-call doctor

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider advanced airway (Patients with a reduced GCS and increased heart rate and history of TCA exposure in the past 1-2 hours require immediate intubation. Contact on-call doctor or RFDS immediately)
- Consider IPPV if required
- Continuous cardiac monitoring, 12 LEAD ECG (ECG changes indicate sodium channel blockade)
- R wave in LEAD AVR
- Sinus Tachycardia
- Increased QRS/QTc duration (QRS duration 120ms+ increases risk of seizures, 160ms+ increases risk of ventricular arrhythmias)
- Gain IV access
- Consider blood draw for i-STAT testing
- Consider IV fluids if indicated
- Consider sodium bicarbonate 8.4% in consultation with on-call doctor (1ml/kg repeated every 1-2 minutes to restore a perfusing rhythm)
- IM/IV midazolam if seizure is evident (0.2mg/KG to a maximum of 10mg, titrated to effect)

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content: - Always offer engagement with Mind Matters or EAP if applicable

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Abdominal trauma refers to any injury to the abdomen, resulting from blunt or penetrating mechanisms. It may involve damage to solid organs (e.g., liver, spleen), hollow viscera (e.g., bowel), blood vessels, or the abdominal wall. Abdominal trauma can lead to life- threatening internal bleeding and organ dysfunction. Many serious abdominal injuries my appear insignificant thus making it difficult to predict severity. The proximity of the organs within the torso makes distinguishing between abdomen, chest and pelvic injuries difficult.
Associated injuries outside that cavity should be considered in all patients.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- History: Blunt trauma (e.g., motor vehicle collision, fall, assault) or penetrating trauma (e.g., stabbing, gunshot)
- Visible injuries: Bruising (such as seat belt signs), abrasions, lacerations, open wounds, evisceration
- Signs of shock: Tachycardia, hypotension, pallor, diaphoresis, cool peripheries, delayed capillary refill
- Distension (swollen and firm, late sign), nausea/vomiting
- Localised or diffuse abdominal pain/discomfort, guarding, rigidity, or tenderness on palpation
- Referred pain: Shoulder tip pain, (Kehr’s sign – may indicate splenic injury)
- Associated injuries such as pelvic fractures, chest injuries, long bone fractures

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Some presentations may only have unexplained shock as the only sign of abdominal trauma
- Significant abdominal injuries may present but may not be apparent and show little to no signs of external of trauma or a trivial pattern of injury or mechanism
- Use minimal fluid resuscitation to achieve a systolic BP of 90mmHg or perfusion of vital organs
- Shoulder tip pain may be indicative of an injury that is irritating the diaphragm
- Be mindful that upper abdominal wounds, especially penetrating trauma, may also cause major thoracic damage
- Motor vehicle accidents: Lap belts have a 30% chance of causing mesenteric or intestinal injury
- Any left upper quadrant pain requires an ultrasound to rule out spleen injury

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Manage catastrophic bleeding with haemorrhage control if required
- Primary Survey with c-spine consideration
- Airway management with c-spine considerations
- Oxygen administration if indicated
- Cover exposed viscera with moist, sterile, non-adherent dressing and plastic wrap. Do not attempt to replace organs
- Immobilisation of impaled objects and positioning if required
- Consider pelvic binder if indicated
- Perform remainder of vital sign survey
- Secondary / CNS Survey
- Urinalysis where appropriate
- Pregnancy test where appropriate
- Clinicians should have a low threshold for calling the on-call doctor

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider advanced airway management as required
- Gain IV Access
- Consider IV fluids (permissive)
- Consider tranexamic acid in consultation with the on-call doctor
- Consider antiemetic
- Administer appropriate pain relief
- Apply cardiac monitoring

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Pattern bruising, such as Cullen’s sign (bruising around the belly button) and Grey Turners sign (bruising to the flanks) may take hours to days to develop
- Trauma that presents with eviscerated bowel should be covered with moist sterile pads or cling wrap

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Traumatic amputation refers to the partial or complete removal of a limb or part of a limb, typically resulting from severe trauma such as motor vehicle collisions, industrial accidents, agricultural incidents, or machinery injuries.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Visible loss or mutilation of limb segment
- Severe bleeding or haemorrhagic shock
- Pain, distress, agitation
- Soft tissue injury, exposed bone, possible contamination
- Entrapment of the limb
- Signs of hypovolaemia: tachycardia, pallor, hypotension
- Potential for traumatic shock (altered mental status, cold, clammy skin)

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Ensure scene safety. Responder safety is paramount
- If entrapped, activate backup early, including RFDS
- If entrapped for extended period with crushing force, be mindful of crush syndrome and reperfusion injuries on release
- Rapid Assessment: Time-critical, focus on airway, breathing, circulation (ABC), and catastrophic haemorrhage control
- Pain Management: Severed nerves are exquisitely painful, multi-modal analgesia is essential, including psychological support
- Preservation of amputated part: If possible, recover and preserve the amputated limb for possible replantation
- Always treat an amputated part as if it may be reimplanted. It may at least be useful for achieving skin coverage of a wound
- Reimplantation is less likely to be successful if warm ischemia time exceeds 6 hours
- Psychological Impact: Amputations are distressing; communicate clearly and compassionately with patient, friends, colleagues

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Haemorrhage control. Stop any catastrophic bleeding, e.g., proximal tourniquet, direct pressure
- Persistent reassurance
- Consider c-spine
- Vital Signs
- Multimodal pain relief approach where practicable, including positioning for comfort where practicable
- Try and maintain patient warmth, consider trauma triad
- Gently irrigate (saline) and dress exposed area (stump) with sterile dressings
- Reassess frequently, monitor for haemorrhagic shock
- Secondary Survey
- Retrieve amputated part where possible, place in a sealed bag and place that bag in another bag or container with cool substance. Do not allow direct contact with ice

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Establish Vascular access
- Consider IV Fluids
- Consider IV pain relief, (Fentanyl, Morphine, Ketamine)
- Consider (prophylactic) anti-emetic (Ondansetron, Metoclopramide)
- Consider (prophylactic) IV antibiotics (e.g., cephazolin)
- Consider ADT

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: A burn is tissue damage that is caused by exposure to heat, cold, chemicals, electricity, friction, or radiation. The severity of a burn is determined by the depth and the extent of the body surface area affected. They range from minor to life-threatening and can result in significant fluid loss, infection, and organ dysfunction.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Assess airway burns for (airway swelling may develop hours after injury):
  - Soot in nasal and mouth cavities, cough and hoarseness, black sputum, difficulty in breathing and swallowing
  - Blistering around mouth and tongue, scorched hair, eyebrows or facial hair
  - Pain, pallor, respiratory distress, hypoxia, oedema, anxiety

 **Depth Assessment**
  **- Superficial(First Degree):**
   - Involves the epithelium
  - Pink, Red
  - Painful
  - No Blisters

  **- Superficial Partial(Second Degree):**
   - Epidermis and superficial dermis destroyed
   - Very Painful
   - Often Blistered
   - Pink, moist
   - Blanches

  **- Deep Partial(Second Degree):**
   - Involves epidermis and reticular dermis
  - May blister
  - Mottled pink & white
  - Fairly dry after a day or two
  - Discomfort rather than pain
  - Slow or no cap refill

  **- Full Thickness(Third Degree):**
   - Involves epidermis, dermis, may include fat
  - Does not blanch
  - May be mottled, dry, translucent, black or pale in appearance
  - Full thickness scalds may have a non-blanching appearance

 **Healing & Scarring**
 **- Superficial (First Degree):**
   - Complete, scarless healing within 7 days

 **- Superficial Partial (second Degree):**
   - Heals by epithelial migration within 2 weeks
  - Can convert to deeper injury

 **- Deep Partial (Second Degree):**
   - Will take more than 3 weeks to heal

 **- Full Thickness (Third Degree):**
   - Will not heal, unless very small.
  - Scarring ++
  - Early excision and grafting will reduce scarring and contracture

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Responder safety is paramount, avoid toxic atmospheres, remove patients when it is safe to do so
- Document the time of burn injury
- Assessment of the patient includes inhalation injury; % TBSA affected; site and depth of wounds; the patient’s age; the presence of other injuries, the mechanism of injury; any areas of circumferential burns, co-morbidities and psychosocial issues
- Inhalation of superheated smoke, steam or gases can induce airway oedema and rapid deterioration of airway patency
- Any burns greater than a superficial (first degree) involving the **face, hands, feet, genitalia, major joints, or circumferential burns of the chest or limbs are to be assessed by a major burns service**. These patients **must** be referred
- Circumferential limb burns may lead to compromised vascular supply leading to ischaemia
- Reddened and intact skin areas should not be included when calculating % TBSA burnt
- There may be entry and exit point for electrical burn injuries
- Patient suffering electrocution injuries should be monitored for dysrhythmias and 12- lead ECG must be performed
- Commonly occurring with electrical injuries are long bone fractures and neurological deficits
- Hydrogel dressings can be applied in the absence of water to cool, but this is not preferred
- If access to silver dressings, do not use sodium chloride on the wound as it de-activates the silver dressing
- When cooling the patient, be careful to avoid hypothermia - optimal water temperature is 15° (cool the burn, warm the patient). Do **NOT** use ice
- Covering areas with clingfilm can limit evaporation, heat loss & pain. Be mindful of how tightly it is applied and do not use it as a sole dressing
- Burns sustained within an enclosed/confined space should be considered to have airway burns until proven otherwise
- ALL burns as a result of Hydrofluoric acid must be transported to further care
- Ensure the tetanus status is ascertained if known to the patient
- Ensure fingertips or toes are still accessible to allow for ongoing distal neurovascular assessments

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey with c-spine consideration (mechanism dependant)
- Frequent airway assessments with airway management/ventilatory support if required
- Oxygen therapy (note: carbon monoxide inhalation may provide false SpO2 readings)
- Burn injury assessment (Rule of 9’s or palm of patient = 1% TBSA)
 - Cool burn area for a minimum of 20 minutes, (be wary of hypothermia [cool burn, warm patient])
   - Facial burns, apply a thin layer of emollient, aloe vera for example. NO silver dressings to face
   - Do not attempt to wipe off semisolids (wax, tar, grease, etc.)
   - Dry chemical burns, brush off dry powder then wash with copious amounts of water
   - Consider Diphoterine for chemical burns (not hydrofluoric acid)
   - Hydrofluoric acid, rinse with copious amounts of water until calcium gluconate treatment can be given
- Consider methoxyflurane
- Consider OTC pain relief (paracetamol, ibuprofen)
- Remove jewellery, clothing/constrictions unless adhered to wound
- Apply damp sterile dressings after cooling and/or hydrogels
- Perform Vital Sign Survey
- Elevate all affected areas, when possible, to reduce oedema and infection risk
- Secondary / CNS Survey

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider/be prepared to place advanced airway if signs of airway burns are evident
- Consider salbutamol
- Establish vascular access (intact skin area)
- Administer fluid replacement, (Hartmann’s preferred)
  - 2ml x %TBSA x weight of patient: 50% of total amount over first 8 hours, 50% over 16 hours
  - Normal daily oral fluids to be given concurrently where appropriate
- Administer appropriate pain relief (Fentanyl, Morphine, Ketamine)
- Consider antiemetic
- Consider cardiac monitoring
- Consider debridement of dead skin/non-viable tissue and blisters

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Debridement may improve accuracy of TBSA calculation, and reduce risk of oedema and infection

- Compartment syndrome; signs of ischaemia include:
   - Pain
   - Pallor
   - Paraesthesia
   - Pulselessness or >2 seconds CRT
   - Cool or cold limb
- Referral criteria to state Adult Burn Unit at FSH:
   - Burns greater than 10% total body surface area, full thickness > 5% TBSA
   - Circumferential partial thickness or full thickness burns
   - Burns in conjunction with major trauma
   - Inhalation burns
   - Chemical burns
   - Electrical burns
  - Special area burns i.e. face, neck, hands, feet, perineum/genitalia, joints

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
The “Rule of Nines” is a method used to estimate the total body surface area (TBSA) affected by burns. This rule divides the body into sections, each representing approximately 9% (or a multiple thereof) of the total body surface area. It is particularly useful for quickly assessing burn severity and determining fluid resuscitation needs in emergency situations.

 - **Rule of 9’s***
 - Head (front & back) 18%
 - Chest (9%), Abdomen (9%) Total 18%
 - Back (Upper 9%, Lower 9%) Total 18%
 - Right Arm 9%
 - Left Arm 9%
 - Perineum 1%
 - Right Leg Whole Limb 14%
 - Left Leg Whole Limb 14%

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Chest trauma refers to injury to the chest wall, lungs, heart, or major blood vessels, caused by blunt or penetrating mechanisms. Penetrating trauma involves an object piercing the chest, potentially causing direct injury to internal organs and structures. Both blunt and penetrating chest trauma can rapidly become life-threatening without prompt recognition and intervention. Half of all traumatic deaths have some form of chest injury with most thoracic trauma resulting from blunt force.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Penetrating trauma: entry/exit wound, external bleeding may be evident, internal bleed may be occult
- Blunt trauma: Contusions/abrasions, haematoma, obvious fractures
- Extreme Tachypnoea, haemoptysis
- Bubbling or sucking wounds, surgical emphysema, unequal air entry, asymmetrical or paradoxical chest wall movement, chest hypomobility
- Tracheal shift, jugular vein distension
- Altered level of consciousness
- Cardiac dysrhythmias, ECG changes, hypotension, muffled heart sounds
- Signs of shock: confusion, agitation, decreased consciousness, low blood pressure, rapid weak pulses, pale, cool, clammy

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Do not delay transport for minor interventions if the patient is unstable. Definitive care is in hospital
- Life-threatening injuries may not be initially apparent, and the mechanism of injury is important in guiding further management. Mechanism of injury can predict severity and associated injuries
- Chest injuries may coexist with abdominal, head, or spinal injuries
- Penetrating trauma increases risk for pneumothorax, hemothorax, cardiac tamponade, and major vessel injury
- Penetrating injuries to the chest can cause rapid decompensation. Early recognition and intervention are critical
- Lack of obvious fractures does not exclude injury
- Injuries sustained depend on the mechanism
- Chest pain in trauma can be due can be due to myocardial ischemia, but blunt trauma can precipitate ECG changes as seen in myocardial contusion
 - Adequate analgesia may improve ventilation by allowing improved chest wall expansion, but high dose of opioids may induce respiratory depression
- Pain inhibits effective coughing, making it difficult for patients to clear airway secretions.
Retained secretions further increase the risk of atelectasis (collapsed lung segments) and create an environment for bacterial growth. Analgesia is crucial for adequate ventilation and prevention of pneumonia. It is considered an essential component of chest trauma management and should be initiated as early as possible

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Ensure the scene is safe, responder safety is paramount
- Control catastrophic haemorrhage
- Primary Survey with c-spine consideration
- Airway management/ventilatory support if required
- Oxygen therapy if indicated
- Immobilisation of impaled objects and positioning if required (do no remove object)
- Cover open chest wounds with a chest seal
- Secondary / CNS Survey
- Clinicians should have a low threshold for calling current poison permit holder or RFDS

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider advance airway if indicated
- Gain 2 x large bore IV access
- Consider fluids where indicated
- Administer pain relief, (Fentanyl, Morphine, Ketamine)
- Consider antiemetic
- Consider cardiac monitoring
- Consider IV antibiotics
- Patients in cardiac arrest with suspected tension pneumothorax caused by trauma (with torso involvement) to receive needle thoracentesis if trained to do so
- Arrested and non-arrested patients with signs of pulmonary barotrauma and are suspected of having tension pneumothorax should receive surgical decompression by finger thoracostomy if trained to do so

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
**Open chest wounds:**
   - Covering with an occlusive dressing taped on 3 sides only allow air to escape but not enter the chest
   - If signs of respiratory distress worsen (indicating tension pneumothorax) try removing the dressing from the wound. If this does not help, treat as an absolute emergency

**Impaled objects:**
   - Leave them in place and stabilise, seal around the base of the object with sterile dressings

**Closed injury:**
   - Stabilise rib fractures with patient positioned towards injured side, in a position of comfort
   - Stabilise a flail sternum with the flat of a gentle supporting hand
  - The major life-threatening injuries in thoracic trauma can be found in the mnemonic ATOMFC (airway obstruction/aortic injuries, tension pneumothorax, open pneumothorax with sucking chest wound, massive haemothorax, flail chest and/or cardiac tamponade

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Crush injury occurs when a body part is subjected to a high degree of force or pressure, usually after being squeezed between two heavy objects. This can result in damage to skin, muscle, nerves, blood vessels, and bones. Crush injuries include simple mechanical crush injury, compartment syndrome, and crush syndrome. There are many causes ranging from isolated limb injuries, multisystem trauma, envenomation, drug and toxin exposure, heat stroke, burns and some bacteria/infections. Left untreated, these can lead to tissue necrosis, permanent impairment and crush syndrome.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**Compartment syndrome is characterised by:**
   - Palpable tension of swelling of an anatomical compartment
   - Pain disproportionate to the injury
   - Pain on passive stretching of muscles within the anatomical compartment
   - Paraesthesia of skin and paresis of muscles supplied by nerves traversing the compartment
   - Pallor of skin over the compartment
   - Pulses diminishing as the condition develops is common, but normal peripheral pulses and capillary refill is not uncommon

**Crush syndrome is characterised by:**
   - Compartment syndrome
   - Haemodynamic instability
   - Reperfusion injuries leading to:
   - Lactic acidosis and hyperkalaemia
   - Dysrhythmias
   - Myoglobinuria (dark “tea” coloured urine) leading to renal failure
   - Shock
   - Hypothermia

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- A duration of compression >1 hour significantly increases risk of crush syndrome
- Larger muscle groups (thigh, pelvis) carry higher risk
- High-energy trauma (building collapse, industrial/farm accidents, vehicle entrapment), or prolonged immobilization (unconscious patients)
 - Crush injury should be suspected whenever there is a crushing force. A crush injury should be suspected whenever a part of the body is crushed or compressed or when you are unable to fully see or examine a part of the body
- Prolonged ischaemic and muscular damage = rhabdomyolysis and reperfusion injury on release
- Limbs that present initially numb with a peripheral pulse can lead to rapid swelling with compartment syndrome
- Anticipate hypovolaemic shock post removal of crushing force
- Chest involvement requires immediate removal of crushing force
- Hypothermia is a potential risk for patients suffering crush injuries
- There is insufficient evidence to recommend the routine use of a tourniquet prior to the release of a crushing force to delay the onset of reperfusion injury. The application of a tourniquet is recommended where there is life-threatening bleeding from a limb before or immediately following the removal of the crushing force. Bleeding may be significant following the release of a crushing force
- Compartment syndrome is a surgical emergency. If not diagnosed quickly and treated appropriately it is associated with a high morbidity
- Complications: Acute kidney injury, hyperkalaemia, hypovolemia, sepsis, compartment syndrome, arrhythmias, death

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Ensure scene safety. Responder safety is paramount
- Persistent reassurance
- Manage catastrophic bleeding/prepare for bleeding with a tourniquet
- Primary Survey with c-spine consideration
- Airway management/ventilatory support if required
- Oxygen therapy if indicated
- Vital signs
- Apply limb LEAD ECG
- Secondary / CNS Survey, Monitor for tense, swollen limb with pain out of proportion
- Manage temperature
- Consider patient positioning, i.e., elevate limbs
- Clinicians should have a low threshold for calling the on-call doctor

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider advanced airway if indicated
- IV access should be gained, 2 x large bore where possible
- If compression >1 hour, ensure fluids are running before release of the trapped part to reduce risk of sudden toxin release
- Manage and prevent hypotension with IV fluids
   - Early and aggressive fluids are the cornerstone of preventing acute kidney injury
- Administer pain relief
- Consider antiemetic
- Consider IV antibiotics in consultation with the on-call doctor
 - Anticipate reperfusion injuries:
   - Hyperkalaemia (manage as per that guideline), dysrhythmia, shock

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content: - A person with a crush injury may not complain of pain and there may be no external signs of injury. Any person who has been subjected to crush injury, including from their own body weight, should be taken to hospital for further review and management

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Electrical injuries occur when electrical current passes through the body, causing tissue damage. Injuries are classified as:

 **- High voltage:** >1,000 volts (e.g., industrial, power lines)

 **- Low voltage:** <1,000 volts (e.g., household sources)

 Severity increases with higher voltage, duration of contact, and current pathway. As an example, 0.1mA to the myocardium can result if VF, 1000A can cause sever burn, loss of limb or high-tension injury and >12000A, such as lightning, can cause coma, severe burns and death. Injuries may result from direct contact, electrical arc, or flame/flash burns.
Alternating current (AC) is typically more hazardous than direct current (DC), as AC can cause sustained muscle contraction and cardiac arrhythmias.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Pain
- Burns, entry and exit wounds
- Respiratory arrest or dysfunction
- Cardiac arrest, arrhythmias, palpitations, myocardial damage
- Urinary symptoms
- Neurological deficits, ALOC, seizures, amnesia, motor dysfunction, dysphasia, spinal cord damage
- Fractures – consider spinal injuries
- Compartment syndrome (more likely from high voltage exposure)
- Visual and auditory disturbances

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Scene safety: Confirm source is de-energised before approaching. Avoid water, downed wires, and unstable structures
- Be aware, patients can still be in contact with live wires
- Voltage level: High voltage increases risk of deep tissue and systemic injury that may not be readily obvious ad these need to be sought out
- Damage to underlying structures (e.g. myolysis) is always a possibility and should be investigated
- Patients with entry / exit wounds or cardiac arrhythmia and patients with high voltage injury must be transported to hospital
- All these patients need hospital transfer for bloods
 - Type of current: AC (household/industrial) is more hazardous (lock-in effect) than DC (batteries, lightning) that will throw you away from the source
- Duration of exposure: Longer contact increases severity
- Current pathway: Transthoracic (hand-to-hand/foot) increases risk of cardiac involvement)
- Delayed arrhythmias are rare and occur mostly following cardiac arrest or after exposure to high voltage (>1000V). In these cases, cardiac monitoring is imperative
- Body tissues involved: Muscle, nerve, heart, and vascular structures are most susceptible
- Lightning strike: May cause immediate cardiac and respiratory arrest
- Associated trauma: Falls, blast injuries, secondary injuries
- Moist tissue or wet skin increases conduction and therefore may result in a more severe injury
- Always try and ascertain tetanus status
- Electrical source, voltage, duration of contact, environmental factors at scene are important factors to note

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Responder safety is paramount. Do not approach until the source is confirmed de- energised
- Primary Survey with c-spine consideration
- Consider oxygen with ventilatory support if required
- Burn injury assessment & management
- Perform vital sign survey
- Secondary / CNS survey
  - Remove clothing where necessary and assess for burns and any entry and/or exit wounds
  - Assess for fractures and or muscle contraction
- Urinalysis to detect myoglobinuria

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Apply and assess 12 LEAD ECG
- Manage arrythmia accordingly
  - If in cardiac arrest, follow that guideline
- Consider analgesia
- Consider antiemetic
- Consider IV access and fluids

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- AC, and high DC currents can cause muscular spasm/contraction in which the individual is unable to let go of the electrical source, further, exposure can cause the individual to be hurled away from the electrical source. Household electrical sources are AC
- Lightning injuries: May present with asystole and fixed pupils; aggressive resuscitation is warranted as survival rates are higher than other causes of cardiac arrest

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Eye injuries refer to any harm or trauma affecting the eye or its surrounding structures, including the eyelids, eye socket, and nerves. These can vary in severity from minor surface abrasions and corneal scratches to serious penetrating injuries and chemical burns.
Prompt recognition and appropriate management are essential in all cases to preserve vision and prevent serious complications, including permanent vision loss.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Blunt Trauma: Caused by a direct blow to the eye (e.g., sports injuries or car accidents).
This can result in black eyes, orbital fractures, or damage to internal eye structures like the retina
- Penetrating or Sharp Injuries: Occur when a sharp object punctures or cuts the eye, potentially causing serious damage
- Chemical Burns: Result from exposure to harmful substances, such as cleaning products or industrial chemicals, which can irritate or damage the eye tissues
- Foreign Objects: Small particles like dust, sand, or metal shavings can enter the eye and cause irritation, scratches, or infections
- Thermal or Radiation Injuries: Caused by heat (e.g., explosions, fireworks) or exposure to harmful radiation (e.g., UV rays from the sun or welding arcs)
- Infections: Sometimes, bacterial, viral, or fungal infections resulting from an injury can lead to complications like conjunctivitis or keratitis

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Significant eye injury may be present, despite benign appearance, normal vision and minimal symptoms
- If eyelid oedema makes opening the lids difficult, attempt gentle assessment and document findings
- Nausea associated with analgesia increases the risk of raised intraocular pressure.
Prophylactic antiemetic administration is recommended to avoid secondary injury
- Avoid metoclopramide due to the risk of dystonic reaction perpetuating the injury
- Avoid manipulating the eye as it may result in intraocular pressure (i.e. Eye lid retraction)
- With penetrating injuries, manipulation may result in extrusion of intraocular contents or rupture of the globe
- When flushing an eye, place injured eye down so as not to contaminate the other eye
- For chemical injuries, irrigation takes priority over transport until completed
- Suspect traumatic iritis if eye pain persists following trauma despite treatment with a topical anaesthetic
 - Dependant on nature of the injury, patients may require theatre management. Fast from food and water as needed
- Patients being evacuated by air may require special requirements. Contact RFDS around any requirements

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey with c-spine consideration
- Constant reassurance, keep at rest and discourage eye movement
- Manage catastrophic bleeding (with haemorrhage control as required)
- Do not attempt manipulation

**For penetrating eye injury:**
   - Do not remove any penetrating foreign body from globe. If tolerated, place raised protective eye shield over affected eye, do not pad

**For chemical burns:**
   - Irrigate with Diphoterine (5-15mins) if available or normal saline for a minimum of 30 minutes as early as possible
   - If litmus paper available, test pH

**For lid lacerations:**
   - If required to control bleeding, place direct pressure around eye, avoiding pressure to the globe

 - Position patient supine with 30° head elevation to reduce intra-ocular pressure
 - Note vision, visual field, pupil size and reaction, movement and response to topical eye anaesthetic
 - Secondary / CNS Survey
 - Where appropriate, exam eye with fluorescein
 - Clinicians should have a low threshold for consult with current poison permit holder or RFDS

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Gain IV access
- Administer prophylactic antiemetic
- Consider analgesia
- Monitor patient regularly, recording observations every 10 mins
- Transport priority 1 if time critical, notify receiving facility, or evacuate as soon as is practicable

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Routine padding is no longer recommended. If padding is used, it must not place pressure on the globe. Do not pad an eye with a penetrating injury
- Blunt trauma examples, eye lid injury, orbital blow-out fracture, corneal abrasion, anterior hyphema, traumatic mydriasis, lens dislocation, vitreous haemorrhage, retinal detachment, traumatic iritis, ruptured globe

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Haemorrhage is a potentially life-threatening emergency that requires the early identification of actual and/or potential bleeding sources. Prompt interventions at the scene and early rapid transport to definitive care, where possible, positively impact patient outcomes. Catastrophic bleeding must be managed as a priority. Assess for blood loss in five places:
- External
- Chest
- Abdomen
- Pelvis
- Long bones

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**External Haemorrhage:**
   - Visible bleeding from wounds or body orifices
   - Bruising, swelling, or expanding haematoma
   - Pain at the injury site

 **Internal Haemorrhage:**
  - Abdominal pain or swelling
   - Chest tightness or pain
   - Blood in vomit, urine, or stool (may appear as bright red or dark/tarry)
   - Unexplained bruising
- When considering hypovolaemic shock, refer to that guideline

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Catastrophic haemorrhage refers to severe, life-threatening bleeding that can be fatal within minutes if not rapidly controlled by immediate intervention
- In the majority of cases, direct pressure will be sufficient to control external bleeding.
 - Tourniquets should only be used as a last resort in truly non-compressible extremity bleeding, such as mangled limbs
- In adult patients with traumatic haemorrhage (without traumatic brain injury), permissive hypotension typically aims for a systolic blood pressure of 80–90 mmHg
- In adult patients with traumatic brain injury, a systolic blood pressure of at least 110 mmHg should be targeted
- In adults, hypotension is often defined as a systolic blood pressure below 90 mmHg, or a decrease greater than 30% from the patient's baseline level
- Monitor for the “triad of death”: hypothermia, acidosis, and coagulopathy
 - Older patients can have blunted physiological response, and younger patients can have strong compensatory reflexes
- Decompensating patients might present with Bradycardia and is a late, preterminal sign
- Vagal response to trauma: in some patients’ blood loss can trigger a cardiac reflex and vagal stimulation, causing hypotension and bradycardia. Pain from injuries and procedures (e.g. IV access, intercostal catheter insertion) can also trigger a vagal response
- Some medications, e.g., beta-blockers and calcium channel blockers can prevent a tachycardic response to blood loss

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Address any catastrophic haemorrhage, arresting external haemorrhage by:
   - Direct pressure
   - Limbs: CAT Tourniquet (catastrophic or uncontrolled), document application time, reassess frequently
   - For junctional (groin, axilla, neck) bleeding, use wound packing with haemostatic dressings and direct pressure
   - Elevate the bleeding limb if possible
- Primary Survey with c-spine consideration
 - Airway management/ventilatory support (as required)
 - Consider Oxygen if required
 - Consider posture, lateral, head/limb elevation
 - Haemostatic dressing, Israeli bandage
 - Consider wound packing
 - Consider pelvic binder and/or splinting
 - Keep patient warm
 - Secondary / CNS Survey
 - Minimise on scene time
 - Clinicians should have a low threshold for calling the on-call doctor

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Establish vascular access, 2 x large bore (14g or 16g) cannula
 - The following patients may benefit from fluid therapy:
   - Adult patients with blunt trauma and / or isolated head injury with hypotension and signs of impaired organ perfusion
   - Adult patients with a head injury and penetrating trauma with hypotension and signs of impaired organ perfusion
 - Consider Tranexamic Acid if within time frame (no later than 3 hours post injury)
 - Consider finger thoracotomy in haemothorax differentials
 - Consider analgesia
 - Consider antiemetic

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Adult patients with penetrating trauma, ectopic pregnancy or aortic aneurysm with hypotension and signs of impaired organ perfusion may benefit from permissive hypotension (systolic blood pressure of 80mmHg)
- If major veins of the neck are severed, do not sit the patient up as air may be sucked in and develop into a venous air embolism. If air embolism occurs (sudden deterioration, collapse, neurological changes), lie the patient down and administer high concentration oxygen. Apply local pressure with broad pad and hand
- Patient posture to reduce airway complications attributed to ongoing bleeding must be prioritised over spinal motion restriction
- To reduce the risk of clot dislodgment during extrication, patient movement should be minimised and coordinated
- Penetrating or embedded objects must not be removed in the pre-hospital setting
- Realignment and extension of long bone fractures minimise the tissue space into which bleeding may occur
- Despite significant blood loss, trauma patients may appear well with normal vital signs, due to various factors including:
   - Beta blocker medication
   - The usually hypertensive patient
   - Coagulopathic patient
 - Compensating pregnant/paediatric patient

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
**Minor Injuries:**
 Minor injuries include superficial wounds, abrasions, minor lacerations, contusions, sprains, and simple fractures. These injuries are not immediately life-threatening but require appropriate assessment and management to prevent complications such as infection, further tissue damage, or functional loss.

 **Epistaxis:**
 Epistaxis, or nosebleed, is bleeding from the nasal cavity, nasopharynx, or nostrils. It is common, affecting up to 60% of people at least once in their lifetime. Most cases are benign and self-limiting, but severe or recurrent episodes may indicate underlying pathology or require advanced intervention

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**Minor Injuries:**
 - Localized pain, swelling, or tenderness
- Bruising (contusion)
- Superficial bleeding (abrasion or laceration)
- Limited range of motion (sprain)
- Mild deformity (simple fracture)
- No signs of shock or major trauma

 **Epistaxis:**
 - Bleeding from one or both nostrils
- Blood in the throat or mouth
- Swallowing or spitting blood
- Nasal congestion or blockage
- Dizziness or light-headedness (if blood loss is significant)
- Pallor or signs of shock (rare, in severe cases
- Nausea and Vomiting (frank blood)

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Aseptic technique should be used in the treatment of all open wounds
- Swelling, bruising and deformity are classic signs of fractures. Grade 3 sprains can present this way, and you cannot go wrong treating these as fractures until proven otherwise
- Epistaxis can be a result of increased blood pressure with no other signs
- In terms of epistaxis, consider if the patient is on anticoagulant
- If bleeding persists despite anterior measures, suspect posterior bleeding
- Patients with posterior epistaxis may be bleeding more than can be seen
- In a head-to-head study comparing TXA versus anterior packing with lignocaine/epinephrine alone, patients in the TXA group were more than twice as likely to experience cessation in bleeding with 10 minutes
- Do not pack both nostrils simultaneously unless airway is secured

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey

**Wound care:**
 - Control any external haemorrhage with direct pressure
 - Consider CoPhenylcaine the area for analgesia prior to cleaning
 - Ensure wounds cleaned with sterile water, chlorhexidine or other suitable solution
 - Remove visible debris (do not probe too deeply)
 - Cover wounds with suitable dressing (plaster, sterile gauze, etc.)

**Sprains/strains:**
 - Grade I - Mild degree of ankle sprain: Slight tearing of the ligament with mild swelling
 - Grade II - Moderate degree of ankle sprain: incomplete tearing of the ligament with moderate pain and bruising
 - Grade III - Severe degree of ankle sprain: Complete tearing of the ligament with severe swelling, pain (walking is likely impossible), and bruising
  - Consider compression for sprains, strains
  - Elevate injury where practicable
  - Consider ice to area for 2 minutes every 2 hours
  - Advise rest
 - Ensure, where available, consultation with the onsite Health & Injury Prevention Advisor or Physio takes place

**Simple fracture:**
 - Splint simple fractures

**Epistaxis:**
  - Sit patient in appropriate position, discourage swallowing blood to avoid nausea/vomiting
  - Apply firm pressure with finger and thumb, or have patient perform this, to the nares (soft part of nose) for 10min
  - Consider bolster beneath the nose to catch any blood
  - Consider nasal spray containing oxymetazoline if stocked
  - Consider CoPhenylcaine if bleeding continues
  - Consider nasal packing if epistaxis continues

 - Consider OTC pain relief (paracetamol, ibuprofen [not in active bleeding])
 - Consider Methoxyflurane for pain
 - Assess for distal neurovascular compromise where appropriate
 - Vital signs in all patients
 - Consider secondary survey
 - Enquire about tetanus status where relevant

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
**Wound Care:**
  - Consider Steri-strips, wound glue or suturing where appropriate

 **- Epistaxis**
  - If direct pressure has failed, consider nasal packing with sterile dressing
  - Consider applying IV preparation TXA to packing material in consultation with on- call doctor
  - If bleeding persists despite anterior measures, suspect posterior bleeding
 - Consider antibiotics only in consultation with the on-call doctor
 - Consider analgesia

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Lithium dust, commonly generated during mining, can cause irritation and inflammation of the nasal passages, potentially leading to epistaxis
- Epistaxis Patient education on prevention: humidification, gentle nose care, avoid trauma

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: The NEXUS Criteria (National Emergency X-Radiology Utilisation Study) represent a well- validated clinical decision aid that can be used to safely rule out cervical spine injury in alert, stable trauma patients without the need to obtain radiographic images.

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- The NEXUS Criteria were developed to help physicians determine whether cervical spine imaging could be safely avoided in appropriate patients
- Validation study included a prospective, observational sample of 34,069 patients, aged 1 to 101 years, presenting to 21 US trauma centres. 1.7% of those studied had clinically significant c-spine injuries (CSI). NEXUS Criteria found to have sensitivity of
99.6% for ruling out CSI (2/578)
- Also detected 99.0% (8/818) of ALL c-spine injuries (6 of which were injuries that didn’t require stabilization or specialised treatment)
- Adopting this rule could decrease imaging in these patients by 12.6%
- Subsequent studies have found a sensitivity of 83-100% for CSI with majority finding 90- 100% sensitivity

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Pain is an unpleasant sensory and emotional experience associated with actual or potential tissue damage. Pain is a subjective and individual, multifactorial experience influenced by culture, previous pain events, beliefs, mood and ability to cope. It is therefore emotional as well as sensory in nature. Effective pain management is a core aspect of prehospital care, improving patient comfort, reducing stress responses, and potentially improving outcomes in trauma and medical emergencies.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Verbal and non-verbal behaviours: Moaning, grimacing, guarding, reluctance to move
- Self-reported pain using standardised scales: Numeric Rating Scale (NRS, 0–10), Visual Analogue Scale (VAS), or Wong-Baker FACES (for those with communication difficulties)
- Physiological indicators: Tachycardia, hypertension, tachypnoea, diaphoresis
- Location, onset, character, duration, and alleviating/aggravating factors reported by the patient

 **Score Severity Descriptive**
 - 0 - 3: Severity: Mild
   - Aching, tolerable, minimal impact
- 4 - 6: Severity: Moderate
   - Limits activity, discomfort affects focus
- 7 - 10: Severity: Severe
   - Distressing, distracting, immobilising

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Patients at risk of under-reporting pain and therefore being undertreated for their pain include:
  - Older patients
  - Patients with mental health diagnosis
  - Language or cultural barriers
  - Patients who are pregnant
 - Administration of pain relief can have adverse side effects, such as respiratory depression, hypotension and nausea and/or vomiting
 - Be aware of synergistic effects of combining medications
 - Pain management should be undertaken with a multimodal approach, including reassurance, positioning, splinting, oral, inhaled, topical and intravenous pain relief as examples
- Pain can present in several ways for example somatic, visceral, neuropathic, inflammatory. The presentation of pain should dictate the choice of pain relief given, for exampe:

 - Pain Score: 0 - 3 (examples: Minor sprain, headache):
   - Recommended Management: Paracetamol, NSAID, low dose Codeine
- Pain Score: 4 – 6 (examples: Moderate soft tissue injury):
   - Recommended Management: Tramadol, Codeine, consider IM NSAIDS or IV if oral not tolerated, Methoxyflurane
- Pain Score: 7 – 10 (examples: Major trauma, large fracture):
   - Recommended Management: IV/IM Fentanyl, Morphine, Methoxyflurane (initially)

**Red flags (escalation required):**
  - Pain unrelieved after 2-step management (such as positioning and pain relief)
  - Abdominal pain with guarding and rigidity
  - Headache with confusion/altered GCS
  - Sudden onset of severe headache
  - Chest pain with cardiac risk or SOB
  - Back pain numbness, weakness, or incontinence

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Record vitals, particularly pain score before and after administration of analgesia
- Secondary Survey
- Consider non-pharmacological strategies such as positioning, distraction, reassurance, empathy, immobilisation, splinting, cooling or icing
- Consider over the counter analgesia, paracetamol, ibuprofen, diclofenac if appropriate
- Consider methoxyflurane where appropriate
- Call to on-call doctor for advice and review if stronger pain relief is required and/or is out of scope

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider IV access
- Consider further oral analgesia (Celecoxib, Codeine containing medications, etc)
- Consider IM analgesia (Ketorolac, Morphine, etc)
- Consider IN analgesia (Fentanyl via a 1ml syringe with atomiser, 25 – 50 mcg, repeated 10-15mins)

- Consider IV analgesia (Fentanyl, Morphine, etc)
   - Morphine: 1-2mg, every 15 minutes as required up to maximum of 5 doses in 60 minutes
   - Fentanyl: 1mg per kg initial bolus dose then 20-25mcg every 10 minutes as required to a maximum 200mcgs
- Consider antiemetic (Ondansetron, Metoclopramide, Prochlorperazine, etc)
- Consider cardiac monitoring

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- For Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander patients, pain is often under-assessed and poorly managed due to cultural differences and misunderstanding of pain behaviour. Some Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander patients are reluctant to express their pain. This may be due to:
   - The association they make between pain and past colonial and segregation policies
   - Breaking Aboriginal lore which brings shame to the patient and their community
   - “Nonsense questions”, for example if a patient has a fractured leg, asking them “are you in pain” is considered a “nonsense question” as they are highly likely to be in pain
 - The use of westernised numerical scales or visual tools to undertake an accurate pain assessment may not be understood, particularly if English is not their first language
- To assist in the assessment of the patient’s pain, clinicians should look for non-verbal indicators, such as:
  - Minimal speaking
  - Lying on their side and not wanting to face to clinician or give any eye contact
  - Turning their head away when asked a question
  - Hiding under the blanket
  - Lying completely still, wincing or grimacing, crying, head shaking, or clicking of the tongue
  - “Centring”, which can be interpreted as simulated sleeping and is a process that involves drawing into oneself spiritually and physiologically to block out pain

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Pelvic injuries are high-risk, potentially life-threatening occurrences in trauma, often resulting from significant energy transfer (e.g., crush, falls, vehicle incidents). Injuries can range from minor fractures to life-threatening injuries. Pelvic fractures are associated with significant morbidity and mortality due to potential haemorrhage, organ damage, and long-term functional impairment. Early recognition, accurate diagnosis, and timely management are critical in improving patient outcomes. This guideline provides evidence-based recommendations for the diagnosis, treatment, and management of pelvic injuries.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Mechanism: High-energy trauma, crush, fall from height, or major vehicle incidents
- Pain: Pelvic, lower back, hip, or groin pain-especially with movement or palpation
- Visible signs: Bruising, swelling, deformity, wounds, or seatbelt marks over pelvis/lower abdomen
- Leg features: Unequal leg length, abnormal external rotation, or inability to move lower limbs
- Haemodynamic instability: Hypotension, tachycardia, or signs of shock with trauma history
- Bleeding: Perineal, rectal, vaginal, or urethral bleeding or haematoma
- Difficulty urinating or blood in urine
- Neurological changes: Numbness, weakness, or loss of rectal tone (rare)
- Undifferentiated / uncontrolled hypotension in significant trauma should be a high index of suspicion for intra-pelvic haemorrhage

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Any patient with hypotension and relevant mechanism of injury MUST be considered to have a TIME-CRITICAL pelvic injury
- Haemorrhage from pelvic fracture is essentially bleeding into a free space, potentially capable of accommodating the patients entire blood volume without gaining sufficient pressure-dependent tamponade
- High-energy mechanisms such as motor vehicle crashes or a fall from a height are major risk factors
- Delayed recognition: Subtle signs may be missed, and clinical examination is unreliable, err on the side of caution if there is significant suggestive mechanism to cause a pelvic injury but the patient shows no signs of haemodynamic compromise/hypovolaemic shock and place the pelvic binder under the patient but leave open
- Unnecessary movement and log rolling must be avoided where possible with less than a 15° tilt if required
- Use of the scoop stretcher should be used to avoid log rolling
 - Abdominal, chest, femur, or head trauma are common with high-energy mechanisms
- Where concurrent femur fracture is suspected prioritise pelvis treatment
- Pelvic binders must be applied direct to skin (or nothing more than light underwear)
- Ensure correct position over the greater trochanters prior to tension
- Pelvic binders are often over-tensioned. Be cautious not to reduce the pelvis beyond its normal anatomical position
- Prolonged tension can induce pressure necrosis
- The use of the scoot stretcher is recommended to avoid log rolling the patient, unless extrication requires an extrication board
- Pelvic springing must not be performed, repeated movement or palpation (“springing the pelvis”) increases bleeding risk

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Ensure scene safety, responder safety is paramount
- Manage catastrophic external haemorrhaging, consider pelvic injury as possible catastrophic bleed
- Primary Survey with c-spine consideration
- Airway management/ventilatory support if required
- If possible, expose and inspect for bruising, deformity, or bleeding, but limit further assessment if unstable
- Utilise pelvic binder
- Secondary / CNS Survey
- Consider methoxyflurane
- Consider sublingual antiemetic (Ondansetron Wafer 4mg)
- Apply limb LEAD ECG
- Have a low threshold for calling on-call doctor

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider advanced airway management as required
- Gain IV (2 x large bore)/ IO access
- Consider Tranexamic Acid
- Consider IV fluids
- Consider further pain relief (Fentanyl, Morphine, Ketamine)
- Consider IV antiemetic (Ondansetron 4mg/2ml)

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Hypotension
- Urogenital injuries
- In most instances, considerable force is required to fracture the pelvic bones, therefore associated intra-thoracic and intra-abdominal injuries should always be considered
- Consider distracting injuries as part of the assessment

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
A pneumothorax is a condition characterised by the presence of air in the pleural space, which is the area between the lung and the chest wall. Normally, this space is essentially a vacuum, allowing the lung to expand and contract with breathing. However, when air leaks into this space, it can cause the lung to collapse partially or completely, leading to breathing difficulties.
- It can be classified as:
  - Simple (closed) pneumothorax: Air enters the pleural space without an open wound
  - Open pneumothorax: Air enters the pleural space through a chest wall defect.
  - Tension pneumothorax: A life-threatening condition where air accumulates under pressure, causing mediastinal shift, compromised venous return, and rapid cardiovascular collapse
  - Spontaneous: Primary (no disease) and secondary (underlying lung disease)
  - Non-iatrogenic (traumatic, barotrauma) and iatrogenic (procedure related)

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Dyspnoea/respiratory distress (tightness, difficulty breathing, progressive respiratory deterioration)
- Pleuritic chest pain
- Decreased or absent breath sounds on the affected side (ipsilateral)
- Hyper resonance on the affected side
- Asymmetrical chest movement
- Subcutaneous emphysema (surgical emphysema)
- Tachycardia, hypotension
- Distended neck veins (late sign in tension pneumothorax)
- Tracheal deviation away from affected side (late and unreliable sign)
- Cyanosis, hypoxia
- Open chest wound (sucking chest wound) in open pneumothorax
- Signs of trauma (rib fractures, flail chest, external wounds)

**Late signs:**
  - Decreasing respiratory rate
  - Hypotension
- Decreasing GCS when on O2

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Not all patients will require prehospital intervention, patients who are stable and oxygenating well may be managed with observation and oxygen en route
- Decreased BP or cardiac output are not common in spontaneously breathing patients with tension pneumothorax, if this is detected hypovolaemia should be the primary
- During positive pressure ventilation a higher flow of gas through the pleural defect takes place resulting in a more rapid increase in pleural cavity pressure and progression towards cardiorespiratory arrest
- A higher suspicion for tension pneumothorax should be maintained in the ventilated trauma patient
- The presence of a needle or chest tube does not mean the patient cannot re-develop a tension pneumothorax
- Tension pneumothorax may also persist if there is an injury to the major airways or bronchial tree
- Spontaneous pneumothorax in tall, thin males, such as those with Marfan Syndrome

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Manage catastrophic bleeding if required
- Primary Survey with c-spine consideration
- Consider patient positioning, upright and comfortable or with unaffected side down
- Airway management/ventilatory support if required
- Oxygen therapy if required
- If open pneumothorax, apply a vented chest seal
- Secondary survey
- Clinicians should have a low threshold for consultation with the on-call doctor

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider IV access
- Consider analgesia (Fentanyl, Morphine, Ketamine)
- Consider cardiac monitoring
- Consider needle thoracocentesis in traumatic cardiac arrest with torso involvement
- Indications for Immediate Needle Decompression:
  - Severe respiratory distress and hypoxia (SpO₂ <93%) unresponsive to oxygen
  - Significant hypotension (SBP <80 mmHg) with suspected chest injury
  - Cardiac arrest following chest trauma where tension pneumothorax cannot be excluded
 - Consider finger thoracostomy in consultation with on-call doctor
- Apply ECG
- Consider IV Antibiotics (Cefazoline) in open pneumothorax in consultation with on-call doctor

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content: - There is a high risk of exposure to blood and body fluids. All precautions that serve to minimise risk to the clinician and the patient are to be applied

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: "Post-submersion" or "post-immersion" refers to the period following a submersion or immersion incident, particularly when a person has experienced being submerged in water (or another liquid) to the point of respiratory impairment. This can occur in various scenarios, including drowning accidents, water-related trauma, or incidents involving near-drowning and can ultimately lead to cardiac arrest.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Altered level of consciousness, confusion, agitation
- Dysrhythmias, hypotension, cardiac arrest
- Dyspnoea, non-cardiogenic pulmonary oedema, acute respiratory distress syndrome, aspiration pneumonia, respiratory arrest
- Spinal injury, nausea and vomiting, hypothermia, diving injury

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Tympanic temperature measurement in wet patients is unreliable
- Vomiting and regurgitation often occur following immersion
- Consciousness is not synonymous with recovery, since delayed death from hypoxia can occur
- Gastric distension. Do not attempt to empty the stomach by external pressure
- Soiled airway
- Hypothermia can occur as a secondary result of submersion or through evaporative heat loss after rescue
- If the patient is hypothermic, with no pulse and apnoeic, manage as a hypothermic cardiac arrest
- Potential spinal injury
- Spinal motion restriction is important in all patients with significant mechanism of injury or GCS <15
- Ensure treatable underlying conditions are managed concurrently

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Remove patient from water as soon as possible and only begin rescue breathing in water if immediate exit is impossible
- Remove wet clothing and keep warm
- Primary Survey
   - Utilise lateral positioning to clear airway
   - Consider suction
   - Check for breathing; assist ventilations as required
   - Manage cardiac arrest
- Consider Oxygen
- Vital Sign Survey
   - Closely monitor patients SpO2, temperature and BGL
- Apply cardiac monitoring
- Secondary / CNS Survey (as required)
   - Consider spinal immobilisation if indicated
- Treat hypothermia

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Establish vascular access
- Consider fluid therapy to correct hypovolemia, be mindful to avoid excessive volumes
- Consider analgesia
- Consider antiemetic
- Where trained to do so and available, consider a nasogastric tube to help decompress stomach

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Sequence of events leading to death
   - Water enters the mouth when no longer able to be kept clear
   - Voluntarily spat out or swallowed at first
   - Next conscious response is to hold one’s breath, lasts ~ 1 minute, until inspiratory drive is too high to resist
   - Water is aspirated into the airways, and coughing occurs as a reflex response
   - Laryngospasm may occur (rapidly terminated by the onset of brain hypoxia)
   - Continued aspiration -> hypoxemia -> loss of consciousness and apnoea
   - Final mode of death involves cardiac dysrhythmia: tachycardia -> bradycardia -> PEA -> asystole
- Whole drowning process, from submersion or immersion to cardiac arrest, usually occurs in seconds to a few minutes (> hour in unusual cases of hypothermia)

title: S.P.E.E.D Assessment
synonyms: S.P.E.E.D – Spinal Emergency Evaluation of Deficits

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: The S.P.E.E.D (Spinal Emergency Evaluation of Deficits) assessment is a rapid neurological assessment tool used to evaluate acute spinal cord injuries, focusing on motor and sensory function to determine injury severity and level. It's designed for use in the first hour’s post-injury, helping identify patients who may benefit from early intervention.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Traumatic injury to the spine may result in damage to the vertebral column, the spinal cord, or both structures simultaneously. Spinal cord injury (SCI) is characterised by preserved neurological function above the level of insult, with absent or significantly impaired function below the site of injury; the clinical presentation varies according to the precise anatomical level and severity of the lesion. This guideline encompasses the assessment and management of injuries involving the entire spinal column, including the cervical, thoracic, and lumbar regions.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Loss of sensation (numbness, tingling, loss of pain or temperature) below the injury level
- Loss of motor function or weakness/paralysis in limbs (may be complete or partial)
- Loss of bladder or bowel control
