- Loss of reflexes below the suspected level of injury
- Priapism (persistent penile erection) in males
- Hypotension and bradycardia (neurogenic shock)
- Thermodysregulation
- Respiratory distress (if injury above C5)
- Pain or pressure in neck, back, or head
- Deformity or abnormal positioning of the spine
- Inability to move part(s) of the body immediately after trauma
- Step-off or palpable gap over the vertebral column

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- This CPG should not replace clinical judgment as there may be compelling reasons to immobilise the spine even if all the criteria for a low probability of injury are met
- Patients with spinal cord injury are at a high risk of concomitant shock, haemorrhage and traumatic brain injury
- The KED is inappropriate when rapid extrication is required for patients with life- threatening injuries and/or vital sign alterations. In this circumstance, use manual inline stabilisation with all appropriate resources for extrication
- Patients under the influence of drugs and/or alcohol require additional assessment and questioning before ruling out a c-spine injury and caution must be used in this instance
- All immersion patients without a GCS of 15 should be considered to have a potential spinal cord injury until proven otherwise
 - Paramedics can safely apply the nexus criteria and transport patients without spinal immobilisation but should do so with caution >65 years old
- Hypotensive and tachycardic; consider other injuries
- There is the potential for patient deterioration during lengthy evacuation due the working environment being remote

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Ensure scene safety, responder safety is paramount
- Primary Survey with C-Spine considerations
- Vital Signs
- Secondary Survey
- Assess for signs of spinal trauma, such as pain, tenderness, obvious deformity, or neurological changes, consider Nexus Criteria

 **- Implement spinal precautions if ANY of the following criteria are met:**
  - SPEED tool score of ≤1 in any category, indicating a potential neurological deficit
  - Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) less than 15
  - Presence of spinal pain, tenderness, or deformity
  - Observable neurological deficits (numbness, weakness)
  **- Significant traumatic mechanism, especially if accompanied by:**
  - Influence of alcohol or drugs
  - Distracting injuries
  - Inability to effectively communicate
 - Limb LEAD ECG
 - Utilise vacuum mattress where indicated
 - Have a low threshold for calling the on-call doctor

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Gain IV access, 2 x large bore
- Consider fluids if necessary
- Administer antiemetic
- Consider analgesia
- Hypotensive and bradycardic; consider neurogenic shock
  - Consideration of inotropic support in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider indwelling catheter (IDC) where trained and available

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Patients should be padded appropriately to achieve their individual neutral spinal alignment (i.e. Most adult patients require a small amount of padding under the head, children may require padding under the torso, patients with increased thoracic kyphosis may require additional padding under the head at achieve the correct alignment)
- If a semi-rigid collar is indicated but unable to be applied (for example due to injury, patient habitus etc.) alternative options such as head blocks and/or in-line manual stabilisation should be considered and all considerations and decisions documented accordingly
- Significant cervical spine injury due to hanging is uncommon. Routine cervical spine immobilisation is not required unless the body has fallen from a height greater to or equal to the height of the person
- Cervical spine immobilisation is generally not required if there is an isolated penetrating injury to the neck (e.g. stabbing or gunshot). When multiple mechanisms of injury suggestive of spinal involvement are present immobilisation criteria should apply
- When used the semi-rigid collar must be correctly sized and fitted to suit the patient. If this cannot be achieved the best approach should be reconsidered and alternative options such as head-blocks and/or manual in-line immobilisation should be considered. Patients may sit up on a slight incline (15 degrees) if they are having trouble breathing
- Patients who are co-operative, ambulant and without neurological symptoms may self- extricate short distances and coached to keep their head and neck as still as possible
- Head blocks should be used to limit lateral movement if spinal precautions are indicated. Whenever possible, patients should be removed from scoop stretchers or extrication boards prior to transport due to risk of pressure injury
- Spinal shock is physiological response to spinal cord injury resulting in temporary loss or depression to most spinal reflex activity below the level of the injury (spinal cord concussion)

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI) is a significant cause of morbidity and mortality worldwide. It can result from external forces leading to damage to the brain. The severity of TBI can vary from mild concussions to severe, life-threatening brain damage. Early identification, prompt management, and effective rehabilitation are essential for improving outcomes in patients with TBI.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Altered level of consciousness (confusion, agitation, drowsiness, unresponsiveness)
- Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) less than 15
- Loss of consciousness (even transient)
- Vomiting (especially repeated)
- Severe or worsening headache
- Amnesia (antegrade or retrograde)
- Focal neurological deficits (e.g., limb weakness, facial droop, slurred speech)
- Unequal or unreactive pupils, sensitivity to light or noise
- Seizures
- Signs of skull fracture (periorbital ecchymosis, mastoid bruising, CSF leak from nose/ears)
- Penetrating head injury or visible scalp lacerations
- Bleeding from ears or nose
- Difficulty sleeping or staying asleep

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Suggestive indicators of raised ICP and imminent brain herniation:
  - Systolic hypertension / widening pulse pressure
  - Bradycardia, abnormal respirations. i.e. “Cushing’s triad”
 - Hypoxia, hypotension, and hypothermia can worsen TBI outcomes
 - Hyperventilation should be avoided as it exacerbates cerebral ischaemia and can reduce venous return. It is generally used as a last resort where signs of impending cerebral herniation are evident
 - Exercise caution when considering Nasopharyngeal Airway during treatment of a patient with a suspected base of skull fracture
 - Patients receiving anti-coagulant and/or antiplatelet therapy will have an increased risk of haemorrhage
- Excluding hypoglycaemia is essential
- It is important to avoid raised ICP from impaired venous return. Ensure constricting tapes, ties and collars are loosened from around the neck and patient is positioned head up to 30° if possible
- Do not use the 30° elevation in the hypovolaemic patient
- Reassess airway, breathing, circulation, and neurological status frequently during delays or long transports

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Ensure scene safety, responder safety is paramount
- Manage catastrophic bleeding
- Primary Survey with c-spine consideration, avoid head tilt, utilise jaw thrust
- Airway management/ventilatory support if required
- Consider Oxygen where indicated, maintaining SpO2 above 90%
- Immobilise when mechanism is suggestive of spinal involvement
- Secondary Survey
- Position patient with 30° head elevation if condition permits to improve venous drainage
- As much as is possible, prepare patient for rapid evacuation; prioritise safe, timely transport
- Clinicians should have a very low threshold for calling on-call doctor

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider advanced airway management, spinal precaution considerations
- Establish vascular access, 2 x large bore
- Consider fluids, avoid hypotension with judicious use of crystalloids if needed to maintain a systolic blood pressure > 110mmHg
- Use IV Glucose 10% as required to maintain blood glucose level >4mmol/L
- Apply cardiac monitoring
- Consider Tranexamic Acid
- Consider pain relief
- Consider Ondansetron
- Consider Midazolam
- Consider Ketamine for patients exhibiting combative TBI (if in scope)
- Consider 12 lead (Cranial changes to ECG are a late sign)

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Primary injury is generally irreversible therefore the aim of treatment is to prevent secondary injury by supporting cerebral haemodynamic and metabolism. Hypoxia and hypotension cause the most prominent secondary injuries and may more than double mortality
- Seizures
- Combativeness or agitation
- Clinical deterioration
- Death
- NICE head injury guidance recommends TXA is administered as soon as possible to patients of all ages with a GCS of 12 or less. TXA is administered 62 minutes earlier if given in the pre-hospital setting, this will be even longer in the remote setting, highlighting the importance of pre-hospital administration

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: The primary survey is a systematic, rapid assessment used to identify and manage immediate life threats. It follows the cABCDE approach: catastrophic haemorrhage, Airway, Breathing, Circulation, Disability, and Exposure. The aim is to detect and treat conditions that are immediately life-threatening before progressing to the secondary survey.

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
**c - Catastrophic Haemorrhage**
- Identify and control life-threatening external bleeding immediately
- Apply direct pressure, wound packing, tourniquet (limb), or haemostatic dressings as appropriate

**A - Airway**
- Assess airway patency: is the patient speaking, crying, or breathing?
- Look for foreign body, vomit, secretions, swelling, or trauma
- Open airway: head-tilt chin-lift (non-trauma) or jaw thrust (trauma)
- Insert OPA or NPA if airway not maintainable
- Consider definitive airway if unable to maintain

**B - Breathing**
- Assess respiratory rate, depth, symmetry, and effort
- Auscultate for bilateral breath sounds
- Apply pulse oximetry; administer oxygen if indicated
- Identify and treat: tension pneumothorax, open chest wound, massive haemothorax, flail chest
- Seal open chest wounds with vented chest seal

**C - Circulation**
- Assess heart rate, skin colour, temperature, and capillary refill (<2 sec)
- Identify ongoing haemorrhage - internal or external
- Establish IV/IO access; administer fluids per protocol
- Apply cardiac monitoring

**D - Disability**
- Assess level of consciousness: AVPU or GCS
- Pupils: size, equality, reactivity
- Blood glucose level (BGL)
- Assess for focal neurological deficits

**E - Exposure**
- Expose patient fully to identify all injuries
- Prevent hypothermia - keep exposure brief and cover after assessment
- Log-roll to examine posterior surfaces

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Treat each life threat as it is found before moving to the next step
- Reassess frequently - especially after any intervention
- In trauma, prioritise haemorrhage control and spinal precautions
- Document findings including time of first assessment
- Communicate findings clearly using ISOBAR or IMIST-AMBO when handing over

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: The secondary survey is a systematic head-to-toe examination performed after life threats identified in the primary survey have been addressed. It aims to identify all injuries and abnormalities. The CNS (Central Nervous System) component focuses on neurological status, cranial nerve assessment, and focal deficits.

sections.title: History (SAMPLE)
sections.content:
**S** - Signs & Symptoms: Chief complaint and current symptoms
**A** - Allergies: Medications, environmental, food
**M** - Medications: Current medications including OTC and supplements
**P** - Pertinent past history: Previous illnesses, hospitalisations, surgeries
**L** - Last oral intake: Food and drink, time
**E** - Events leading to presentation: Mechanism, onset, duration

sections.title: Head-to-Toe Examination
sections.content:
**Head & Face**
- Inspect and palpate scalp for lacerations, haematoma, deformity
- Eyes: pupils (size, equality, reactivity), periorbital bruising (raccoon eyes), Battle's sign
- Ears: blood, CSF otorrhoea
- Nose: bleeding, CSF rhinorrhoea, septal deviation
- Mouth: airway, dentition, lip/tongue injuries

**Neck**
- Tracheal position, JVD, surgical emphysema, tenderness
- Apply cervical collar if indicated

**Chest**
- Inspect for symmetry, deformity, paradoxical movement
- Palpate ribs, sternum, clavicles
- Auscultate: bilateral breath sounds, heart sounds

**Abdomen**
- Inspect for distension, guarding, bruising (seat belt sign)
- Palpate all four quadrants: tenderness, rigidity, masses
- Pelvis: gentle compression for instability (do not rock)

**Perineum & Genitalia**
- Priapism (spinal cord injury indicator)
- Urethral blood, scrotal bruising

**Extremities**
- Inspect for deformity, swelling, open wounds
- Assess CSM (circulation, sensation, movement) distally
- Pulses: radial, posterior tibial

**Posterior**
- Log roll maintaining spinal alignment
- Inspect spine for tenderness, deformity, step-off
- Buttocks, posterior thighs

**CNS Survey**
- GCS: Eyes, Verbal, Motor (document score and components)
- Pupils: direct and consensual light reflex
- Limb power: grip strength bilaterally, lower limb movement
- Sensation: light touch, pinprick in all limbs
- Reflexes if indicated
- Coordination: finger-nose, heel-shin if patient able

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Secondary survey must not delay definitive care for life threats
- Document all findings with timestamps
- Reassess after every intervention and during transport
- In an unresponsive patient, CNS survey focuses on GCS, pupils, and limb response to pain
- Deterioration in GCS of 2 or more points is clinically significant

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Spinal assessment in the prehospital setting involves determining whether spinal precautions are required based on mechanism, symptoms, and clinical findings. The goal is to avoid unnecessary immobilisation (which carries risks) while not missing a clinically significant spinal injury. Tools such as the NEXUS criteria and Canadian C-Spine Rule guide clinical decision-making.

sections.title: Indications for Spinal Precautions
sections.content:
**Apply spinal precautions if ANY of the following are present:**
- Significant mechanism: high-speed MVC, fall from height, axial loading, diving injury, hanging
- Complaint of neck or back pain or tenderness
- Neurological symptoms: paraesthesia, weakness, numbness in any limb
- Altered level of consciousness (GCS < 15)
- Distracting injury
- Intoxication (alcohol or drugs)
- Inability to communicate reliably
- Pre-existing spinal pathology (e.g. ankylosing spondylitis, rheumatoid arthritis, prior spinal surgery)

**Spinal precautions may not be required if ALL of the following are met (NEXUS Low-Risk Criteria):**
- No midline cervical tenderness
- No altered alertness
- No evidence of intoxication
- No distracting injury
- No focal neurological deficit

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
**1. Scene Assessment**
- Determine mechanism of injury
- Identify forces involved (direction, magnitude)

**2. Primary Survey**
- Maintain manual in-line stabilisation if spinal injury suspected during airway management

**3. Clinical Assessment**
- Ask about neck/back pain, tenderness, numbness, tingling, weakness
- Assess GCS and level of alertness
- Palpate posterior midline cervical and thoracolumbar spine for tenderness or step-off
- Assess for distracting injuries
- Assess neurological function: limb movement, grip strength, sensation

**4. Decision**
- If spinal precautions indicated: apply semi-rigid cervical collar (if appropriate) and maintain spinal alignment throughout transport
- If clinically cleared per NEXUS/protocol: document reasoning clearly

**5. Immobilisation**
- Maintain neutral spinal alignment with appropriate padding
- Use head blocks to limit lateral movement
- Minimise time on extrication board; transfer to trolley as soon as practical
- Consider patient comfort and airway management needs throughout

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Routine immobilisation of all trauma patients is not evidence-based
- Semi-rigid collar alone does not adequately immobilise the cervical spine
- Patients with penetrating neck injury generally do not require cervical immobilisation
- Cooperative, ambulant patients without neurological symptoms may self-extricate short distances with coaching
- Document all assessment findings and clinical reasoning for any decision to apply or withhold spinal precautions

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Assessment of pulse and respirations are fundamental vital signs that provide essential information about cardiovascular and respiratory status. Accurate measurement guides clinical decision-making and helps detect deterioration early.

sections.title: Pulse Assessment
sections.content:
**Technique**
- Use fingertips (index and middle fingers) - never the thumb (has its own pulse)
- Common sites: radial (wrist), carotid (neck), brachial (antecubital fossa), femoral (groin), dorsalis pedis (foot)
- In cardiac arrest: check carotid or femoral pulse
- Count for 60 seconds (or 30 seconds × 2 for regular rhythms)

**Assess:**
- **Rate**: beats per minute
- **Rhythm**: regular, regularly irregular, irregularly irregular
- **Character**: strong/bounding, normal, weak/thready

**Normal Adult Ranges:**
- Heart rate: 60–100 bpm
- Tachycardia: > 100 bpm
- Bradycardia: < 60 bpm

**Paediatric Normal Ranges (approximate):**
- Neonate: 120–160 bpm
- Infant (< 1yr): 100–160 bpm
- 1–5 years: 80–130 bpm
- 5–12 years: 70–120 bpm
- > 12 years: 60–100 bpm

sections.title: Respiratory Rate Assessment
sections.content:
**Technique**
- Observe chest rise and fall - ideally without the patient knowing (to avoid voluntary control)
- Count each complete cycle (inspiration + expiration) as one breath
- Count for 60 seconds (or 30 seconds × 2)
- Assess immediately after pulse to maintain unawareness

**Assess:**
- **Rate**: breaths per minute
- **Depth**: normal, shallow, deep
- **Pattern**: regular, Cheyne-Stokes, Kussmaul, Biot's
- **Effort**: accessory muscle use, nasal flaring, intercostal recession, tracheal tug

**Normal Adult Ranges:**
- 12–20 breaths per minute
- Tachypnoea: > 20 bpm
- Bradypnoea: < 12 bpm
- Apnoea: absence of breathing

**Paediatric Normal Ranges (approximate):**
- Neonate: 40–60 bpm
- Infant: 30–50 bpm
- 1–5 years: 20–40 bpm
- 5–12 years: 15–30 bpm
- > 12 years: 12–20 bpm

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Respiratory rate is one of the most sensitive indicators of deterioration - do not omit or estimate
- Irregular pulse (especially irregularly irregular) warrants ECG assessment
- A weak, thready pulse with tachycardia suggests haemodynamic compromise
- Absent distal pulses with preserved central pulses indicates poor perfusion
- Always document pulse and respiratory rate with time

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Pulse oximetry is a non-invasive method of continuously monitoring peripheral oxygen saturation (SpO₂) and heart rate using photoplethysmography. It is a fundamental tool in prehospital monitoring but has important limitations that clinicians must understand.

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
**Equipment**
- Pulse oximeter probe (finger, earlobe, or forehead clip)

**Procedure**
1. Explain the procedure to the patient
2. Ensure the probe site is clean and dry; remove nail polish if applied to fingertip
3. Apply probe to finger (index or middle preferred), earlobe, or forehead
4. Allow 15–30 seconds for reading to stabilise
5. Record SpO₂ value and waveform quality (plethysmograph)
6. Note patient's current oxygen therapy status at time of reading

**Interpretation - Adult Normal:**
- SpO₂ 95–100%: Normal
- SpO₂ 94%: Lower limit of acceptable; consider supplemental oxygen
- SpO₂ 90–93%: Mild hypoxaemia - oxygen indicated
- SpO₂ < 90%: Significant hypoxaemia - urgent intervention required
- SpO₂ < 85%: Severe hypoxaemia - life-threatening

sections.title: Limitations & Pitfalls
sections.content:
**False readings / inaccurate results:**
- Poor peripheral perfusion (shock, hypothermia, vasoconstriction) - low-volume waveform
- Carbon monoxide poisoning - SpO₂ falsely elevated (CO-Hb reads as O₂-Hb)
- Methaemoglobinaemia - SpO₂ tends towards 85% regardless of true saturation
- Nail polish (especially dark colours), acrylic nails
- Motion artefact - use waveform to assess signal quality
- Anaemia - can maintain SpO₂ despite reduced O₂ carrying capacity
- Jaundice - may affect reading
- Ambient light interference

**Important:**
- Always correlate with clinical findings - SpO₂ is ONE parameter, not the whole picture
- A normal SpO₂ does not exclude hypoventilation (use EtCO₂ for this)
- In CO poisoning: treat empirically with high-flow O₂ regardless of SpO₂

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Always document SpO₂ alongside the FiO₂ being delivered (e.g. SpO₂ 98% on 15L NRB)
- A good plethysmograph waveform confirms probe is reading accurately
- Ear lobe or forehead probes are more reliable in low-perfusion states
- In paediatrics: normal SpO₂ ≥ 95% (≥ 94% in neonates with certain cardiac conditions - follow protocol)

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Auscultation is the process of listening to sounds produced by the body, most commonly the lungs and heart, using a stethoscope. In the prehospital setting it is used to assess ventilation, identify abnormal breath sounds, confirm endotracheal tube placement, and assess cardiac rhythm.

sections.title: Technique
sections.content:
**Stethoscope**
- Use the diaphragm (flat side) for high-pitched sounds: breath sounds, bowel sounds, normal heart sounds
- Use the bell (concave side) for low-pitched sounds: heart murmurs, extra heart sounds
- Ensure earpieces are angled forward to match ear canal direction
- Eliminate background noise where possible

**Lung Auscultation**
1. Expose the chest; ask patient to breathe deeply through mouth
2. Auscultate bilaterally - at least 2 sites per side anteriorly (apices, bases)
3. Compare left and right sides at each level
4. Posterior auscultation preferred when accessible

**Breath Sound Landmarks (Anterior)**
- Upper lobes: 2nd intercostal space, mid-clavicular line
- Lower lobes: 4th–5th intercostal space, anterior axillary line

**Heart Auscultation**
- Aortic: 2nd ICS, right sternal border
- Pulmonary: 2nd ICS, left sternal border
- Tricuspid: 4th ICS, left sternal border
- Mitral (Apex): 5th ICS, mid-clavicular line

sections.title: Interpretation of Findings
sections.content:
**Normal Breath Sounds:**
- Vesicular: soft, rustling, heard over most lung fields
- Bronchial: loud, hollow, heard over trachea and mainstem bronchi
- Bronchovesicular: intermediate, heard over main bronchi

**Abnormal Breath Sounds:**
- **Absent / reduced**: pneumothorax, haemothorax, pleural effusion, mucous plug, endobronchial intubation
- **Crackles (rales)**: fine = pulmonary oedema, pneumonia; coarse = secretions, bronchitis
- **Wheezes (rhonchi)**: bronchospasm (asthma, COPD, anaphylaxis) - high-pitched; secretions - low-pitched
- **Stridor**: upper airway obstruction - inspiratory, high-pitched
- **Pleural rub**: pleurisy - leathery, scratching sound

**Confirming ETT Placement:**
- Auscultate epigastrium first (absent gurgling)
- Then bilateral chest (equal breath sounds)
- Combine with waveform capnography for definitive confirmation

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Prehospital environments are noisy - use clinical context alongside auscultation findings
- Unilateral absent breath sounds after trauma = pneumothorax until proven otherwise
- Always auscultate before and after any airway intervention
- Document findings as bilateral equal air entry, or describe specific abnormalities

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Blood glucose monitoring (BGM) is used to measure the concentration of glucose in capillary blood. It is an essential assessment tool in any patient with altered consciousness, known diabetes, or when metabolic disturbance is suspected. Normal fasting blood glucose is 3.9–5.5 mmol/L.

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
**Equipment**
- Glucometer with calibrated test strips (check expiry date)
- Lancet device and lancets
- Alcohol wipe
- Gloves
- Sharps container

**Procedure**
1. Explain the procedure and obtain consent
2. Don PPE (gloves)
3. Select site: fingertip (lateral aspect preferred - less painful), heel in neonates
4. Clean site with alcohol wipe and allow to dry completely
5. Lance the fingertip with a single motion; wipe away the first drop of blood
6. Apply second drop to test strip (do not smear)
7. Read result and record with timestamp
8. Apply pressure to puncture site
9. Dispose of lancet immediately into sharps container

**Interpretation:**
- < 2.8 mmol/L: Severe hypoglycaemia - treat immediately
- 2.8–3.9 mmol/L: Mild–moderate hypoglycaemia - treat
- 3.9–7.8 mmol/L: Normal (fasting / pre-meal)
- 7.9–11.1 mmol/L: Elevated - monitor, assess context
- > 11.1 mmol/L: Hyperglycaemia - assess for DKA/HHS
- > 33.3 mmol/L: Critical - HHS likely; urgent management

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Always check BGL in any patient with altered consciousness, seizure, or unexplained behaviour change
- Cold, poorly perfused, or oedematous fingers may give falsely low readings - use alternative site
- Alcohol not fully dried will falsely lower result
- Glucometers calibrated for plasma give results ~10–15% higher than whole blood readings
- Glucometers are not accurate in patients with severe anaemia or haematocrit abnormalities
- Always treat the patient, not the number - correlate with clinical findings

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Blood pressure (BP) measurement quantifies the force of blood against arterial walls and is a fundamental vital sign. It is expressed as systolic (peak pressure during contraction) over diastolic (pressure during relaxation) in mmHg. BP can be measured manually (auscultatory method) or by automated non-invasive blood pressure (NIBP) device.

sections.title: Procedure - Manual (Auscultatory)
sections.content:
**Equipment**
- Sphygmomanometer (mercury or aneroid)
- Stethoscope
- Cuff sized appropriately: bladder should encircle ≥ 80% of arm

**Procedure**

1. Patient seated or supine; arm at heart level, palm up, elbow slightly flexed

2. Apply cuff to bare upper arm, 2–3 cm above antecubital fossa

3. Palpate radial pulse; inflate cuff until pulse disappears; note pressure, then add 30 mmHg

4. Place stethoscope diaphragm over brachial artery in antecubital fossa

5. Deflate cuff at 2–3 mmHg/sec

6. **Systolic**: first regular Korotkoff sound (tapping)

7. **Diastolic**: sounds become muffled then disappear (Phase V)

8. Deflate fully, remove cuff, record result

**Procedure - Palpatory (field / noisy environments)**

1. Palpate radial pulse

2. Inflate cuff until pulse disappears; deflate slowly

3. Pressure at which pulse returns = systolic BP (palpatory)

4. Note: diastolic BP cannot be determined by this method

5. Palpatory SBP is typically 5–10 mmHg lower than auscultatory

sections.title: Interpretation
sections.content:
**Adult Normal:** Systolic 100–139 mmHg, Diastolic 60–89 mmHg

**Classification:**
- Hypotension: SBP < 90 mmHg (or > 40 mmHg drop from baseline)
- Normal: SBP 90–139 mmHg
- Stage 1 Hypertension: SBP 140–159 / DBP 90–99 mmHg
- Stage 2 Hypertension: SBP ≥ 160 / DBP ≥ 100 mmHg
- Hypertensive Emergency: SBP ≥ 180 with end-organ damage symptoms

**Shock Indicators (Adults):**
- SBP < 90 mmHg - definitive hypotension, aggressive intervention
- SBP 90–100 mmHg - borderline; correlate with clinical signs
- Narrowing pulse pressure (SBP–DBP < 25): early shock indicator

**Paediatric BP targets:**
- Minimum SBP = 70 + (2 × age in years)
- Hypotension = SBP below age-appropriate minimum

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Use correct cuff size - too small gives falsely high readings; too large gives falsely low
- Avoid measuring on affected limb (IV, injury, dialysis fistula, mastectomy side)
- In noisy environments, palpatory method is more reliable than auscultatory
- Bilateral BP difference > 15 mmHg may indicate aortic dissection or vascular injury
- NIBP is unreliable in patients with atrial fibrillation or severe vasoconstriction
- Document arm used and patient position

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Electrocardiography (ECG) records the electrical activity of the heart over time. Prehospital 12-lead ECG acquisition is a key intervention for time-sensitive cardiac conditions, particularly STEMI, and has been shown to reduce door-to-balloon times. Continuous 3- or 4-lead monitoring is used for rhythm assessment and arrhythmia detection.

sections.title: Electrode Placement
sections.content:
**Limb Leads (4 electrodes)**
- RA (Right Arm): right wrist or right shoulder/upper arm
- LA (Left Arm): left wrist or left shoulder/upper arm
- RL (Right Leg): right ankle or right lower abdomen / hip
- LL (Left Leg): left ankle or left lower abdomen / hip

**Precordial Leads (6 electrodes) - 12-Lead**
- V1: 4th ICS, right sternal border
- V2: 4th ICS, left sternal border
- V3: Between V2 and V4
- V4: 5th ICS, mid-clavicular line
- V5: Anterior axillary line, horizontal to V4
- V6: Mid-axillary line, horizontal to V4 and V5

**Right-Sided Leads (RV infarct screening)**
- V3R–V6R: Mirror image of left precordial leads on right chest
- Indicated when: inferior STEMI (II, III, aVF elevation), bradycardia, hypotension

**Posterior Leads (posterior STEMI)**
- V7: Posterior axillary line
- V8: Tip of left scapula
- V9: Left paraspinal region
- Indicated when: ST depression in V1–V3 (may represent posterior STEMI)

**MCL1 (Modified Chest Lead 1 - continuous monitoring)**
- Positive electrode: 4th ICS, right sternal border (V1 position)
- Negative electrode: left shoulder / outer left clavicle
- Ground electrode: right shoulder / outer right clavicle
- Use: single-lead continuous rhythm monitoring when a full 12-lead is not in place; resembles V1 and is optimal for P wave identification, bundle branch block morphology, and differentiating supraventricular from ventricular rhythms

sections.title: Acquisition & Troubleshooting
sections.content:
**For quality 12-lead:**
1. Prepare skin: clip chest hair if necessary; dry skin; use abrasive pad if poor contact
2. Apply electrodes to correct anatomical positions
3. Ensure patient is still and warm - shivering causes artefact
4. Enter patient demographics before acquisition if time permits
5. Acquire tracing; assess quality before transmitting or printing
6. Record time of acquisition

**Artefact Sources:**
- Patient movement or shivering: muscle/motion artefact - baseline wander
- Loose electrode or poor contact: wandering baseline, flat line on one lead
- Electrical interference (60 Hz): regular fine oscillations
- Reversed limb leads: inverted P waves and QRS in Lead I; check aVR

**STEMI Criteria (for cath lab activation):**
- ST elevation ≥ 1 mm in ≥ 2 contiguous limb leads
- ST elevation ≥ 2 mm in ≥ 2 contiguous precordial leads (V1–V4)
- New LBBB with clinical presentation consistent with ACS
- Posterior changes: ST depression V1–V3 + tall R waves (posterior STEMI)

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- A 12-lead ECG should be acquired and transmitted within 10 minutes of patient contact in suspected ACS
- Electrode placement errors are the most common cause of diagnostic inaccuracy
- Always print or transmit with patient ID, time, and clinical context
- Serial ECGs are essential - initial ECG may be normal in evolving STEMI
- Reciprocal changes (ST depression in opposite leads) increase diagnostic confidence for STEMI

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Waveform capnography continuously measures the concentration of carbon dioxide (CO₂) in exhaled breath. It is the gold standard for confirming and monitoring endotracheal tube (ETT) placement and provides real-time data on ventilation, perfusion, and metabolic status. EtCO₂ reflects CO₂ produced by metabolism, transported by the circulation, and excreted by the lungs.

sections.title: Application & Setup
sections.content:
**Methods:**
- **Mainstream**: sensor positioned directly in the airway circuit (ETT, BVM with adapter)
- **Sidestream**: small sample of exhaled gas aspirated via nasal cannula or inline adapter - usable in non-intubated patients

**For intubated patients:**
1. Connect inline EtCO₂ adapter between ETT and ventilation circuit
2. Confirm waveform: should show characteristic rectangular capnogram with Phase I (baseline), Phase II (expiratory upstroke), Phase III (alveolar plateau), Phase 0 (inspiratory downstroke)
3. Note EtCO₂ value (normal: 35–45 mmHg / 4.6–6.0 kPa)

**For non-intubated patients (nasal cannula):**
1. Apply EtCO₂ nasal cannula
2. EtCO₂ values may be 2–5 mmHg lower than in intubated patients due to air dilution
3. Use to assess respiratory rate and trend rather than absolute values

sections.title: Waveform Interpretation
sections.content:
**Normal Capnogram:** Rectangular waveform with a flat alveolar plateau

**Normal EtCO₂:** 35–45 mmHg (4.6–6.0 kPa)

**Abnormal Findings:**
- **No waveform after intubation**: oesophageal intubation - reintubate immediately
- **Gradually decreasing EtCO₂**: hyperventilation, hypothermia, reducing cardiac output, pulmonary embolism
- **Suddenly absent waveform (intubated)**: ETT displacement, circuit disconnect, cardiac arrest
- **Elevated EtCO₂ (> 45 mmHg)**: hypoventilation, increased metabolism, CO₂ rebreathing
- **Shark fin waveform (oblique Phase III)**: bronchospasm - asthma, COPD
- **Cleft in Phase III**: spontaneous breathing efforts during positive pressure ventilation
- **Rising EtCO₂ during CPR**: indicates improving ROSC - consider pulse check
- **EtCO₂ < 10 mmHg during CPR**: poor prognosis; reassess CPR quality and reversible causes

**ROSC Indicator:** Sudden sustained rise in EtCO₂ ≥ 35 mmHg during CPR = likely ROSC

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Waveform capnography is the gold standard for ETT confirmation - use in addition to clinical assessment
- CO poisoning: EtCO₂ may be low despite elevated carboxyhaemoglobin
- Target EtCO₂ in TBI: 35–40 mmHg - avoid hyper or hypoventilation
- In CPR: aim for chest compressions that generate EtCO₂ ≥ 20 mmHg
- Always document EtCO₂ value and waveform quality at time of reading

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: The Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) is a standardised neurological assessment tool used to objectively measure and communicate a patient's level of consciousness. It assesses three components: Eye opening, Verbal response, and Motor response. Scores range from 3 (no response) to 15 (fully alert and oriented). It should be used in conjunction with pupil assessment and a clinical neurological examination.

sections.title: Scoring
sections.content:
**Eye Opening (E) - max 4**
- 4: Spontaneous - eyes open without stimulation
- 3: To voice - eyes open in response to verbal command
- 2: To pain - eyes open to painful stimulus
- 1: None - no eye opening
- C: Eyes closed due to swelling (document as C, not scored)

**Verbal Response (V) - max 5**
- 5: Oriented - knows person, place, date
- 4: Confused - sentences but disoriented
- 3: Words - intelligible words but not sentences
- 2: Sounds - incomprehensible sounds (moaning, groaning)
- 1: None - no verbal response
- T: Intubated / tracheostomy (document as T, not scored)

**Motor Response (M) - max 6**
- 6: Obeys commands - follows 2-step command
- 5: Localises pain - moves purposefully toward pain source
- 4: Withdraws - pulls away from pain in normal flexion
- 3: Abnormal flexion (Decorticate) - flexion at elbow, wrist; adduction
- 2: Extension (Decerebrate) - extension at elbow, wrist; internal rotation
- 1: None - no motor response

**Total GCS = E + V + M (3–15)**
- 13–15: Mild impairment
- 9–12: Moderate impairment
- ≤ 8: Severe impairment - consider airway protection
- 3: Deepest measurable unconsciousness

sections.title: Assessment Technique
sections.content:
**Applying Painful Stimuli (if needed)**
- Central stimulus (for motor): sternal rub, trapezius pinch, supraorbital ridge pressure
- Peripheral stimulus (for localisation): nail-bed pressure using pen
- Use minimal force necessary; document site used

**Considerations:**
- Assess before giving sedative or analgesic medications where possible
- Intoxication, hypoglycaemia, hypothermia, and postictal state all affect GCS - document contributing factors
- AVPU is a rapid screening tool; GCS provides more precise and reproducible detail
- Serial GCS measurements are more valuable than a single score - document with timestamps
- Deterioration of 2 or more GCS points is clinically significant

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Always document component scores (e.g. E3V4M5) not just the total
- GCS ≤ 8: patient unable to protect airway - consider advanced airway management
- In paediatric patients: use Paediatric GCS (modified verbal scoring for pre-verbal children)
- GCS is not valid immediately post-seizure (postictal state) - reassess after recovery
- Pupils are not part of the GCS but must always be assessed alongside it

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Pain is a subjective, multidimensional experience and is the most common presenting complaint in the prehospital setting. Systematic pain assessment guides analgesic decisions, helps establish a therapeutic relationship, and provides a baseline for monitoring treatment response. Pain should be assessed and documented at every contact.

sections.title: Assessment Tools
sections.content:
**Numeric Rating Scale (NRS) - Adults and children ≥ 8 years**
- Ask: 'On a scale of 0 to 10, where 0 is no pain and 10 is the worst pain you can imagine, what is your pain right now?'
- 0: No pain
- 1–3: Mild
- 4–6: Moderate
- 7–10: Severe

**Wong-Baker FACES - Children 3–8 years**
- Six faces ranging from smiling (0) to crying (10)
- Patient points to face that best represents their pain

**Behavioural Pain Scale - Non-verbal / unconscious patients**
- Observe: facial grimacing, vocalisation (moaning/crying), body posturing, movement
- Use FLACC scale (Face, Legs, Activity, Cry, Consolability) for children

**OLDCARTS - Characterising Pain**
- **O**nset: when did it start? sudden or gradual?
- **L**ocation: where is it? does it radiate?
- **D**uration: how long has it been present?
- **C**haracter: sharp, dull, burning, crushing, cramping, stabbing, aching?
- **A**ggravating factors: what makes it worse?
- **R**elieving factors: what makes it better?
- **T**reatment: what has been tried? did it help?
- **S**everity: current score and worst/best score

sections.title: Reassessment & Documentation
sections.content:
- Reassess pain score after every analgesic intervention: 5–10 minutes post-administration
- Clinically meaningful improvement = reduction of 2 or more points on NRS
- Document: initial score, interventions given (drug, dose, route, time), reassessment score
- Pain that is not responding to appropriate analgesia warrants clinical review
- Consider non-pharmacological measures: positioning, immobilisation, ice, reassurance, distraction (especially in paediatrics)

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Pain is subjective - accept the patient's self-report as valid
- Culture, age, anxiety, and prior experiences influence pain expression
- Adequate analgesia does not mask signs of surgical abdomen or serious injury in the prehospital setting
- Untreated pain causes physiological stress, worsens outcomes, and erodes trust
- Document pain scores with timestamps at assessment, post-intervention, and on handover

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Tympanic temperature measurement uses infrared technology to measure the temperature of the tympanic membrane, which reflects core body temperature via the carotid artery blood supply. It is the preferred method in the prehospital setting due to speed, ease of use, and non-invasiveness. Normal core body temperature is 36.1–37.2°C.

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
**Equipment**
- Tympanic thermometer
- Probe cover (new cover for each patient)

**Procedure**
1. Attach a new, clean probe cover
2. For adults and children > 1 year: gently pull the pinna upward and backward to straighten the ear canal
3. For infants < 1 year: pull pinna gently downward and backward
4. Insert the probe snugly into the ear canal, directed toward the tympanic membrane (angled toward the nose/eye)
5. Press the measurement button and hold until the device beeps
6. Read and document the result and time
7. Remove probe cover and dispose

**Interpretation:**
- < 35.0°C: Hypothermia (mild 32–35°C, moderate 28–32°C, severe < 28°C)
- 35.0–36.0°C: Sub-normal - monitor and warm
- 36.1–37.2°C: Normal
- 37.3–38.0°C: Low-grade fever
- 38.1–39.0°C: Fever
- 39.1–40.0°C: High fever - antipyretics and cooling
- > 40.0°C: Hyperpyrexia - urgent management
- > 41.0°C: Life-threatening - heat stroke or extreme infection

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Ensure correct insertion angle - pointing at TM, not the ear canal wall
- Recent outdoor cold exposure, ear wax accumulation, or otitis media can affect accuracy
- In suspected hypothermia, tympanic temperature may underestimate severity - use rectal or oesophageal probe if available
- Always use a fresh probe cover for infection control
- Bilateral readings may differ by up to 0.5°C - use the higher reading
- In febrile children, temperature must be interpreted alongside clinical signs - treatment threshold is clinical, not numeric

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Rapid Antigen Tests (RATs) are point-of-care diagnostic tests that detect specific antigens - most commonly for SARS-CoV-2 (COVID-19) and influenza A/B - in minutes using nasopharyngeal, nasal, or throat swabs. In the prehospital setting, RATs inform PPE decisions, infection control measures, transport destination planning, and clinical management.

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
**Equipment**
- RAT kit (check expiry date and storage conditions)
- PPE: gloves, mask, eye protection (standard precautions minimum)
- Appropriate swab as per kit instructions (nasal, throat, or combined)

**Procedure (Nasal Swab - most common):**
1. Don appropriate PPE
2. Explain procedure to patient; obtain consent
3. Remove swab from sealed packaging
4. Insert swab approximately 2–3 cm into one nostril
5. Rotate swab 5 times against the nasal wall; repeat in second nostril with same swab
6. Remove swab, insert into extraction tube and rotate 5 times while pressing against tube walls
7. Squeeze out liquid from swab before withdrawing
8. Apply 3–4 drops to the test device window as directed
9. Read result at the time specified by the manufacturer (typically 15 minutes)
10. Do not read after the specified time window

**Result Interpretation:**
- **Positive (2 lines - C and T)**: Antigen detected - treat as positive, apply appropriate infection control
- **Negative (1 line - C only)**: Antigen not detected - does not exclude infection; correlate with clinical presentation
- **Invalid (no C line or no lines)**: Test failed - repeat with new kit

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Sensitivity of RATs is lower than PCR - a negative RAT does not exclude infection
- Positive RAT has high specificity - treat as confirmed in symptomatic patients
- A negative RAT in a patient with high clinical suspicion should be managed as if positive until confirmed otherwise
- Follow local health authority guidelines for mandatory reporting of positive results
- Dispose of all test materials as per biohazard/infectious waste protocols
- Document result, time, and kit lot number in patient record

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Functional status assessment evaluates a patient's ability to perform activities of daily living (ADLs) and instrumental activities of daily living (IADLs) at their baseline. In the prehospital setting, it provides essential context for interpreting clinical findings, guides resuscitation goals discussion, informs transport decisions, and helps identify acute deterioration from baseline.

sections.title: Assessment Domains
sections.content:
**Basic ADLs (physical self-care):**
- Mobility: walking unaided, use of walking aid, wheelchair dependent, bed-bound
- Transfers: independent, requires assistance, hoisted
- Continence: continent, uses pads, catheterised, incontinent
- Feeding: independent, requires assistance
- Bathing/grooming: independent, requires assistance
- Dressing: independent, requires assistance

**Instrumental ADLs (complex daily activities):**
- Meal preparation, shopping, housework
- Medication management: takes independently, supervised, administered by carer
- Financial management, telephone use
- Driving / use of transport

**Cognitive Baseline:**
- Lives alone or with carer?
- Known dementia or cognitive impairment?
- Usual orientation and communication ability
- Any recent change in cognitive function

**Frailty Screening (Clinical Frailty Scale):**
- 1–2: Fit to mildly vulnerable
- 3–4: Managing well to vulnerable (some dependence)
- 5–6: Mildly to moderately frail
- 7–8: Severely frail to very severely frail
- 9: Terminally ill

**Current vs Baseline Comparison:**
- Key question: 'How does this compare to how they normally function?'
- Acute decline from baseline is clinically significant regardless of absolute score

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Functional status provides essential context for frail, elderly, and chronically ill patients
- A previously independent patient who is now bed-bound indicates significant acute illness
- Functional status informs resuscitation decisions - document baseline carefully
- Collateral history from family, carers, or residential facility staff is often invaluable
- Advance care directives, DNAR orders, or resuscitation plans should be identified and documented
- The Clinical Frailty Scale score should be documented for all patients > 65 years or those with chronic illness

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Bag valve mask (BVM) ventilation delivers positive-pressure breaths to patients who are apnoeic or have inadequate respiratory effort. It is a fundamental airway skill used in resuscitation and pre-intubation management. Effective BVM ventilation requires a patent airway, a good mask seal, and appropriate tidal volume.

sections.title: Equipment
sections.content:
- BVM device (adult: 1500 mL bag; paediatric: 500 mL bag)
- Appropriate mask size (fits bridge of nose to chin cleft)
- Oxygen supply and tubing
- Oropharyngeal (OPA) or nasopharyngeal (NPA) airway adjunct
- Suction available

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
**Single-rescuer technique:**

1. Position patient supine; open airway with head-tilt chin-lift (or jaw thrust if spinal precautions)

2. Insert OPA/NPA if tolerated

3. Select correct mask size

4. Apply mask using C-E grip: thumb and index finger form 'C' over mask; remaining three fingers form 'E' under mandible to lift jaw

5. Maintain seal while squeezing bag with other hand

6. Deliver breath over 1 second - enough to produce visible chest rise

7. Rate: 10–12/min adult (1 breath every 5–6 seconds); 12–20/min paediatric

**Two-rescuer technique (preferred):**

1. Rescuer 1: applies mask with both hands using two-thumb technique for superior seal

2. Rescuer 2: squeezes bag

**Tidal volume:** ~500–600 mL adult (approximately half to two-thirds bag squeeze)

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Avoid over-ventilation - excessive tidal volume causes gastric insufflation and aspiration risk
- Attach oxygen at 15 L/min with reservoir bag for FiO₂ ~0.85
- If chest does not rise: reposition head, reassess seal, check for obstruction, suction
- Consider 2-person technique whenever possible - significantly improves tidal volume delivery
- During CPR: 30:2 ratio (unsynchronised) or 1 breath per 6 seconds with continuous compressions (advanced airway in place)

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Non-invasive positive pressure ventilation (NIPPV) delivers positive airway pressure via a tight-fitting mask without endotracheal intubation. Prehospital CPAP (Continuous Positive Airway Pressure) and BiPAP (Bilevel Positive Airway Pressure) improve oxygenation and reduce work of breathing in cardiogenic pulmonary oedema, severe asthma, and COPD exacerbations.

sections.title: Indications & Contraindications
sections.content:
**Indications:**
- Acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema (ACPO) - strong evidence
- Acute COPD exacerbation with type 2 respiratory failure
- Severe asthma (not responding to bronchodilators)
- Hypoxic respiratory failure (SpO₂ < 90% despite high-flow O₂)

**Contraindications:**
- Apnoea or respiratory arrest (use BVM)
- Unable to protect airway / GCS ≤ 8
- Vomiting / high aspiration risk
- Facial trauma precluding mask fit
- Undrained tension pneumothorax
- Haemodynamic instability (relative)

sections.title: Setup & Application
sections.content:
**CPAP setup:**

1. Select appropriate mask size (full-face preferred for better seal)

2. Explain procedure to patient - cooperation is essential

3. Set CPAP pressure: start at 5–7.5 cmH₂O; titrate up to 10–12.5 cmH₂O if needed

4. Connect oxygen (typically 15 L/min or per device flow requirement)

5. Apply mask with patient holding initially, then secure harness

6. Monitor: SpO₂, respiratory rate, work of breathing, patient tolerance

7. Titrate to SpO₂ ≥ 94% and reduced work of breathing

**BiPAP typical settings (per local protocol):**
- IPAP: 10–15 cmH₂O
- EPAP: 4–5 cmH₂O

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Early application improves outcomes in ACPO - do not delay
- Reassess every 5–10 minutes; deterioration may require RSI
- Gastric insufflation is a risk - have suction immediately available
- Remove mask briefly if patient vomits
- Document: initial SpO₂, RR, work of breathing, pressure used, and response

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Supplemental oxygen corrects or prevents hypoxaemia. The appropriate delivery device and flow rate depends on the patient's clinical condition, target SpO₂, and the FiO₂ required. Oxygen is a medication and should be titrated to target SpO₂ - routine high-flow oxygen is not appropriate for all patients.

sections.title: Delivery Devices & FiO₂
sections.content:
**Nasal Cannula (NC):**
- Flow: 1–6 L/min
- FiO₂: ~24–44%
- Use: mild hypoxaemia, patient comfort, chronic respiratory conditions

**Simple Face Mask:**
- Flow: 5–10 L/min (minimum 5 L/min to prevent CO₂ rebreathing)
- FiO₂: ~35–50%
- Use: moderate hypoxaemia

**Non-Rebreather Mask (NRB):**
- Flow: 10–15 L/min
- FiO₂: ~60–90%
- Use: severe hypoxaemia, CO poisoning, anaphylaxis, major trauma

**Venturi Mask:**
- Fixed FiO₂: 24%, 28%, 31%, 35%, 40%, 60%
- Use: COPD, when precise FiO₂ titration is required

**BVM with O₂ reservoir:**
- Flow: 15 L/min
- FiO₂: ~85%
- Use: apnoea, pre-oxygenation for intubation

sections.title: Target SpO₂
sections.content:
**General target: SpO₂ 94–98%**

**COPD / chronic hypercapnia: SpO₂ 88–92%** - high-flow oxygen can suppress hypoxic drive and worsen hypercapnia

**Carbon monoxide poisoning: 100% O₂ (NRB or BVM) regardless of SpO₂** - SpO₂ is falsely normal due to CO-Hb

**Post-ROSC: SpO₂ 94–98%** - avoid hyperoxia which worsens neurological outcomes

**Neonates: SpO₂ 91–95%** - hyperoxia causes retinopathy

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Titrate to target - do not apply maximum flow by default
- SpO₂ probe unreliable in: poor perfusion, hypothermia, nail polish, CO poisoning, methaemoglobinaemia
- Document: device, flow rate, initial and post-treatment SpO₂
- Humidification for flows > 4 L/min if prolonged use

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: High-quality chest compressions are the cornerstone of resuscitation. They generate approximately 25–30% of normal cardiac output and maintain cerebral and myocardial perfusion during cardiac arrest. Minimising interruptions to compressions is one of the most important modifiable factors in survival.

sections.title: Adult Technique
sections.content:
**Position:**

1. Patient supine on firm surface

2. Kneel beside patient at chest level

3. Place heel of hand on lower half of sternum (centre of chest)

4. Place second hand on top; interlace fingers; keep fingers off chest

**Compressions:**

- Depth: 5–6 cm
- Rate: 100–120/min
- Full chest recoil between compressions - do not lean
- Compression:ventilation ratio: 30:2 (BLS); continuous compressions with 1 breath/6 sec (advanced airway)
- Minimise interruptions - pause < 5 seconds for any intervention

sections.title: Paediatric Technique
sections.content:
**Infant (< 1 year):**
- Two-finger technique (single rescuer) or two-thumb encircling technique (two rescuers)
- Depth: 4 cm (approximately ⅓ AP diameter)
- Rate: 100–120/min
- Ratio: 15:2 (two rescuers) or 30:2 (single rescuer)

**Child (1 year to puberty):**
- One or two-hand technique depending on size
- Depth: 5 cm (approximately ⅓ AP diameter)
- Rate: 100–120/min
- Ratio: 15:2 (two rescuers)

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Rotate compressor every 2 minutes to maintain quality
- Use feedback device if available (metronome, CPR prompt)
- Do not stop compressions to check rhythm unless defibrillator is charged and ready
- Consider mechanical CPR device for prolonged resuscitation or during transport
- CPR fraction > 80% is associated with improved survival

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Defibrillation delivers a high-energy unsynchronised electrical shock to terminate ventricular fibrillation (VF) or pulseless ventricular tachycardia (pVT). Early defibrillation is one of the strongest predictors of survival from cardiac arrest - survival decreases approximately 10% per minute without defibrillation.

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
**AED (Automated External Defibrillator):**

1. Power on AED

2. Attach pads: right - upper right chest (below clavicle); left - left mid-axillary line (V6 position / apex)

3. Follow voice/visual prompts - stand clear during analysis

4. If shock advised: ensure all clear, press shock button

5. Immediately resume CPR for 2 minutes before next rhythm check

**Manual Defibrillator:**

1. Confirm shockable rhythm (VF/pVT)

2. Apply pads in same positions (or paddles with conductive gel)

3. Select energy: biphasic 150–200 J (first shock); 150–360 J (subsequent) - per device recommendation

4. Charge while CPR continues

5. Clear - confirm all clear verbally and visually

6. Deliver shock

7. Immediately resume CPR - do not pause to check rhythm

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Pre-charge defibrillator before rhythm check to minimise pause
- Do not shock in water or near flammable gases
- Remove transdermal patches before pad placement
- Implanted pacemaker/ICD: position pads ≥ 8 cm from device
- Consider anteroposterior pad placement for obesity or poor response

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: The modified Valsalva manoeuvre (REVERT technique) is a first-line vagal manoeuvre for haemodynamically stable supraventricular tachycardia (SVT). It increases vagal tone via reflex baroreceptor activation, slowing AV nodal conduction and terminating re-entrant SVT. The modified technique (with leg raise) doubles conversion rates compared to standard Valsalva.

sections.title: Procedure (REVERT Method)
sections.content:
**Indications:** Haemodynamically stable regular narrow-complex tachycardia (presumed SVT)

**Contraindications:** Haemodynamic instability, suspected MI, carotid artery disease

1. Patient semi-recumbent (45°)

2. Instruct patient to blow hard into 10 mL syringe for 15 seconds (generating 40 mmHg strain) - plunger should be difficult to move

3. Immediately lay patient flat and passively raise legs to 45° for 15 seconds (to increase venous return and augment the reflex)

4. Return to semi-recumbent position

5. Monitor ECG continuously - conversion typically occurs within 30–60 seconds

6. If unsuccessful after 1 attempt: repeat once, then proceed to adenosine per protocol

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- REVERT trial (2015) showed 43% conversion rate vs 17% for standard Valsalva
- Document: pre- and post-manoeuvre ECG strip
- Do not use carotid sinus massage prehospital without experienced supervision
- If haemodynamically unstable: proceed directly to synchronised cardioversion

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Pressure Immobilisation Technique (PIT) is the recommended first aid for Australian elapid snake bites (taipan, brown snake, tiger snake, death adder) and funnel-web spider bites. It slows lymphatic venom absorption by combining firm pressure bandaging with rigid limb immobilisation, delaying systemic envenomation.

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
**Indications:** Suspected elapid snake bite; funnel-web spider bite
**Not indicated:** Red-back spider, marine envenomation, bee/wasp stings, or if bite site is torso/face/neck

1. Keep patient calm and still - movement accelerates venom absorption

2. Apply firm crepe bandage directly over bite site (do not remove clothing; cut away if necessary)

3. Extend bandage as far up the limb as possible (entire limb)

4. Bandage should be firm (similar to a sprain bandage) - you should not be able to slide a finger under it easily, but it should not cause pain or impair arterial flow

5. Immobilise the limb with a splint or sling (rigid immobilisation is critical)

6. Keep patient supine and do not allow walking

7. Do NOT: wash the bite site (venom identification), cut or suck the wound, apply tourniquet

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Do not remove bandages until patient is in a hospital with antivenom and resuscitation facilities
- Mark the bite site on the bandage with a pen
- Venom swab from bite site can be used for identification kit - do not wash
- Transport supine with limb elevated if tolerated

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Transcutaneous pacing (TCP) delivers electrical impulses through the chest wall via pacing pads to stimulate cardiac contraction. It is indicated for haemodynamically significant bradycardia unresponsive to atropine, complete heart block, or symptomatic bradycardia awaiting transvenous pacing.

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
**Indications:** Haemodynamically unstable bradycardia, complete heart block, atropine-refractory symptomatic bradycardia

1. Apply pacing pads: anterior - left precordium (V3–V4 position); posterior - left infrascapular region

2. Connect to defibrillator/pacing unit in pacing mode

3. Set rate: 60–80 bpm (or higher per clinical need)

4. Start output at minimum (0 mA); increase in 5–10 mA increments

5. Identify electrical capture: pacing spike followed by broad QRS on monitor

6. Confirm mechanical capture: palpate femoral pulse coinciding with each QRS (radial pulse unreliable due to artefact)

7. Set output at 10% above capture threshold

8. Provide analgesia/sedation - pacing is painful (muscle contractions at 50–90 mA typical)

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Electrical capture without mechanical capture = pseudo-capture - always confirm by palpating femoral pulse
- Sedate/analgese patient if conscious - consider ketamine or midazolam per protocol
- Concurrent pharmacological therapy (atropine, adrenaline infusion) may be needed
- Capture threshold typically 50–100 mA

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Synchronised cardioversion delivers an electrical shock timed to the R-wave of the QRS complex to avoid delivery during the relative refractory period (T-wave), which could induce VF. It is used for haemodynamically unstable tachyarrhythmias with a pulse.

sections.title: Indications & Contraindications
sections.content:
**Indications (haemodynamically unstable with pulse):**
- Atrial fibrillation / flutter
- SVT refractory to vagal manoeuvres and adenosine
- VT with pulse

**Contraindications:**
- Pulseless (use unsynchronised defibrillation)
- Digoxin toxicity (relative - risk of VF)
- Multifocal atrial tachycardia (will not cardiovert)

**Signs of haemodynamic instability:** altered conscious state, chest pain, acute heart failure, hypotension (SBP < 90 mmHg)

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
1. Confirm haemodynamic instability - if stable, pharmacological cardioversion preferred

2. Sedate if time permits and patient is conscious - midazolam ± ketamine per protocol

3. Apply pads (same positions as defibrillation)

4. Confirm synchronise mode is active (should see sync marker on R-waves)

5. Energy selection:
   - AF/flutter: 100–200 J biphasic
   - SVT / regular narrow complex: 50–100 J
   - VT with pulse: 100–200 J

6. Charge; confirm all clear

7. Deliver shock (device may delay briefly to synchronise with R-wave)

8. Reassess rhythm and haemodynamic status

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- After each shock the monitor may revert to unsynchronised mode - reactivate sync before each subsequent shock
- Document: pre- and post-shock rhythm strip, energy used, number of shocks, patient response
- If patient loses pulse: switch to unsynchronised defibrillation immediately
- Anticoagulation considerations for AF > 48 hours duration - hospital decision, not prehospital

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: The adrenaline autoinjector (EpiPen) delivers a fixed IM dose of adrenaline for the emergency treatment of severe allergic reactions and anaphylaxis. It is designed for rapid self-administration or administration by a bystander/clinician. EpiPen Jr (0.15 mg) for 15–30 kg; EpiPen (0.3 mg) for ≥ 30 kg.

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
1. Remove EpiPen from carrier; grip in dominant hand (fingers away from both ends)

2. Remove blue safety cap (pull straight off - do not bend)

3. Hold orange tip pointing downward

4. With a smooth, swift motion, press orange tip firmly against outer mid-thigh (can be through clothing)

5. Hold firmly in place for 3 seconds (until click heard and window turns orange)

6. Remove and massage injection site for 10 seconds

7. Note time of administration

8. A second dose may be given after 5–15 minutes if no improvement

9. Dispose of used device in sharps container (needle remains exposed after use)

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Outer mid-thigh is preferred - faster absorption than deltoid in anaphylaxis
- Call for emergency help immediately - EpiPen buys time, it does not replace definitive care
- Patient should be transported supine (or semi-recumbent if dyspnoeic) - do not allow standing
- If accidentally injected into finger/hand: vasospasm risk - seek medical review
- Repeat dose if symptoms recur or do not improve within 5–15 minutes

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Intramuscular injection delivers medication directly into muscle tissue for absorption into the systemic circulation. IM injection is used when IV access is unavailable, when rapid absorption is required, or for specific medications (e.g. adrenaline, morphine, ketamine, glucagon).

sections.title: Injection Sites
sections.content:
**Deltoid (preferred for most IM medications in adults):**
- Locate by placing 3 fingers below acromion process
- Inject into the triangle formed below - avoid deltoid if < 1 mL volume
- Maximum volume: 1–2 mL

**Vastus Lateralis (anterolateral thigh - preferred for adrenaline and paediatrics):**
- Outer third of anterolateral thigh, middle third of the thigh length
- Maximum volume: 3–5 mL adult; 1–3 mL child
- Preferred for adrenaline in anaphylaxis

**Ventrogluteal (adults only):**
- Place hand on greater trochanter, index finger on ASIS, middle finger pointing to iliac crest - inject into the V
- Lower risk of nerve/vessel injury than dorsogluteal

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
1. Confirm medication, dose, route, and patient (5 rights)

2. Draw up medication using appropriate syringe and needle (21–25G, 16–38 mm length depending on patient and site)

3. Clean skin with alcohol swab; allow to dry

4. Spread skin taut (Z-track technique for irritant drugs) or bunch if thin patient

5. Insert needle at 90° (or 45° for deltoid in thin patients) with smooth swift motion

6. Do not aspirate routinely (evidence does not support this for most sites)

7. Inject at steady rate; withdraw smoothly

8. Apply light pressure with gauze - do not rub (disperses medication)

9. Dispose of needle directly into sharps container - do not resheath

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Needle length selection: obese patients require longer needle for muscle penetration
- Adrenaline for anaphylaxis: always anterolateral thigh
- Maximum volumes per site to avoid pain and tissue damage
- Document: drug, dose, route, site, time, lot number

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: The mucosal atomisation device (MAD) delivers medication as a fine mist to the nasal mucosa for rapid systemic absorption, bypassing the need for IV access. Highly vascularised nasal mucosa allows absorption comparable to IM injection for many drugs. Commonly used for fentanyl, midazolam, and naloxone.

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
1. Draw up prescribed dose into syringe; attach MAD atomiser tip

2. Tilt patient's head slightly back

3. Insert MAD nozzle just inside one nostril (1 cm) - point toward the top/outer wall of nasal passage

4. Apply firm, fast plunger pressure to atomise the dose (rapid, not slow push)

5. If volume > 0.5 mL per nostril: split dose between both nostrils to maximise absorption surface area

6. Maximum volume per nostril: 0.5–1 mL

7. Gently press on the nostril for a few seconds after administration

8. Document drug, dose, nostril(s) used, and time

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Absolute maximum per nostril: 1 mL (larger volumes run off and reduce bioavailability)
- If patient is bleeding from nose or has nasal congestion: reduced absorption - consider alternative route
- Onset: fentanyl 5–10 min; midazolam 5–10 min; naloxone 5–10 min
- Highly concentrated solutions are preferred (smaller volume = better absorption)
- Do not use with patients who cannot breathe through nose (blocked, trauma)

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: IV giving sets (administration sets) connect IV fluid bags to an IV cannula for fluid or medication delivery. Standard sets deliver approximately 20 drops/mL (adults); blood giving sets and microdrip sets are also used in prehospital care.

sections.title: Types of Giving Sets
sections.content:
**Standard (macrodrip) giving set:**
- Drop factor: 20 drops/mL
- Use: IV fluid resuscitation, most IV medications

**Microdrip (paediatric) giving set:**
- Drop factor: 60 drops/mL
- Use: precise fluid delivery, paediatric patients, infusions

**Blood administration set:**
- Built-in 170–200 micron filter
- Use: pRBC, FFP, platelets
- Primed with 0.9% NaCl only (not Hartmann's - calcium precipitates)

**Burette (Buretrol):**
- 100–150 mL graduated chamber
- Use: precise paediatric fluid/medication delivery

sections.title: Priming & Setup
sections.content:
1. Check fluid bag for clarity, expiry, and integrity; confirm with prescription

2. Remove giving set from packaging aseptically; close roller clamp

3. Spike the bag: remove bag port protector and spike cover; insert spike firmly with a twisting motion

4. Squeeze drip chamber until half full

5. Open roller clamp and prime tubing until fluid flows continuously (free of air bubbles)

6. Close roller clamp

7. Connect to IV cannula; set drip rate

8. Drip rate formula: drops/min = (Volume mL × drop factor) ÷ time in minutes

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Label all lines with drug name, concentration, date, and time
- Inspect for air in tubing before connecting
- Blood sets: use within 4 hours once opened; do not use with dextrose
- Gravity infusion rate is affected by bag height (higher = faster) and patient position

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: A metered dose inhaler (MDI) with a spacer chamber delivers inhaled medication to the lower airways more effectively than an MDI alone. Using a spacer eliminates the need for precise hand-breath coordination, increases lung deposition, and reduces oral steroid deposition. Used for bronchodilators (salbutamol, ipratropium) in asthma and COPD.

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
**With spacer (preferred):**

1. Shake MDI vigorously for 5 seconds

2. Remove cap from MDI and spacer mouthpiece

3. Insert MDI into back of spacer

4. Ask patient to exhale fully

5. Seal lips around spacer mouthpiece (or apply mask for children/compromised patients)

6. Press MDI once to release one actuation

7. Patient takes 4–5 slow deep breaths through the spacer (5 second breath-hold if possible)

8. Wait 30–60 seconds between actuations

9. Repeat for prescribed number of doses

**Mask attachment (infants/altered consciousness):**
- Attach appropriately sized mask to spacer in place of mouthpiece
- Apply snugly to face; actuate and allow 5–6 tidal breaths per actuation

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- One actuation at a time - do not stack multiple puffs then inhale
- Document: drug, number of actuations, patient response (SpO₂, auscultation, work of breathing)
- Spacer should be cleaned periodically - static charge reduces drug delivery
- For severe asthma: nebuliser is preferred over MDI/spacer when available

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: A nebuliser converts liquid medication into a fine aerosol for inhalation into the lower airways. It is used for bronchodilators in acute asthma, COPD, anaphylaxis with bronchospasm, and for nebulised analgesics. Oxygen-driven nebulisation (6–8 L/min) is standard in most prehospital settings.

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
1. Assemble nebuliser chamber and attach mouthpiece or mask

2. Add medication to nebuliser cup (add normal saline diluent if required to reach 3–5 mL total volume)

3. Connect oxygen tubing at 6–8 L/min (or compressed air if clinically indicated)

4. Confirm aerosol mist is produced

5. Patient sits upright if able and breathes normally through mouthpiece or mask

6. Continue until nebuliser chamber is empty (spluttering indicates near-complete delivery - approximately 10–15 minutes)

7. Reassess: SpO₂, respiratory rate, breath sounds, work of breathing

8. May repeat per protocol

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Oxygen-driven nebulisation preferred for most patients; use air-driven for COPD if at risk of hypercapnia
- Keep patient upright - improves aerosol delivery to lower airways
- Continuous nebulisation may be used in severe asthma under direct clinical supervision
- Document: drug, dose, diluent, flow rate, and response

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Penthrox delivers methoxyflurane - a volatile halogenated analgesic - via a hand-held inhaler for rapid short-term pain relief. Onset of analgesia is within 6–10 breaths. It is self-administered under supervision and is particularly useful in trauma, procedural pain, and during extrication.

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
1. Remove Penthrox from packaging; check device is assembled correctly

2. Add 3 mL of methoxyflurane to the inhaler chamber

3. Insert the activated charcoal absorber (black) into the side hole - reduces exhaled waste gas and clinician exposure

4. Instruct patient: hold device, place mouthpiece in mouth, breathe in and out normally

5. Patient controls their own inhalation rate - do not force deep breaths

6. Cover the hole on the side with a finger to increase concentration; uncover for lower concentration

7. Maximum dose per administration: 6 mL per occasion (2 × 3 mL inhalers)

8. Do not use for more than 15 minutes continuously

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Patient self-administration is the safety mechanism - sedation causes patient to drop device
- Ensure adequate ventilation of the space (ambulance windows open)
- Clinician should not inhale exhaled gas - stand clear of patient's exhalation
- Contraindicated: hepatic impairment, renal impairment, malignant hyperthermia susceptibility, MSTeP history
- Onset 6–10 breaths; peak effect 2–3 minutes; wears off rapidly on cessation

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Peripheral intravenous (IV) cannulation provides vascular access for fluid and medication administration. Prehospital IV access should not delay scene time for time-critical conditions (e.g. STEMI, trauma); it should be established en route when possible.

sections.title: Site Selection
sections.content:
**Preferred sites (proximal to distal):**
- Antecubital fossa (cephalic, basilic, median cubital veins) - large bore access, rapid infusion
- Forearm (cephalic, basilic)
- Dorsum of hand
- External jugular (for difficult access or cardiac arrest)

**Avoid:**
- Injured limb, limb with dialysis fistula, post-mastectomy limb
- Over joints (increases dislodgement risk)
- Lower extremities (risk of thrombophlebitis)

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
1. Gather equipment: appropriate cannula (14–22G), tourniquet, alcohol swab, dressing, flush

2. Apply tourniquet 10–15 cm above site; ask patient to clench fist

3. Visualise and/or palpate vein; clean with alcohol swab and allow to dry

4. Bevel up, advance at 10–30° until flashback of blood

5. Lower angle slightly; advance cannula 1–2 mm further to ensure tip is in lumen

6. Advance plastic cannula fully while withdrawing needle

7. Release tourniquet; apply finger pressure proximal to tip; remove stylet

8. Attach extension set or cap; flush with 5–10 mL 0.9% NaCl

9. Secure with transparent dressing; label with date/time/gauge

10. Dispose of needle immediately in sharps container

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Use largest bore cannula the vein can accommodate in high-acuity patients (14–18G for resuscitation)
- Two large-bore cannulae in antecubital fossa provide equivalent flow to a central line
- Confirm patency with flush before administering medication
- Maximum 2 attempts before reassessing site or calling for assistance

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Prehospital blood collection allows early laboratory processing, reducing time to diagnosis on arrival at hospital. Samples are typically collected during IV cannulation to avoid a second venepuncture. Common panels include FBC, UEC, coagulation, blood culture, lactate, and crossmatch.

sections.title: Common Blood Tubes
sections.content:
**Order of draw (to prevent additive cross-contamination):**
1. Yellow/gold (SST - Serum Separator): biochemistry, LFTs, troponin
2. Blue (Citrate): coagulation, INR, APTT
3. Green (Lithium Heparin): urgent biochemistry, ionised calcium
4. Purple/lavender (EDTA): FBC, crossmatch
5. Pink (EDTA for blood bank): group & crossmatch (separate tube)
6. Grey (Fluoride Oxalate): glucose, lactate

**Common prehospital request:** FBC, UEC, coagulation, crossmatch, troponin, lactate

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
1. Collect during IV cannulation when possible (wastes less blood, one venepuncture)

2. Prior to connecting giving set: attach blood tube holder to cannula hub

3. Insert tubes in correct order of draw

4. Fill each tube to fill line - invert additive tubes gently 8–10 times to mix

5. Label tubes immediately at bedside with: patient name, DOB, date, time, collector ID

6. Store per local protocol (room temperature or refrigerated depending on test)

7. Document sample collection in patient record

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Mislabelling is the most common and serious error in phlebotomy
- Do not collect from same limb as IV infusion (dilutes sample)
- Haemolysis (from excessive vacuum, vigorous mixing, or difficult draw) invalidates potassium, LDH, and some other results
- Crossmatch samples must be labelled at the bedside - never away from the patient

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: External jugular (EJ) venous access is an alternative IV route when peripheral veins are inaccessible. The EJ vein is visible in the neck and provides a large, accessible vein particularly useful in cardiac arrest, shock, or obese patients with difficult peripheral access.

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
1. Position patient supine (or Trendelenburg 15°) - distends EJ vein

2. Turn patient's head away from insertion site

3. Identify EJ vein: runs from angle of jaw to clavicle midpoint

4. Apply gentle finger occlusion just above the clavicle to distend the vein

5. Clean skin with alcohol swab

6. Insert cannula (14–16G preferred) bevel up, aimed toward ipsilateral shoulder, at 15–30°

7. Advance on flashback; thread cannula; release occlusion

8. Connect and flush; secure firmly (EJ cannulae dislodge easily with head movement)

9. Note: position may affect flow - flush during patient movement

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Line may kink when patient turns head - acceptable for short-term access
- Intraosseous access is preferred over EJ when rapid high-flow resuscitation is needed
- Caution in suspected cervical spine injury - limit neck manipulation
- May not be accessible with cervical collar in place

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Intraosseous (IO) access provides rapid vascular access via the medullary cavity of bone when IV access cannot be established. It can be used in all ages and for all resuscitation drugs and fluids. The EZ-IO drill is the most commonly used device in prehospital settings.

sections.title: Sites
sections.content:
**Proximal tibia (first choice - all ages):**
- 2 cm below tibial tuberosity on anteromedial flat surface
- Needle length: 15 mm (paediatric/lean adult), 25 mm (standard adult), 45 mm (obese/large)

**Proximal humerus (adults - useful in arrest with CPR in progress):**
- Greater tubercle: 2 cm above surgical neck on anterolateral surface
- Patient arm adducted, hand on abdomen
- Higher flow rates than tibia

**Distal tibia:**
- 3 cm proximal to medial malleolus
- Alternative when proximal tibia is inaccessible

**Distal femur (paediatric):**
- 1–3 cm above the lateral femoral condyle

sections.title: Procedure (EZ-IO)
sections.content:
1. Identify site; cleanse with antiseptic

2. Select appropriate needle length (must penetrate cortex and enter marrow)

3. Prime EZ-IO: attach needle set to driver; position tip at insertion site

4. Apply firm downward pressure; engage power driver until decreased resistance (marrow entry)

5. Remove stylet with counterclockwise rotation; attach primed extension set

6. Confirm placement: needle is firm in bone, does not rock; 10 mL NaCl flushes without resistance or extravasation

7. Aspirate to confirm marrow (not always achieved - absence does not confirm misplacement)

8. Secure with EZ-IO stabiliser

9. If conscious: flush with 2% lignocaine (40 mg) 2 minutes before first medication

10. For drugs: administer followed by 10 mL NaCl flush and elevate limb

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- All IV drugs and fluids can be given IO - same doses as IV
