- Fluids require pressure bag or syringe to infuse against bone marrow pressure
- Maximum dwell time: 24 hours (hospital to establish definitive IV)
- Contraindicated: fracture proximal to insertion site, previous IO in same site within 24h, infection at site, osteogenesis imperfecta
- Document: site, needle size, ease of insertion, flush confirmation

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: The Combat Application Tourniquet (CAT) is the most widely used military and prehospital tourniquet for life-threatening limb haemorrhage. Early tourniquet application before haemorrhagic shock occurs is associated with significantly improved survival.

sections.title: Application
sections.content:
**Self-application / one-handed:**

1. Route limb through loop; position 5–7 cm above wound (or as high as possible for junctional injuries)

2. Pull free end of self-adhering band tight; secure

3. Twist windlass until bleeding stops (typically 3–5 turns)

4. Lock windlass in clip; secure tail strap over windlass

5. Note time of application on white label on windlass clip

**Two-handed application:**

1. Open CAT; pass limb through loop

2. Position 5–7 cm above wound

3. Tighten band by pulling free end; secure

4. Twist windlass until bleeding stops; lock and secure

5. Mark time

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Apply tight enough to stop bleeding - partial occlusion worsens blood loss and increases pain without benefit
- Effective tourniquet is painful - reassure patient
- Do not remove prehospital - hospital will assess and manage
- Mark time of application on tourniquet and in patient record
- Maximum recommended application time before ischaemic injury risk increases: up to 2 hours (survival benefit outweighs limb risk)

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Eye irrigation is indicated for chemical exposure (acid or alkali), foreign body removal, and thermal injury to the eye. Alkali burns are more severe than acid burns due to liquefactive necrosis - prolonged irrigation is critical. Copious, continuous irrigation with normal saline or water is the primary treatment.

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
1. Ensure personal protective equipment (gloves, eye protection for irrigant splash)

2. Remove contact lenses if present (chemical irrigation will damage lenses and impair flushing)

3. Position patient supine or semi-recumbent, head turned slightly toward affected eye

4. Hold eyelids open (Morgan lens or IV giving set for continuous irrigation; alternatively use gloved fingers)

5. Direct a continuous steady stream of normal saline from inner canthus to outer canthus

6. Chemical irrigation: minimum 1 litre (alkali) to 30 minutes continuous irrigation; reassess pH with litmus paper - target pH 7

7. Foreign body: irrigate to flush out loose particles; do not attempt to remove embedded foreign body

8. Pad eye after irrigation if required for comfort

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Do not delay irrigation for triage - begin immediately at scene for chemical exposure
- Alkali burns (cement, lime, bleach) require far longer irrigation than acid burns
- Never apply pressure to a suspected open globe injury (ruptured globe)
- Document: substance, time of exposure, duration of irrigation, post-irrigation visual acuity if assessable

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Finger thoracostomy is a definitive emergency procedure to decompress a tension pneumothorax in intubated or unconscious patients. It creates an open hole in the chest wall through blunt dissection. It is preferred over needle thoracocentesis in intubated trauma patients as it is more reliable and does not occlude.

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
**Indications:** Suspected tension pneumothorax in a patient who is intubated, unconscious, or needle decompression has failed

1. Identify insertion site: 4th or 5th ICS, anterior axillary line (same as chest drain site)

2. Prepare: gloves, scalpel (10 blade), curved haemostat

3. Make a 2–3 cm incision through skin and subcutaneous tissue directly over the superior aspect of the rib (avoid neurovascular bundle inferior to rib)

4. Use curved haemostat to blunt-dissect through intercostal muscles into the pleural space

5. A rush of air confirms correct placement and diagnosis

6. Insert finger to confirm pleural space entry and sweep to clear clot or tissue

7. Do not close - leave open; cover with non-occlusive dressing for transport

8. Proceed to formal chest drain insertion at hospital

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Superior to rib edge prevents damage to intercostal neurovascular bundle
- Bilateral thoracostomies may be required in bilateral tension pneumothorax (consider after traumatic arrest)
- May be indicated prior to intubation to prevent tension during positive pressure ventilation
- Document: side, time, clinical indication, air released, patient response

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Fractures and dislocations require appropriate immobilisation to prevent further injury, reduce pain, limit blood loss, and facilitate transport. Prehospital management focuses on neurovascular assessment, analgesia, and splinting in the position of comfort or anatomical alignment.

sections.title: Assessment
sections.content:
**Assess the 6 Ps before and after splinting:**
- Pain
- Pallor
- Paraesthesia (numbness/tingling)
- Paralysis (inability to move)
- Pulselessness
- Poikilothermia (cold limb)

**Circulation check:** palpate distal pulse; compare to contralateral limb; capillary refill

sections.title: Management
sections.content:
**General principles:**

1. Expose and inspect limb - document deformity, open wounds, skin integrity, neurovascular status

2. Provide analgesia prior to manipulation where feasible (methoxyflurane, fentanyl, ketamine)

3. Cover open fractures with moist sterile dressing - do not attempt reduction prehospital (except neurovascular compromise)

4. Realign only if: absent distal pulse, severe angulation preventing splinting, or prolonged extrication

5. Splint in position found or corrected anatomical alignment - from joint above to joint below

6. Pad bony prominences

7. Reassess neurovascular status after splinting

8. Elevate if possible; apply ice pack if available (wrapped)

**Common splinting techniques:**
- SAM splint / vacuum splint for long bones
- Box splint for ankle/foot
- Traction splint for isolated midshaft femur fracture

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Femur fracture can accommodate 1–2 L blood loss - consider haemorrhagic shock
- Pelvic fracture: do not log roll; apply pelvic binder
- Shoulder dislocation: immobilise in position of comfort - do not attempt reduction prehospital unless isolated and prolonged scene time
- Document: mechanism, neurovascular status pre/post, splinting type used

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Helmet removal may be required to access the airway or assess an unconscious trauma patient. It requires a two-person technique to maintain cervical spine immobilisation throughout the procedure. Helmets should NOT be removed if: the patient is conscious with no airway compromise, the helmet fits snugly, or removal would cause further injury.

sections.title: Two-Person Technique
sections.content:
**When to remove:** Airway compromise, respiratory arrest, cardiac arrest, inability to maintain airway
**When to leave:** Helmet fits well, conscious patient, no airway compromise (immobilise with helmet on)

1. Rescuer 1 (head): kneel at head; stabilise cervical spine by placing hands on each side of helmet, thumbs on mandible, fingers on occiput

2. Rescuer 2 (chin): kneel beside patient; unfasten or cut chin strap; place one hand on mandible (thumb on one side, fingers on other); place other hand at occiput, taking over cervical spine control

3. Rescuer 1: expand sides of helmet slightly to clear ears; tilt helmet backward to clear nose, then forward and backward off head in a rocking motion; remove slowly

4. Rescuer 1: immediately takes over cervical spine control

5. Apply cervical collar once airway is accessible

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Communication between rescuers is essential throughout
- Remove at the pace of the slowest step - do not rush
- Pad behind head after removal to maintain neutral alignment (helmet elevates occiput)
- Full-face helmet: visor must be raised or removed before starting

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Lateral patellar dislocation is the most common direction of patella dislocation, with the patella displacing laterally. Reduction is often achieved with minimal intervention and is within prehospital scope when neurovascular compromise is present or when transport time is prolonged.

sections.title: Reduction Technique
sections.content:
**Assessment first:** confirm lateral displacement of patella, neurovascular status of limb

**Milch / extension technique:**

1. Provide analgesia (methoxyflurane, fentanyl, or Entonox)

2. Patient supine; extend knee fully while gently applying medial pressure to the lateral edge of the patella

3. As the knee extends, the quadriceps relaxes and the patella often relocates spontaneously

4. A click or clunk is felt/heard on reduction

5. Assess neurovascular status post-reduction

6. Apply splint in extension for transport; ice if available

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Adequate analgesia improves success and reduces patient distress
- Do not attempt reduction if: suspected fracture on palpation, mechanism inconsistent with simple dislocation
- Document: pre- and post-reduction neurovascular status, number of attempts, analgesia used

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Epistaxis (nosebleed) is most commonly anterior (Little's area - Kiesselbach's plexus) and self-limiting. Posterior epistaxis is less common but more serious. Minor injuries include abrasions, lacerations, and contusions that do not require surgical intervention.

sections.title: Epistaxis Management
sections.content:
**Anterior epistaxis:**

1. Patient sitting upright, leaning forward (prevents swallowing blood)

2. Pinch the soft part of the nose (not the bony bridge) firmly

3. Apply continuous pressure for 10–15 minutes

4. Apply ice pack to bridge of nose

5. Do not blow nose after bleeding stops

**Persistent / posterior epistaxis:**
- Nasal packing with ribbon gauze or epistaxis balloon (per scope)
- Consider tranexamic acid (topical or systemic) per local protocol
- Supine with airway monitoring if posterior bleed suspected

sections.title: Minor Wound Management
sections.content:
1. Apply direct pressure with sterile dressing

2. Irrigate wound with normal saline under pressure

3. Remove gross contamination with saline-soaked gauze

4. Apply appropriate dressing

5. Assess for retained foreign body, tendon or vessel injury

6. Refer for formal wound closure if gaping, deep, or complex

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Consider anticoagulation / antiplatelet medication as a contributing factor
- Posterior bleeds: blood visible in oropharynx, bilateral bleeding, failed anterior packing
- Facial fracture: do not pack blindly - refer early
- Document: site, duration, interventions, and clinical response

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Minor wound management aims to control bleeding, reduce infection risk, and prepare the wound for definitive closure. Key principles are haemostasis, thorough irrigation, and appropriate dressing. Tetanus prophylaxis should be considered for all open wounds.

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
1. Apply direct pressure with sterile gauze to control haemorrhage

2. Expose wound; remove gross contamination

3. Irrigate copiously with normal saline under pressure (10–20 mL syringe + 18G IV catheter) - reduces infection risk

4. Assess: wound depth, neurovascular status distal, tendon visibility, foreign body risk

5. Apply appropriate dressing: non-adherent primary layer; absorbent secondary; bandage

6. Steri-strips may be applied to small superficial lacerations for temporary closure

7. Elevate if a limb wound

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Do not close contaminated wounds prehospital - risk of abscess
- Bites (human, animal): high infection risk; irrigate thoroughly; do not close
- Scalp wounds bleed profusely - direct pressure effective; rarely dangerous in isolation
- Document: mechanism, wound site and dimensions, neurovascular status, interventions

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Needle thoracocentesis is an emergency procedure to decompress a life-threatening tension pneumothorax. It involves inserting a large-bore needle/cannula into the pleural space to release trapped air and relieve mediastinal shift. It is a temporising measure - finger thoracostomy is preferred in intubated patients.

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
**Clinical diagnosis of tension pneumothorax:**
- Haemodynamically compromised + respiratory distress
- Absent or reduced breath sounds unilaterally
- Tracheal deviation (late sign)
- Distended neck veins (may be absent in hypovolaemia)
- Mechanism consistent with pneumothorax

**Site 1 - 2nd ICS, MCL (traditional):**

1. Identify: 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line

2. Insert 14G (adult) or 16G (child) IV cannula over superior border of 3rd rib

3. Rush of air confirms diagnosis

4. Remove needle, leave cannula open (may apply flutter valve - Heimlich valve or glove finger)

**Site 2 - 4th/5th ICS, AAL (preferred in obese/muscular patients):**
- Same ICS as finger thoracostomy - better reliability

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Needle decompression may fail due to chest wall thickness - finger thoracostomy is more reliable
- Monitor for re-tension - cannula can kink or clot
- Do NOT wait for all classic signs - haemodynamic compromise + unilateral absent breath sounds in trauma is sufficient
- Document: side, site, clinical findings, air released, patient response

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Pelvic binders are applied to unstable pelvic fractures to reduce the pelvic volume and tamponade venous and arterial haemorrhage. They are indicated for suspected open-book (AP compression) and vertical shear pelvic fractures following significant trauma with haemodynamic compromise.

sections.title: Application (SAM Pelvic Sling / Commercial Binder)
sections.content:
**Contraindications:** Lateral compression fractures (may worsen with binder)

1. Assess pelvis gently once only - do not repeatedly rock or compress

2. Position binder centre over greater trochanters (NOT over iliac crests)

3. Slide binder under patient at level of greater trochanters

4. Close binder and apply tension per device instructions - adequate tension is firm, not maximal

5. Tie/lock per device design

6. Reassess haemodynamic status and leg length discrepancy

7. Do not log roll patient with suspected unstable pelvic fracture

**Improvised sheet binder:**

- Fold sheet to 30 cm width; position at greater trochanter level; twist to tighten and tie securely

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Greater trochanter landmark - NOT over iliac crest
- Apply early - do not wait for hypotension
- Internally rotate feet and tape together after binder application
- Document: time of application, haemodynamic status before and after

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: QuikClot Combat Gauze is a kaolin-impregnated haemostatic gauze for junctional and compressible haemorrhage not amenable to tourniquet application (groin, axilla, neck, perineum). Kaolin activates the intrinsic clotting cascade and accelerates haemostasis.

sections.title: Procedure (Wound Packing)
sections.content:
1. Identify wound and control gross haemorrhage with initial direct pressure

2. Remove packaging; identify the radiopaque marker strip (ensures visibility on X-ray)

3. If possible, identify the bleeding point within the wound

4. Pack gauze directly into wound, starting at the point of haemorrhage - not superficially

5. Use your fingers to pack as deeply as possible, layering gauze into the wound

6. Continue packing until wound is fully filled

7. Apply firm direct pressure over the packed wound for minimum 3 minutes

8. If bleeding persists through packing: add more gauze on top; do not remove initial gauze

9. Apply pressure bandage over packed wound to maintain compression

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Superficial packing does not work - gauze must reach the bleeding source
- 3 minutes of direct pressure minimum - inform the patient and maintain counting
- Leave packing in situ for hospital removal
- Document: wound site, number of gauze pads used, duration of pressure, bleeding control achieved

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: The log roll is a controlled coordinated technique to turn a patient while maintaining spinal alignment. It is used for posterior examination, patient transfer, placement of a long spinal board or vacuum mattress, and management of patients found prone.

sections.title: Procedure (3+ Rescuers)
sections.content:
1. Rescuer 1 (team lead - at head): manually immobilises cervical spine in neutral alignment; directs the roll

2. Rescuer 2 and 3 (and 4 if available): position beside patient

3. Rescuer 2: controls near shoulder and hip; Rescuer 3: controls hip and lower leg

4. Extend the arm away from roll direction along patient's side

5. On team lead command: roll patient in one smooth motion toward rescuers, maintaining spinal alignment

6. Inspect back for wounds, deformity, and haematoma

7. Place board or equipment as needed

8. On command: roll back to supine

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Team leader at head controls timing - do not roll until commanded
- Minimum 3 rescuers for log roll; 4 for obese patients or femur fracture
- Do not log roll suspected unstable pelvic fractures - scoop or lateral transfer instead
- Document: findings on posterior inspection

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: A cervical collar limits flexion, extension, and lateral rotation of the cervical spine. It is used as an adjunct to manual stabilisation when spinal precautions are indicated. A collar alone does not fully immobilise the cervical spine - manual stabilisation remains essential until full immobilisation is achieved.

sections.title: Sizing & Application
sections.content:
**Sizing (Stifneck / equivalent):**

1. Measure from top of shoulder to chin with neck in neutral position

2. Match measurement to collar size markings

3. Select: no-neck (for large neck), short, regular, tall

**Application:**

1. Rescuer 1 maintains manual cervical stabilisation in neutral alignment

2. Rescuer 2 fits collar: slide posterior piece behind neck (or position front piece first per brand instructions)

3. Secure chin cup under chin; fasten velcro firmly

4. Ensure airway is not compromised and collar is not too tight

5. Maintain manual stabilisation until full immobilisation is achieved (vacuum mattress / LBB)

6. Reassess neurological status after application

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Collar does NOT replace manual immobilisation
- Never apply collar if: patient resists or has muscle spasm (force increases injury risk)
- Remove if: patient vomits, airway needs management, causes distress that worsens outcome
- Document: indications, collar size, patient tolerance and any neurological changes

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: The vacuum mattress conforms to the patient's body shape and provides full-body immobilisation superior to a long spinal board for suspected spinal injuries. On suction, polystyrene beads pack together creating a rigid custom-fitted shell. Preferred over long board for primary immobilisation of suspected spinal injury.

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
1. Position deflated mattress flat; spread beads evenly

2. Log roll or scoop patient onto mattress in neutral supine position

3. Mould mattress up around sides of patient and under head/neck while maintaining cervical stabilisation

4. Apply suction valve; pump until mattress is rigid

5. Check: mattress is firm throughout; patient is fully supported in neutral alignment

6. Apply straps: chest, hips, lower legs

7. Reassess neurological status

8. Transfer to stretcher - minimum 4 rescuers for lift

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- If mattress loses rigidity during transport: re-apply suction immediately
- Carry pump in ambulance at all times
- Adjust head positioning to maintain neutral alignment - pad if required
- Avoid prolonged immobilisation of conscious patient if possible - pressure injury risk

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Spinal immobilisation is indicated when clinical assessment suggests a risk of unstable spinal injury. Contemporary evidence supports selective immobilisation using validated clinical decision tools rather than universal immobilisation. When indicated, the goal is to maintain spinal alignment and prevent secondary cord injury during extrication and transport.

sections.title: Indications
sections.content:
Spinal precautions indicated when NEXUS or Canadian C-Spine criteria are not cleared:
- Altered level of consciousness (GCS < 15) following trauma
- Midline spinal tenderness or pain on palpation
- Distracting injury
- Focal neurological deficit
- Intoxication
- Mechanism: high-energy trauma, axial load, diving/submersion

sections.title: Full Immobilisation Package
sections.content:
1. Manual cervical stabilisation maintained throughout

2. Apply appropriately sized cervical collar

3. Extricate using suitable technique (log roll, scoop stretcher, Kendrick Extrication Device)

4. Position on vacuum mattress (preferred) or long spinal board

5. Apply head blocks / head immobiliser on vacuum mattress or board

6. Secure straps: chest, hips, lower extremities

7. Reassess neurological status before and after immobilisation

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Do not delay extrication for a conscious, time-critical patient to apply full immobilisation
- Vacuum mattress preferred over hard board - reduces pain and pressure injury
- Consider patient's ability to protect own airway when supine - may need head-of-bed elevation
- Document: NEXUS/Canadian C-Spine assessment result, neurological status, immobilisation device used

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: The lateral trauma position (modified recovery position) is used to maintain an open airway and prevent aspiration in an unconscious or obtunded trauma patient when spinal injury has been cleared or is considered unlikely. It is preferable to supine positioning when the patient cannot protect their airway.

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
1. Confirm spinal precautions are not required (or cannot be maintained with supine positioning due to airway risk)

2. Kneel beside patient; extend near arm at right angles to body

3. Bring far arm across chest; place patient's hand against their near cheek

4. Flex far knee; use this as a lever to roll patient toward you in a controlled manner

5. Adjust top knee and arm to stabilise position

6. Tilt head back slightly to maintain open airway; mouth facing down for drainage

7. Monitor airway continuously; have suction ready

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Airway patency takes precedence over spinal precautions in the unconscious patient - 'airway first'
- Left lateral preferred for late pregnancy (relieves aortocaval compression)
- Reassess neurological status and airway position frequently
- Document: position used, reason for lateral over supine, GCS, and airway management

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Wound closure reduces bleeding, protects from contamination, and promotes healing. Prehospital suturing is appropriate for clean, simple lacerations where transport time is prolonged. Steri-strips (adhesive wound closure strips) are suitable for superficial, low-tension lacerations.

sections.title: Steri-Strip Application
sections.content:
1. Ensure wound is clean and haemostasis achieved; dry wound edges

2. Approximate wound edges manually

3. Apply Steri-strip across wound: half on each side perpendicular to the wound

4. Start at centre; work toward each end alternating sides

5. Apply parallel strips 3–4 mm apart

6. Apply bridging strip along wound edges for extra support if required

sections.title: Simple Interrupted Suturing
sections.content:
1. Achieve haemostasis; irrigate wound; consider local anaesthetic (lignocaine 1%)

2. Select appropriate suture: 4-0 nylon for face; 3-0 nylon for scalp/body; 2-0 for high tension

3. Load needle driver with needle; grasp at 2/3 from tip

4. Enter skin 3–5 mm from wound edge at 90°; pass through to opposite side equidistant

5. Tie square knot: instrument tie technique (3 throws: 2 forward + 1 reverse)

6. Cut tails 4–5 mm from knot

7. Space sutures: 4–5 mm apart for face; 8–10 mm for scalp/body

8. Document: site, number of sutures, suture material used, date

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Do not close: contaminated wounds, bites, wounds > 6–8 hours old
- Always irrigate before closure
- Deep sutures for deep tissue tension before skin closure
- Remove sutures: face 3–5 days; scalp 7–10 days; body 7–14 days

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: The Fernotrac (Thomas splint variant) is a traction splint that applies countertraction to reduce pain, restore length, and reduce haematoma in isolated closed midshaft femur fractures. Traction splints are contraindicated for hip, knee, ankle, open femur fractures, and avulsion injuries.

sections.title: Application
sections.content:
**Indications:** Isolated closed midshaft femur fracture, no contraindications
**Contraindications:** Open fracture, hip/pelvis fracture, knee/ankle injury, avulsion

1. Provide analgesia before application

2. Measure splint against uninjured leg: ischial tuberosity to 15 cm beyond heel

3. Apply ischial strap snugly at proximal thigh

4. Apply ankle strap or traction hitch to foot

5. Extend ratchet/traction mechanism until patient reports pain relief - approximately 10% of body weight

6. Apply support straps along length of thigh

7. Reassess neurovascular status

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Traction should reduce not worsen pain - if pain increases, reassess application and confirm diagnosis
- Do not apply more than 15 kg traction
- Remove and reassess on hospital arrival
- Document: pre/post neurovascular status, traction force applied

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: The Kendrick Traction Splint (KTS) is a compact, lightweight traction splint used for isolated midshaft femur fractures. Its compact folding design makes it suitable for confined-space rescue and air medical transport.

sections.title: Application
sections.content:
1. Provide analgesia

2. Unfold and extend KTS to appropriate length (measured against uninjured leg)

3. Position ischial pad at ischial tuberosity; secure proximal strap

4. Apply ankle strap around foot/ankle

5. Connect traction cord to ankle strap

6. Apply gentle, steady traction by tightening ratchet or winch mechanism until pain is reduced

7. Secure support straps along thigh

8. Reassess neurovascular status

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Same indications and contraindications as Fernotrac
- Preferred in confined spaces and air transport due to compact design
- Reassess traction during patient movement

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: The Sager traction splint is positioned medially and can be used for bilateral femur fractures (unlike most unilateral splints). It applies countertraction via a perineal strap against the ischium.

sections.title: Application
sections.content:
1. Provide analgesia

2. Position Sager along medial aspect of injured leg; ischial pad against ischial tuberosity

3. Secure thigh strap

4. Apply ankle strap

5. Extend traction bar and apply traction via ankle coupling until pain relief - generally 10% of body weight, maximum 15 kg

6. Secure calf and thigh straps

7. Reassess neurovascular status

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Bilateral application possible with a single Sager splint (unique to Sager design)
- Reassess traction and neurovascular status frequently during transport
- Same contraindications as other traction splints

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: The Slishman Traction Splint (STS) is an ultra-compact device that fits in a cargo pocket. It uses a cam-lock traction mechanism and proximal attachment to provide traction for midshaft femur fractures, particularly in tactical and austere environments.

sections.title: Application
sections.content:
1. Provide analgesia

2. Attach proximal cuff just distal to greater trochanter (NOT groin)

3. Extend splint along lateral aspect of leg

4. Apply ankle hitch

5. Engage cam-lock mechanism; apply traction to pain relief

6. Secure intermediate straps

7. Reassess neurovascular status

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Ultra-compact - ideal for tactical/TCCC environments
- Same contraindications as all traction splints
- Reassess during transport

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Direct pressure is the most fundamental haemorrhage control technique. Combined with trauma/pressure bandages (e.g. Israeli Emergency Bandage, rolled gauze), it provides sustained compressive force to control compressible external haemorrhage in areas where tourniquets cannot be applied.

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
**Direct pressure:**

1. Apply sterile dressing or clean cloth directly to wound

2. Apply firm, continuous pressure with flat hand and body weight

3. Maintain for minimum 5–10 minutes without lifting to check - lifting disrupts clot formation

**Israeli Emergency Bandage (Pressure Bandage):**

1. Place non-woven pad directly over wound

2. Wrap elastic bandage around limb/body part with moderate tension

3. Thread elastic through pressure bar applicator; pull back over bandage to create pressure point

4. Continue wrapping and secure with closure clip

**Wound packing (junctional / deep wounds):**

- Pack gauze into wound starting at bleeding source
- Apply firm pressure with fist for 3 minutes
- Secure with bandage

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Do not remove first dressing if soaked - add additional dressing on top
- Pelvic and abdominal wounds: direct pressure plus wound packing; transport immediately
- Haemostatic gauze preferred over standard gauze for junctional wounds
- Document: estimated blood loss, interventions, time of application

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Traumatic procedural analgesia provides pain relief prior to and during painful prehospital procedures including splinting, reduction, extrication, and wound management. Multimodal analgesia combining different classes reduces doses and side effects. Adequate analgesia improves patient cooperation, reduces physiological stress response, and facilitates better procedure outcomes.

sections.title: Analgesic Options
sections.content:
**Methoxyflurane (Penthrox):**
- Onset: 6–10 breaths
- Duration: 25–30 min per 3 mL
- Use: mild–moderate procedural pain; self-administered

**Fentanyl (IN / IV / IM):**
- Onset: IN 5–10 min; IV 2–3 min
- Dose: 1–2 mcg/kg IV/IN; 2 mcg/kg IM
- Use: moderate pain; adjunct for procedures

**Ketamine (sub-dissociative):**
- Dose: 0.1–0.3 mg/kg IV; 0.5–1 mg/kg IM
- Onset: IV 1–2 min; IM 5–10 min
- Use: moderate–severe pain; maintains airway and haemodynamics; synergistic with opioids
- Monitor: emergence reaction - can be reduced with midazolam

**Methoxyflurane + ketamine (combination):**
- Effective for extrication analgesia

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Perform a pain score before and after every analgesic administration
- Ketamine preserves respiratory drive and blood pressure - preferred in haemodynamically unstable patients
- Multimodal approach (non-opioid + opioid) reduces opioid requirements
- Document: agent, dose, route, time, VAS/NRS pain score before and after

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Prehospital childbirth occurs when delivery is imminent and transport to hospital is not possible. The paramedic's role is to facilitate a safe delivery, manage complications, and provide immediate neonatal care. Most normal deliveries require calm reassurance and support rather than active intervention.

sections.title: Stages of Labour
sections.content:
**Stage 1:** Onset of regular contractions to full cervical dilation (10 cm)
- Early: contractions every 5–10 min, 30–45 sec duration
- Active: contractions every 2–3 min, 60 sec duration
- Transition: frequent intense contractions; imminent delivery

**Stage 2:** Full dilation to delivery of baby
- Urge to push; perineal bulging; crowning

**Stage 3:** Delivery of placenta (5–30 minutes after birth)

**Imminent delivery signs:** urge to push, crowning visible, uncontrolled bearing down, extreme anxiety

sections.title: Delivery Procedure
sections.content:
1. Position mother semi-recumbent or left lateral; maintain privacy

2. Prepare: gloves, sterile drapes, cord clamps (×2), scissors, bulb syringe, warm blanket for neonate, oxytocin

3. As head crowns: apply gentle counter-pressure (do not forcibly hold back) with one hand to prevent explosive delivery

4. Support head as it delivers - typically face down, then rotates

5. Check for nuchal cord: feel for cord around neck; if present: slip loop over head or double-clamp and cut if tight

6. Support head downward to deliver anterior shoulder; then upward for posterior shoulder

7. Support body as it delivers; hold securely (neonates are slippery)

8. Note time of delivery

9. Place baby on mother's abdomen, dry thoroughly with warm towel, skin-to-skin

10. Clamp cord at 2 cm and 5 cm from neonatal umbilicus; cut between clamps (delay clamping 1–3 min if baby is vigorous)

11. Administer oxytocin per protocol after placental delivery

12. Deliver placenta with gentle controlled cord traction once signs of separation (cord lengthens, uterus firms)

sections.title: Neonatal Assessment (APGAR)
sections.content:
**APGAR Score** (at 1 and 5 minutes):
- Appearance (skin colour): 0 = blue/pale; 1 = blue extremities; 2 = pink
- Pulse: 0 = absent; 1 = < 100; 2 = ≥ 100
- Grimace (reflex): 0 = none; 1 = grimace; 2 = cry/cough
- Activity (muscle tone): 0 = limp; 1 = some flexion; 2 = active
- Respiration: 0 = absent; 1 = irregular; 2 = strong cry

**Score 7–10:** Normal - routine care
**Score 4–6:** Moderate depression - stimulate, supplemental O₂
**Score 0–3:** Severe depression - begin neonatal resuscitation

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Do not pull on the head - guide, do not force
- If placenta does not deliver within 30 minutes: transport without waiting
- Estimate blood loss accurately - > 500 mL PPH
- Keep baby warm - hypothermia is a major risk for neonates
- Document: time of delivery, APGAR scores, cord clamping time, complications

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Shoulder dystocia occurs when the baby's anterior shoulder becomes impacted behind the maternal pubic symphysis after delivery of the head. It is an obstetric emergency requiring immediate manoeuvres - the HELPERR mnemonic guides management. Cord compression with shoulder dystocia can cause foetal hypoxia within 4–5 minutes.

sections.title: HELPERR Manoeuvres
sections.content:
**H - Call for Help**

**E - Evaluate for Episiotomy** (if available in scope; does not resolve bony dystocia but improves access)

**L - Legs (McRoberts Manoeuvre):** Hyperflexion of maternal thighs onto abdomen - flattens lumbar lordosis, increases AP pelvic diameter; most effective first step

**P - Suprapubic Pressure:** Assistant applies firm downward pressure above pubic symphysis toward the foetal face to dislodge the anterior shoulder; do NOT press on fundus

**E - Enter (Internal Rotational Manoeuvres):**
- Rubin II: pressure on posterior aspect of anterior shoulder toward foetal chest
- Woods Screw: pressure on anterior aspect of posterior shoulder
- Combination: Rubin + Woods (corkscrew manoeuvre)

**R - Remove the Posterior Arm:** Sweep posterior arm across foetal chest and deliver it

**R - Roll the patient** (Gaskin Manoeuvre): Roll mother onto hands and knees - gravity and position change may free shoulder

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Do NOT apply fundal pressure - worsens impaction
- Do NOT pull on the head or attempt rotation by neck - risk of brachial plexus injury
- McRoberts + suprapubic pressure resolve majority of cases
- Document: time of head delivery, manoeuvres used and sequence, time of full delivery, neonatal condition

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Breech presentation (buttocks or feet presenting first) occurs in approximately 3–4% of term deliveries. Prehospital breech delivery requires a hands-off approach until the body is delivered to the umbilicus, then active assistance for head delivery. The key danger is head entrapment.

sections.title: Management
sections.content:
**If delivery is not imminent:** transport immediately - do not attempt to convert to cephalic

**If delivery is imminent:**

1. Position mother semi-recumbent with buttocks at edge of surface

2. Hands off until baby delivered to umbilicus - allow passive descent

3. Once at umbilicus: loop of cord - pull down gently to create slack

4. Allow arms to deliver spontaneously; if arms are extended: sweep one arm across chest at a time (Lovset's manoeuvre)

5. **Head delivery (Mauriceau-Smellie-Veit):**
   - Place baby face-down on your forearm
   - Insert two fingers of one hand into vagina, under jaw
   - Other hand: two fingers on shoulders, middle finger on occiput
   - Apply flexion to head; gentle downward traction to deliver occiput then face

6. If head will not deliver: apply suprapubic pressure

7. Dry and assess neonate; be prepared for resuscitation

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Speed of transport vs delivery imminence: if head is visible, delivery will occur before arrival
- Never pull on the body - risks arm elevation and head entrapment
- After umbilicus delivered: maximum 3–5 minutes to head delivery before cord compression causes hypoxia
- Have neonatal resuscitation equipment ready

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Postpartum haemorrhage (PPH) is defined as blood loss > 500 mL within 24 hours of vaginal delivery (> 1000 mL for caesarean). Primary PPH (within 24 hours) is most common, usually due to uterine atony (4Ts: Tone, Trauma, Tissue, Thrombin). It is a leading cause of maternal mortality.

sections.title: 4T Causes
sections.content:
**Tone (70–80%):** Uterine atony - uterus soft and boggy
**Trauma (20%):** Laceration of cervix, vagina, perineum
**Tissue (5%):** Retained placenta or membranes
**Thrombin (1%):** Coagulopathy (pre-existing or acquired)

sections.title: Management
sections.content:
**Uterine atony (most common):**

1. Bimanual uterine massage: one hand on fundus abdominally; other hand internally (hospital only) - or external massage only prehospital

2. Encourage breastfeeding (stimulates oxytocin release)

3. Administer oxytocin per protocol (IM or slow IV infusion)

4. IV access (large bore ×2); fluid resuscitation with Hartmann's

5. Administer tranexamic acid if blood loss > 500 mL or coagulopathy suspected

6. Elevate legs; keep warm

7. Rapid transport - PPH is an emergency

**Retained placenta:**
- Do not pull on cord
- Transport for manual removal under anaesthesia

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Estimate blood loss: weigh pads if possible; pool of blood on surface ≈ 500 mL
- Oxytocin: give IM (10 IU) if IV not available
- Uterine atony: fundus should be firm at or below umbilicus after delivery; if soft and high: atony
- Always reassess mother and neonate simultaneously
- Document: estimated blood loss, oxytocin given, time, haemodynamic status

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: In the supine position, the gravid uterus (> 20 weeks gestation) can compress the inferior vena cava (IVC) and aorta, reducing venous return and cardiac output by up to 30%. This causes supine hypotension syndrome - maternal hypotension, faintness, and foetal compromise. Left lateral displacement or tilt relieves compression.

sections.title: Techniques
sections.content:
**Conscious patient:**
- Position in full left lateral position for transport
- If supine required (resuscitation): apply left lateral tilt

**Left lateral tilt for supine patient (e.g. CPR, trauma):**

Method 1 - Manual displacement:
- Assistant stands beside patient and manually displaces uterus to the left (preferred during CPR - maintains flat surface for compressions)

Method 2 - Wedge/tilt:
- Place wedge (foam, rolled blanket, equipment bag) under right hip/lower back to tilt patient 15–30° to the left
- Ensures left lateral displacement of uterus

**During CPR:**
- Manual left uterine displacement by a dedicated rescuer is preferred over tilting the patient - maintains firm surface for compressions

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Applies to pregnancy > 20 weeks (fundus above umbilicus)
- Any position that avoids aortocaval compression is acceptable
- Even 15° tilt significantly improves venous return
- During prehospital arrest in pregnancy: prioritise high-quality CPR + left uterine displacement + rapid transport for perimortem caesarean

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Fluorescein staining is a rapid, minimally invasive diagnostic technique used to identify corneal epithelial defects. Sodium fluorescein dye is applied topically to the conjunctival sac and illuminated with a cobalt blue light source. Defects in the corneal epithelium (abrasions, ulcers, foreign body tracks, herpetic dendrites) absorb the dye and fluoresce bright green under cobalt blue illumination, while intact epithelium does not stain.

sections.title: Indications
sections.content:
- Suspected corneal abrasion (following trauma, foreign body, or contact lens-related injury)
- Evaluation of corneal foreign body - to identify epithelial tracking or residual defect after removal
- Suspected corneal ulceration
- Chemical or UV (arc eye / photokeratitis) exposure
- Any red eye with pain and/or photophobia where corneal integrity is uncertain

sections.title: Contraindications & Precautions
sections.content:
- Known allergy to sodium fluorescein (rare)
- Soft contact lenses must be removed before application - fluorescein permanently stains soft lenses
- Do not use if a penetrating eye injury is suspected - any manipulation risks extrusion of intraocular contents
- Use with caution in patients with known iodine or contrast dye allergy (cross-reactivity is rare but possible)

sections.title: Equipment Required
sections.content:
- Sodium fluorescein 2% eye drops or fluorescein-impregnated strips
- Sterile normal saline (to moisten strips, if used)
- Cobalt blue light source (pen-torch with cobalt blue filter, ophthalmoscope on blue filter, or Wood's lamp)
- Sterile gloves
- Tissues / gauze
- Topical anaesthetic drops (e.g. proxymetacaine 0.5%) - optional, to aid examination in a painful eye; requires on-call doctor authorisation

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
1. **Explain the procedure** to the patient and obtain consent.

2. **Position the patient** seated or semi-recumbent with head supported.

3. **Wash hands** and don sterile gloves.

4. **Remove contact lenses** if not already done.

5. **If using fluorescein strips:**
   - Moisten the tip of the fluorescein strip with one drop of sterile saline (do not over-saturate).
   - Ask the patient to look up.
   - Gently touch the moistened strip to the lower conjunctival fornix (pull the lower lid down slightly).
   - Ask the patient to blink several times to distribute the dye.

6. **If using 2% fluorescein drops:**
   - Ask the patient to look up.
   - Pull the lower eyelid gently down to expose the conjunctival sac.
   - Instil 1 drop into the lower conjunctival fornix.
   - Ask the patient to blink to distribute the dye.

7. **Illuminate with cobalt blue light:**
   - Darken the room if possible.
   - Shine the cobalt blue light at the eye from approximately 30–40 cm.
   - Systematically examine the entire cornea and conjunctiva.

8. **Interpret the findings:**
   - **Normal (negative):** No green fluorescence on cornea or conjunctiva.
   - **Corneal abrasion:** Irregular or linear green staining pattern on corneal surface.
   - **Foreign body track:** Linear or punctate staining in the path of a foreign body.
   - **Corneal ulcer:** Dense, circumscribed area of staining, may have surrounding epithelial haze.
   - **Herpetic dendrites:** Branching (dendritic) pattern - contact on-call doctor immediately.

9. **Document findings:** Record which eye(s), location and approximate size of any defects, and staining pattern observed.

10. **Irrigate excess dye** with saline after examination if tolerated.

sections.title: Post-Procedure & Notes
sections.content:
- Fluorescein will temporarily cause mild yellow discolouration of the skin and tears - advise the patient this is normal and will resolve quickly.
- Do NOT re-insert soft contact lenses until the dye has completely cleared and the eye has healed.
- If herpetic dendrites are identified on staining, contact the on-call doctor immediately - antiviral therapy is required and topical steroids are contraindicated.
- Corneal abrasions less than one-third of the corneal diameter and without signs of infection may be managed conservatively; larger or infected defects require on-call doctor review.
- Do NOT apply an eye patch over a corneal abrasion - current evidence does not support patching.
- Topical anaesthetic drops (proxymetacaine/tetracaine) should ONLY be used to facilitate examination - they are NOT to be prescribed or self-administered for ongoing pain relief as they impair corneal healing.
